Thus, the child categorically refuses to participate in play that takes place when the child is seated at a table in which only small things are needed that lie on the desk. When we take his fingers, place them on the book and say, '~d for fun, these will be children," he objects that there is no such game. Fingers are too closely connected to his body to serve as an object for the required indicating gesture. In the same way, very large objects standing in the room, such as a cabinet or someone present, cannot participate in this game. In this way we see a completely clear experimentally obtained demarcation of the two functions of speech of which we spoke in dealing with the spoken language of the child. In itself, the object fulfills the function of replacement: the pencil replaces the nursemaid and the clock, the pharmacy; but only the gesture that refers to them imparts this sense to them, indicates this sense. In older children, the objects affected by the indicating gesture have the tendency not only to replace the objects they stand for, but also to denote them. The child's first very important "discovery" occurs here. When we put down a book with a dark cover and say that this will be a forest, the child himself adds, "Well, yes, this a forest because it is black, dark there." In this way, he isolates one of the characteristics of the object that is for him an indication that the book must represent a forest. In precisely this way, when the metallic bottle cap represents a cab, the child points to it and says, "This is the seat." When the clock represents the pharmacy, one child points to the face and says, "Here is the medicine and the pharmacy," and another points to the ring and says, "This is the entry, it's the door to the pharmacy." Picking up the small bottle that plays the part of the wolf, the child points to the neck and says, '~d this is his mouth." To the question of the experimenter pointing to the cork, '~d what is this?" the child responds, '~d this-he caught a cork and is holding it in his teeth." In all the examples, we see one and the same thing-specifically, the usual structure of the things seems to change under the influence of the new meaning that it acquired. Because the clock represents the pharmacy, one of its characteristics is isolated and assumes the function of a new sign, an instruction as to how the clock represents the pharmacy. The ordinary structure of things (cork closing the bottle) begins to be represented in the new structure (the wolf holds the cork in his teeth). A change in structure is so strong that in some experiments we observed time and again that the child retains the symbolic meaning of the object. The clock represented the pharmacy in all the games while the other objects rapidly and frequently changed their meaning. In making a transition to a new game, we put in the same clock and according to a new plan of action announced, "This is a bakery." The child placed his hand on the clock as if to cut it in half and said, "All right, here is the pharmacy, and here is the bakery." In this way, the old meaning became independent and served as means for a new meaning. We could establish an independent meaning even outside a game: when the knife dropped, the child cried, "The doctor fell." Thus, objects even without an indicating gesture retained the acquired meaning. Tracing the history of isolating and representing signs, we cannot help but remember the analogy to the development of speech and the meaning of words. As we have seen, in that case words were given a certain meaning through some graphic characteristic that indicated this meaning. The word "chemila" [ink] means a liquid for writing because of its old meaning and because of the characteristic "chemoe" [black]. In just the same way that the clock represented a pharmacy for

Development of Written Language

137

the child, the numbers represented medicine. Thus, the sign acquired an independent objective development that did not depend on the gesture of the child, and in this we see a second great epoch in the development of the written language of the child. The matter is the same with drawing. Here we see that the written language of the child does not arise naturally. We have already said that the initial drawing develops from a gesture of the hand equipped with a pencil. In this case, it leads to the fact that the representation begins independently to stand for some object. This is expressed by the fact that the drawn lines are given an appropriate name. C. Buhler called attention to the fact that the process of development of children's line drawing advances gradually: oral representation is transformed from subsequent into simultaneous. And finally the name precedes the drawing. This means that an intention to represent something abstract develops on the basis of the representation of a form that was drawn and named after the drawing was completed. Speech moving ahead [of action] serves as a means for important mental progress. G. Gettser, wishing to study how mentally mature a school-age child must be to learn to write, was the first to develop a broad formulation of this problem. She tried to study how the child's function of symbolic representation of things, which is so important for learning to write, develops. To do this, Gettser had to elucidate experimentally the development of the symbolic function in children age three to six years. The experiments consisted of four basic series. The symbolic function in play was studied in the first series. The child in play had to represent the father or mother and do what they do during a day. In the process of play, arbitrary interpretation of the objects involved in playing occurred and the researcher could trace the symbolic function assigned to the objects in play. Construction material was used to represent the mother or father, which was then colored with colored pencils. In the second and third series, special attention was given to the moment the appropriate meaning was assigned. Finally in the fourth series, in a game of postman, the researcher was able to observe to what extent the child is able to perceive a purely arbitrary combining of signs since the blocks colored with different colors served as signs of different kinds of letters delivered by the postman: telegrams, newspapers, remittances, packages, letters, postcards, etc. Thus, experimental study placed in one order the various kinds of activities united only by the fact that each of them was based on the symbolic function, and the study tried to establish a genetic connection between all of these types of activity and the development of written language. In Gettser's experiments, we can see quite clearly how the symbolic meaning in play develops with the help of a graphic gesture and with the help of a word. Here the child's egocentric speech was very apparent. While in some children everything is represented with the help of movement and mimicking and speech is not involved as a symbolic means, in other children speech accompanies action: the child talks and acts. In a third group, purely verbal expression begins to dominate unsupported by any other kind of activity. Finally, the fourth group of children hardly played at all and speech was the only means of representation, while mimicking and gesture were relegated to the background. The experiment demonstrated that with years, the percentage of purely play actions gradually diminishes and that speech begins to dominate. The most essential conclusion from this genetic study, as Gettser says, is the fact that the difference in play between three-year-old and six-year-old children is not in the perception of symbols, but in the method by which the various forms of representation are used. It seems to us that this is the most important conclusion showing that symbolic

138

Chapter 7

representation in play and at a very early stage is, in essence, a unique form of speech that leads directly to written language. As the child develops, naming moves gradually to the forefront of the process of representation and in this way, this process assumes the function of recording the word just named. Even the three-year-old understands the imaginative function of the construction; a four-year-old names his products before he begins to build them. The same thing happens in drawing. It seems that the three-year-old does not know the symbolic meaning of the picture ahead of time and children master this completely only by the age of seven. Prior analysis of children's drawing shows without a doubt that from the psychological point of view, we must consider it as unique speech of children. As we know, at first the child draws from memory and if a suggestion is made that he draw his mother who is sitting opposite him or some object that is before him, he draws without once looking at the original, and as a result draws not what he sees, but what he knows. We see another indication of this in the fact that the child's picture not only does not take into account, but contradicts the real perception of the object. So what develops is what Buhler calls an x-ray picture. The child imagines a person in clothing, but while he does so, he draws his legs, stomach, and purse in his pocket and even money in the purse, that is, things about which he knows but which cannot be seen in the picture. Drawing a man in profile, the child gives him a second eye and draws both legs of a man on horseback portrayed in profile. Finally, in the child's drawing very important parts of an object are omitted while, for example, the child draws legs growing directly from the head, leaving out the neck and body. All of this shows that the child draws, in the words of C. Buhler, essentially just as he speaks. This justifies our considering the drawing of the child as a preliminary stage of his written language. As far as psychological function is concerned, the child's drawing is a unique graphic speech, a graphic story about something. The technique of children's drawing shows without a doubt that the drawing is specifically a graphic story, that is, the child's unique written language. For this reason, the child's drawing itself, according to the correct observation of C. Buhler, is more speech than representation. As J. SullyB° showed, the child did not try for representation; he is much more a symbolist than a naturalist, he does not at all bother about a full and precise likeness, but wants only to produce a certain indication of the thing that he is drawing. The child tries rather to name and designate than to represent. C. Buhler accurately points out that the child begins to draw when he has already had much success in verbal, oral speech and it has become habitual. Further, Buhler says that speech in general dominates and forms the greatest part of mental life according to its laws. Drawing also belong to this part; about drawing, we might say that in the end, it too IS swallowed up by speech since all graphic ability for expression of the average modern cultured man flows out in writing. The child's memory at that time does not contain simple forms of representation, but for the most part consists of a predisposition to judgment enveloped in speech or capable of being enveloped in it. When the child discloses new treasures of his memory in drawing, this is done by way of speech, as if by narration. The most important characteristic of this method is a certain abstraction to which all verbal description necessarily and naturally belongs. Thus, we see that drawing is graphic speech that develops on the basis of verbal speech. In this sense, the schematics that are characteristic of the initial drawings of children resemble verbal concepts that convey only the essential and constant characteristics of objects.

Development of Written Language

139

This stage of speech, in contrast to written speech, is still symbolism of the first degree. The child depicts not words, but objects and representations of these objects. But the child's development of drawing in itself is not anything understandable or developing purely mechanically. Its critical moment is in the transition from the simple making of marks on paper to using pencil marks as signs that represent or signify something. All psychologists agree that here, as C. Buhler says, the child must discover that the lines he makes can mean something. Sully explains this discovery using the example of when a child, in drawing without any idea or meaning, by chance produced a spiral line in which he suddenly saw a certain similarity and joyfully cried, "Smoke, smoke!" Most psychologists assume that in drawing, the child discovers in the form he has drawn a similarity with some object, and from this his drawing acquires the function of a sign. We might suggest that what occurs, occurs somewhat differently: due to a number of circumstances, the child comes upon the fact that the drawing can represent something. Thus, children usually recognize things in the drawings of others before they themselves draw. But the process of recognizing what is drawn, although it is encountered in early childhood, is nevertheless, as observation has shown, not the initial discovery of symbolic function. Initially, the child, if he does see the similarity between the drawing and an object, takes the drawing to be a similar object, but not as a representation or symbol. A girl who was shown a picture of her doll cried, '~ doll! Just like this one!" It is possible that she regarded the picture as still another object that was the same as one she had. According to Gettser, none of the observations can compel us to assume that to understand an object is to understand as well what a drawing represents. For the girl, the drawing was not a representation of the doll, but another doll that was the same as hers. Evidence of this is the fact that the child treats the drawing as an object for quite a long time. K. Buhler observed how this girl tried to remove the lines and colors she had drawn on a green background from the paper, etc. What caught my attention was that an older child who was already naming his drawings and correctly identifying the drawings of others still persisted for a long time in treating the drawing as a thing. For example, when a man was presented in a drawing with his back to the viewer, the child turned the paper over to see his face. Even in five-year-olds, we always observed that in response to the question: "Where is the face and where is the nose?" the child turned the paper over and only after he did that did he answer that they were not there, they were not drawn. We believe that Gettser's opinion has the firmest foundation; it states that primary symbolic representation must be related specifically to speech, that all other symbolic meaning of signs is created on the basis of speech. In fact, going back to the beginning, the moment of naming during drawing very clearly indicates the strong pressure of speech under which children's drawing develops. This soon turns into the real written language which we had the opportunity to observe experimentally when we proposed to the children that they use signs to represent a more or less complex sentence. Here, as we have already said, we saw in the drawing the manifestation of gestures (stretched out arms, pointing fingers) or lines that replaced them and could in this way, like a filter, separate the figurative from the indicative function of the word. In these experiments, most clearly apparent was the tendency in school-age children to move from purely pictographic to ideographic writing, that is, to representing the separate relations and meanings with abstract symbolic signs. We found the supremacy of speech over writing to be most distinct in the writing of one of the school children who did a separate drawing for each word of the sen-

140

Chapter 7

tence. A sentence such as, "I don't see the sheep, but they are there," was written in the following way: a figure of a person ("I"), the same figure with a blindfold ("don't see"), two sheep ("sheep"), a pointing finger and several trees behind which the same sheep can be seen ("but they are there"). The sentence "I respect you," was rendered as follows: a head ("I"), another head ("you"), two human figures one of which is holding a hat in his hands ("respect"). Thus, we see how the drawing obediently follows the sentence and how oral language penetrates into a child's drawing. In doing the assignment, the children often had to make real discoveries and invent appropriate methods of representation, and we actually could confirm that the development of speech is decisive in the development of the child's writing and drawing. Observing the spontaneous manifestations of children's writing, Stern gives a number of examples that show how this development occurs and how the whole process of writing proceeds. The child, learning to write independently, writes from the lower left edge of the paper to the right, every new line is added from the top, etc. A. R. Luria,s1 in connection with our general research, aimed to elicit and track the moment of discovery of the symbolism of writing in order to study it systematically. The research showed that the history of writing begins in the child significantly earlier than when the teacher first places a pencil in his hand and shows him how to write letters. If we do not know the prehistory of children's writing, we will not understand how a child quickly learns written language, the most complex device of cultural behavior. We will begin to understand that this can occur only under the condition that during the first school years, the child mastered and developed a number of devices leading him directly to the process of writing, preparing him and facilitating incredibly the mastery of ideas and the technique of writing. In experiments, Luria placed the child who did not yet know how to write in a situation where he had to produce a certain primitive piece of writing. The child was told to remember a certain number of sentences, usually significantly exceeding the physical capability of the child to remember. When he was convinced that he could not remember, he was given a piece of paper and advised to note or write the proposed sentences. Often the child met this proposal with perplexity and said that he doesn't know how to write, but he was firmly advised to somehow figure out how the pencil and paper could help him remember. In this way, the researcher gave the child a certain device and watched to what extent the child would prove capable of mastering it, to what extent the lines made with the pencil stopped being simple marks for the child and became signs for remembering the corresponding meanings. This device reminds us of the experiments of Kohler with monkeys when he did not wait for them to get the idea of picking up a stick, but placed them in situations where they had to use it as a tool; he placed a stick in their hands and waited to see what would happen. Experiments have shown that three- to four-year-old children cannot relate to writing as a means: they write the given sentence mechanically over a series of scribbles and write before they hear. The child writes, imitating an adult, but this is still a stage of the child's writing in which he cannot use writing as a series of mnemotechnical signs; the writing does not help him at all to remember the given sentences; remembering, he does not look at his writing at all. But these experiments should be continued to determine whether the situation begins to change suddenly and essentially. Among our material, we sometimes found cases that were surprising at first glance, that deviated sharply from what has just been said. The child writes just as unthinkingly, in an undifferentiated way, makes scribbles and

Development of Written Language

141

marks that don't mean anything, but in reproducing sentences, he gives the impression that he is reading them, pointing to quite definite marks and showing without error many times in succession which marks mean what. The child develops a completely new relation to his marks and they are the first to turn into mnemotechnical signs. For example, the child distributes the separate marks on the paper in such a way that he connects a certain sentence with each mark. A unique topography develops: one mark in the corner signifies a cow, another at the top, a chimney-sweep, etc. In this way, the marks are a primitive indicating sign for memory, a sign of something that must be reproduced. We have a solid basis for seeing in this mnemotechnical stage the first precursor of future writing. The child gradually converts the non differentiated marks into indicating signs; the symbolic lines and scribbles are replaced by figures and pictures and the latter yield to signs. Experiments made it possible to describe not only the moment itself of this discovery, but also to trace its course depending on certain factors. Insertions indicating number and form added to sentences first break the meaningless character of the writing that expresses nothing when different ideas and images are represented by completely similar marks and scribbles. Inserting data on number into this material, we quite easily elicited differentiated writing in four- and five-year-olds that reflected the number inserted. The need to write number was perhaps the first thing that gave rise to writing. In precisely the same way, including color or form plays a leading role in the child's discovery of the mechanism of writing. Sentences such as, "Black, black smoke is coming out of the chimney"; "In the winter, there is snow"; "The mouse with the long tail"; "Lyala has two eyes and one nose," very quickly lead the child to making a transition from writing that fulfills the role of an indicating gesture to writing that contains a representation in embryonic form. From this, the child makes a direct transition to drawing and in this way, we witness his transition to pictographic writing. Pictographic writing develops very easily in the child because, as we have seen, the child's drawing is essentially a unique graphic speech. However, as experiments show, even here the child experiences conflict the whole time: the drawing as a means is still frequently replaced by a drawing as a self-sufficient direct process. It is especially easy to observe this in retarded children who move to independent drawing from drawing the given sentences according to association. The child, instead of writing, begins to draw pictures. From pictographic writing the child makes a gradual transition to the ideogram if the drawing does not directly convey the content of the sentence. Experiments have demonstrated that the child proceeds along a detour and instead of the whole that is difficult to draw, he draws the parts that are easy to draw, a schematic, and sometimes, conversely, he produces the whole situation in which the meaning of the given sentence is contained. We have already said that the transition to symbolic writing, as our experiments have shown, is signified by the appearance of a number of simply drawn gestures or lines that represent gestures. Studying how the child writes without knowing how to write, but already knowing his letters, we see that he goes through the same stages that we have just described. The development of writing consists not only of a constant improvement of some one device, but also in sharp leaps that mark the transition from one device to another. The child who knows how to write letters, but has not yet discovered for himself the mechanism of writing, still writes in the same nondifferentiated way, separating individual letters and their parts, but later cannot reproduce them. Experiments have shown that the child who knows letters and can, with help, isolate their individual sounds in words does not at all immediately come to a full

142

Chapter 7

mastery of the mechanism of writing. However, even here in what we have just discussed, we do not find the. most important moment that characterizes the real transition to written language. It is easy to note that everywhere written signs represent symbols of the first order directly signifying objects or actions, and the child at the stage we are describing does not approach symbolism of the second order which consists of using written signs for oral symbols of words. For this, the child must make a special discovery-specifically, not only things, but speech also can be drawn. Only this discovery brought humanity to the brilliant method of writing with words and letters; it also brings the child to literate writing and, from the psychological point of view, must be constructed like the transition from drawing things to drawing speech. However, it is difficult to trace how this transition is accomplished because the appropriate research has not yet resulted in definite results and the generally accepted methods of teaching writing do not allow a thorough observation of the transition process. One thing is not subject to doubt: in all probability, real written language of the child (not mastery of the habit of writing) develops in a similar way, the way the transition is made from drawing things to drawing words. Various methods of teaching writing confirm that in different ways. Many methods use auxiliary gestures as a means of uniting the written and the oral symbol; others use drawing depicting the corresponding object, and the whole secret in teaching written language consists in preparing and organizing this natural transition properly. As soon as this is done, the child masters the mechanics of written language and he only needs to perfect this method subsequently. With the present state of psychological information, many will think the idea extremely forced that all the stages we have considered-play, drawing, and writing-may be presented as different moments of an essentially single process of developing written language. The breaks and jumps in the transition from one device to another are somewhat large for the connection between the separate moments to be sufficiently obvious and clear. But experiments and psychological analysis lead us specifically to this conclusion and show that no matter how complex the process of development of written language seems to us, no matter how zig-zag, broken, and confused its course it might seem at a superficial glance, actually we have before us a single line of the history of writing that leads to higher forms of written language. The higher form, which we touch in passing, consists in the fact that the written language of symbolism of the second order becomes again symbolism of the first order. The initial written symbols serve as a sign of verbal symbols. Understanding written language is done through oral speech, but gradually this path is shortened, the intermediate link in the form of oral speech drops away, and written language becomes a direct symbol just as understandable as oral speech. One has only to imagine what an enormous break occurs in the whole cultural development of the child due to the mastery of written language, due to the possibility of reading and consequently to being enriched by everything that human genius has created in the sphere of the written word in order to understand the decisive moment experienced by the child in the discovery of writing. For us one problem in the development of higher forms of written language is now important: the problem of silent and oral reading. Research into writing has shown that, in contrast to the old school which cultivated reading aloud, silent reading is socially the most important form of written language and has two important advantages in addition. From the first year of schooling, silent reading surpasses reading aloud in number of fixations of eye movements over the line. Therefore, the process of eye movement in itself and perception of letters is facilitated in silent reading, the character of movement becomes more rhythmical and reverse movement of the eyes occurs less often. Vo-

Development of Written Language

143

calization of visual symbols impedes reading and the speech reactions slow down perception, hamper it, and break up attention. Not only the process of reading itself, but as strange as it may seem, understanding is better with silent reading. Research has demonstrated a correlation between reading rate and understanding. Most think that understanding is greater with slower reading; however, actually with rapid reading, understanding is better or the different processes occur at different rates and the rate of understanding is more compatible with a rapid reading rate. In reading aloud, there is a visual interval when the eyes move forward and are synchronized with the voice. If during reading we fix the place at which the eyes rest and the sound which at the given moment is being pronounced, we find the visual-sound interval. Research shows that the interval grows gradually, that good reading involves a large visual-sound interval and that the rate of reading and the interval increase together. Thus, we see that the visual symbol is more and more liberated from the oral. If we recall that school age is exactly the age of growth in the formation of internal speech, it becomes clear what a powerful means for perception of internal speech we have in silent or quiet reading. Unfortunately, experimental research has thus far studied reading as a sensory-motor habit and not as a mental process of a very complex order. But even here, research showed that some mechanisms of reading depend on the type of material. To a certain degree, the work of the visual mechanism is subordinate to the processes of understanding. How can we imagine understanding in reading? We cannot now provide any very clear answer to this question, but everything we know thus far forces us to assume that, like every other process, at a given stage of development, using the law of written language, it becomes an internal process. That which we usually call understanding of what is read must be determined first of all from the genetic aspect as a given moment in the development of the mediated reaction to visual symbols. It is clear to us that understanding does not mean that in reading each sentence we generate pictures of all the objects mentioned in it. Understanding cannot be reduced to a graphic resurrection of the object or even to naming the word; more likely, it consists in operating with the sign itself and referring it to meaning, to a rapid movement of attention, and isolating different points that are at the center of our attention. An imbecile's reading gives a very clear example of reading without understanding. P. Ya. rroshin 82 describes an imbecile who in reading became delighted by every word: ''l\ ladybird (ay, a bird, a bird!-Agitated pleasure) doesn't know (doesn't know!-What a revelation)" or "Count Vitte came (he came, he came!) to Petersburg (to Petersburg, Petersburg!)," etc. Concentration of attention, attaching it to each separate sign, inability to control attention and transfer it so as to be oriented in the complex internal space that might be called a system of relations are the imbecile's basic traits of "understanding" the text. Conversely, normal understanding is the process of establishing relations, isolating the important, in a transition from separate elements to the meaning of the whole. Characterizing briefly the history of the development of written language in the child, we come to four most important practical conclusions. First: it would be natural to teach writing at a preschool age. Actually, if the discovery of the symbolic function of writing is possible in children of a very young age, as Gettser's experiments have shown, then teaching writing must be made a requirement in preschool education. We actually see a number of instances that indicate that teaching writing in our system comes too late from the psychological point of view.

144

Chapter 7

Considering children from the point of view of teaching them writing, P. P. Blonskii indicates that a child learning to read and write at four and a half years of age must be considered a genius and a child learning it at four and a half to five years three months of age, as being highly intelligent. Moreover, we know that teaching reading and writing in most European and American countries begins at the age of six as a rule. The research of Gettser showed that eighty percent of three-year-old children have learned arbitrary combining of sign and meaning, and at six years, they are fully capable in this operation. According to her observations, mental development between the ages of three and six consists not so much in the development of the operation itself of using arbitrary signs as in successes that produce the attention and memory of the child. Gettser assumes that even three-year-old children in most cases could be taught to read and write, since this is connected with mastery of symbolic writing. 'lIue, Gettser did not take into account that writing is symbolism of the second order while her data only cover symbolism of the first order. Wholly justifiably, she criticizes education in which a child of three to four years is taught reading and writing: she points to the system of M. Montessori83 who teaches reading and writing in kindergarten and to many French schools that also do this. From the psychological aspect, says Gettser, this is not impossible, but it is difficult because of the child's insufficient memory and attention. C. Burt84 presents information relative to England where compulsory schooling begins at the age of five, but if there is space, three- to five-year-old children are admitted to school, where they are taught the alphabet. At four years, most children know how to read. Montessori speaks strongly for teaching reading and writing at an earlier age. She teaches reading and writing to four-year-olds. As a rule, in the process of play, by preliminary exercises, all children in her kindergartens in Italy begin to write at the age of four and at the age of five, they read as well as a six-year-old in first grade, which as compared with Germany represents a two-year advantage. The uniqueness of the Montessori method consists of writing development as a natural instant in the process of the development of the hand; for children, the difficulty in writing is not in knowing the letters, but in insufficient development of the small musculature of the hand. With the aid of painstaking exercises, Montessori makes it possible for children to learn to write not by writing, but by drawing, making lines. They learn to write in order to approach writing, and for this reason, they begin to write at once, suddenly or spontaneously. The process of teaching writing takes very little time. Two of her four-year-olds took less than a month and a half to learn to write to the extent that they could write letters independently. From observations of child development in the family where books and pencils are used intensively and especially where there are older children who read and write, we know that at the age of four to five, the child spontaneously learns to read and write just as he learns the spoken language. The child independently begins to write individual numbers and letters, to read them in signs, to make words of them and does in a natural way what Montessori organizes in the kindergarten. But Montessori's experience shows that the matter is much more complex than it seems at first glance. If, on the one hand, the teaching of writing in school lags since children as young as four to five years old can learn the mechanics from both the motor aspect and the aspect of symbolic function, then, on the other hand, how strange it is that teaching writing at the age of six or eight is premature: that 'Editor's note: Modern research and practice of teaching have shown the possibility and expediency of teaching reading and writing at the age of six.

Development of Written Language

145

is, artificial in the sense that Wundt understands early development of spoken language by the child. This means that the technique is available to the child before his need for written language matures and before he needs written language. If writing, like muscular activity and like symbolic perception, develops very easily from play, then we must not forget that according to the psychological significance it has in behavior, it is far removed from play. In this sense, Montessori's critics are right who point to the limitations of this understanding of development, which follows from Montessori's naturalistic anatomism and leads to mechanical passivity of the child. After a month and a half, says Gessen, four- and five-year-old children begin to write with calligraphy that amazes us. But for the time, we will leave the correctness and the elegance of the letters produced by the children and turn our attention to the content of what was written. What do Montessori's children write? "We wish Engineer Talani and Directress Montessori a good Easter"; "I wish the superior, the teacher, and Doctor Montessori well"; "Kindergarten, Campaign Street," etc. We do not deny the possibility of teaching reading and writing to preschoolers, we even consider it desirable for the child going into school to know how to read and write, but teaching must be set up so that reading and writing satisfy the child's need. If the knowledge is used only to write formal greetings to the authorities and the first words are clearly dictated by the teacher, then obviously such a task will be a purely mechanical means that may soon bore the child; his activity will not appear in it, and his developing personality will not grow. Reading and writing must be needed by the child. Here, as apparent as it can be, is the basic contradiction that characterizes not only the experiment of Montessori but teaching writing in school as well: writing is taught as a certain motor habit and not as a complex cultural activity. For this reason, together with the first question on transferring the teaching of writing to the preschool age, the requirement for lifelike writing which may be compared to the requirement for lifelike arithmetic is self-evident. This means that writing must make sense to the child, must be elicited by a natural want, a need, included in a lifelike task essential to the child. Only then will we be convinced that it will develop in the child not as habits of the hand and the fingers, but as a truly new and complex aspect of speech. Many pedagogues like Gettser, not agreeing with the general spirit of teaching reading and writing as practiced by Montessori, speak for a transfer of the teaching of writing from the school to the kindergarten, but even here an erroneous approach to the problem and an underestimation of the significance of written language are apparent. Like speech, say the pedagogues, knowing how to read and write in the elementary sense of the word is more a habit of the mental order. Nothing could be more wrong than such an approach to writing. We have seen what a complex prehistory writing passes through before its definitive development, what leaps, metamorphoses, and discoveries are necessary for it to develop and be established. We know what a major change speech introduces into the behavior of the child. For this reason, we must not consider mastery of reading and writing as a simple mental habit. The indicated exactness even of the most perfect and liberated school methods of teaching reading and writing can be explained not by the fact that teaching reading and writing cannot become the subject of a school lesson, but by the fact that none of these methods takes into account the main thing and instead of written language, gives the child writing habits. Not for nothing does Gettser say that there is no basic difference between knowing how to read and write, knowing how to speak, dress and undress oneself, and knowing elementary drawing. Gettser

146

Chapter 7

sees Montessori's merit in that she demonstrated that knowing how to write is to a significant degree a purely "muscular capability." We see in this the weakest point in the Montessori method. For her, writing is purely muscular activity and for this reason her children write letters that have no content. Between knowing how to write and knowing how to dress, there is a major difference that we tried to emphasize throughout this whole chapter. The moving, muscular aspect of writing, which undoubtedly plays an enormous role, is a subordinate aspect, and it is specifically the failure to understand this that may explain the lack of success of Montessori. What conclusion can be reached from this? W Stern disputes Montessori's opinion that it is necessary to teach a fouryear-old child to read and considers it not by chance alone that such teaching begins at the beginning of the seventh year of life in all cultured countries. For confirmation, Stern cites the observation of M. Mukhov: it is specifically the paucity of play in the Montessori kindergartens that spurs the children to turn to reading and writing. In kindergartens set up according to the system of F. Fr6bel,85 where the child has many more activities, observations, work for developing fantasy and interests, and independence in play, children of this age rarely show an interest in reading and writing of themselves. The opinion of Mukhov has received indirect confirmation from observation of how a child himself arrives at a need to read and write without didactic influence. Here, as Stern says, maturation of this capability occurs along entirely different paths. All of our observations presented above had the purpose of demonstrating to what degree knowing how to read and write differs in a major way from knowing how to dress and undress. We tried to disclose all the uniqueness and complexity of the path by which the child comes to being taught to write. The gross simplification of the tasks with which psychological consideration of the pedagogical process is usually confronted is most clearly apparent when even the best pedagogues are inclined to consider both habits-writing and dressing-as mainly one and the same thing. Genuine psychological analysis shows, and every pedagogue knows from experience, to what degree this is not one and the same thing, to what degree teaching writing must go through a complex path of development. Teaching writing as a habit leads specifically to the principle of writing that is not lifelike, to gymnastics of the fingers, and not to the cultural development of the child. When we read the letters of Montessori's children and admire their calligraphy, we cannot ignore the impression that we have children before us who learned how to hit the keys, but are deaf as far as the music their fingers are making is concerned. The third point we wanted to make as a practical conclusion of our research is the requirement for natural teaching of writing. Here Montessori did a great deal. She showed that the motor aspect of this activity may be elicited in the natural process of the child's play, that writmg must not be thrust on the children, but cultivated. She presented the natural path of the development of writing. Moving along this path, the child comes to writing as to a natural moment in his development and not as to external training. Montessori showed that the natural element for teaching reading and writing is the kindergarten, and this means that the best method for teaching is one in which the children do not learn to read and write, but in which both habits are the subject of play. For this, it is necessary that the letter become the same kind of element in the life of the child as, for example, speech is. In the same way that children teach themselves to speak, they must themselves learn to read and write. The natural method of teaching reading and writing consists of appropriately affecting the situation in which the child finds himself. The child must need reading

Development of Written Language

147

and writing in his play. But what Montessori did with respect to the motor aspect of habit should be done with respect to the internal aspect of written language and its functional assimilation. It is also necessary to bring the child to an internal understanding of writing, necessary that writing should appear in the development of the child. For this, we can indicate only the most general path. Just as hand work and mastery of lines are preparatory exercises in the Montessori method for the development of the habit of writing, the activities we have indicated-drawing and play-must be preparatory stages in the development of children's written language. The pedagogue must organize the necessary actions of the child, the whole complex transition from one method of written language to another. The pedagogue must lead the child through the critical moments right up to the discovery that not just things can be drawn, but also speech. But this methodology of teaching writing is a matter for the future. If we should want to summarize the practical requirements and express them in a single position, then we could say that consideration of the problem leads us to the requirement to teach a child written language and not writing the alphabet. Montessori used her method to teach not only normal children, but also retarded children of the same mental age, correctly pointing out that she was developing Seguin's method first applied to retarded children. She succeeded in teaching several retarded children to write orthographically and calligraphically so well that she was able to have them participate in a general examination together with normal children. The retarded passed the examination quite well. Thus, we have two exceptionally important instructions. First, that a retarded child of the same mental age can learn to read and write. However, apparent here with even greater clarity is the absence of a demand of lifelike writing and teaching of written language of which we have spoken. Specifically because of these methods, Gettser rejects Montessori's principle, indicating that at an early age, children do not yet come to understanding written words and that Montessori's results are a focus that has no pedagogical value. According to Gettser, teaching should begin before the child reaches the mental maturity that is needed for learning written language. As far as the method of teaching is concerned, Gettser also favors devices of preschool education so that the child would be prepared for reading and writing by drawing, and reading and writing would appear in the process of play and not as school subjects. The importance of mastering written language as such and not just external reading and writing is so great that sometimes researchers directly divide the retarded into those who read and those who don't. Actually, if we divide the retarded according to degrees of mastering speech, then we must say that the idiot is a person who has not mastered speech at all; the imbecile learns only oral speech, but the retarded is capable of learning written language as well, and it is all the more important and more difficult to teach the retarded not only the mechanisms of reading and writing, but also true written language and knowing how to write and express his thought in writing. We already know that for the retarded child the same task is more creative than for the normal child. In order to master written language, the retarded child must exert more creative force; for him it is a more creative act than for the normal child. In experiments, we saw how retarded children work through the same crucial moments in developing written language that we observed in normal children, but with great effort and great expenditure of creative energy. In this sense, we might say that understanding what is written and being able to read is in itself the crown of all cultural development of which the retarded child is capable.

148

Chapter 7

With blind children, we saw some experimental evidence that learning to read and write is, to some degree, not a simple motor habit, a simple muscle activity, because in the blind habit is something completely different, the content of muscle activity is very different compared to that in the sighted. Also, while the motor aspect of writing is completely different, in the blind child, the mental aspect of writing remains the same. The blind cannot master written language as a system of visual habits, and for this reason he experiences a great retardation in the development of the whole activity connected with signs as we have already ascertained with respect to development of speech. The absence of drawing greatly retards the development of written language in the blind child, but his play in which gesture also contributes meaning and sense to an object bring him by a direct path to writing. The blind read and write with raised points that represent our letters. The profound uniqueness of all motor habit when the blind child reads with two fingers can be explained by the fact that tactile perception is constructed in a completely different way from visual perception. It would seem that here we have a completely different motor habit, but psychologically, as Delacroix says, the process of teaching a blind child coincides with the same process in the sighted; as it does there, attention gradually transfers from the signs themselves to what they signify, and the processes of understanding are developed and established in exactly the same way. In the development of writing in the blind, we see a clear example of how cultural development occurs in the abnormal child. Where he experiences a divergence between the system of signs developed in the process of historical development and his own development, we create a special cultural technique, a special system of signs that fulfills the same function psychologically. The uniqueness of development of written language in the deaf-mute has not been appreciated sufficiently, and probably the fatal mistake in all teaching of speech to the deaf is that they are taught oral speech initially and then written language, while it should be the other way around. For the deaf-mute child, the basic type of speech, symbolism of the first order, must be written language. He must learn to read and write the way a child learns to speak, and oral speech must be developed in the deaf-mute as reading of what is written. Then the written language will become the basic means for verbal development of the deaf-mute child. If we teach the deaf-mute child written language and not just calligraphy, then we will be able to bring him to a higher degree of development, a degree which he will not reach by social interaction with other people, but may reach only through reading books.

Chapter 8 DEVELOPMENT OF ARITHMETIC OPERATIONS We know that the principle of ordering, that is, giving a quantity a certain structure, making it possible to perceive a certain quantity by sight, remains a basic principle of the psychology of operations with great numbers. It is much easier for us to perceive the absence of a soldier from a company than the absence of a person in an unregimented crowd. If we hear a song or poetry and if one beat or one syllable is out of place, then, although we may not know the beat or these syllables, we feel the slip directly upon hearing it. The child proceeds in the same way. He takes an unordered pile of objects, places them in rows like a company of soldiers and sees at once that one is missing. Children understand the significance of ordering. This is expressed in the following example. Children who are accustomed to using cubes for building things begin very early to check the results of dividing the cubes or blocks according to what they are building, for example, a model of a tractor. All the children build it the same way and each sees whether he has enough blocks for a tractor. The children check the results of the division simply according to the tractors. It is remarkable that the children relate to putting figures together not as to a goal in itself, but as an arithmetic game, and specifically, as a means and a demonstration. If all of them build a tractor and one child says, "I made a clock," then the children will demand that he break up his clock and make a tractor. One must build something that is comparable. This is a unit of counting. Children protest when they are building tractors and one builds a clock. They see that in this way they are deprived of the means of checking, that here they lack a common denominator. Even more interesting are the experimental cases where we made the point of testing more difficult. The children had to divide a number of pencils of different colors, shapes, and sizes. These were not cubes or blocks that were completely alike from which it was easy to build tractors. From the point of view of arithmetic, the children proceeded completely correctly, but from our point of view, incorrectly. They began by distributing groups of pencils; they all seemed different. Then the children tried to make the groups equal. One pencil is longer, another, shorter .... And here the children began to make long wands of the pencils. All pencils were placed into wands and each child got one such wand. But one child had five short and another, two long pencils. From the point of view of arithmetic, this is an incorrect division, but from the formal point of view from which the child approaches the problem, it is correct. Another most important point consists of the following: we cannot have a remainder greater than the divisor; the situation the children encounter is different. A child makes the distribution with the help of "tractors." The division consists of the following: the children make several "tractors" or "clocks" immediately. Six

149

150

Chapter 8

blocks are needed for each tractor and there are participants in the game. One child has five blocks as a result. Can this be divided among four children? Yes, but a tractor cannot be built with five blocks; as a result, for the child, five blocks are a number that exceeds the divisor and is a remainder. This is a quantity that cannot be divided under the given method of division. Here we see experimental evidence of the fact that such division is a mediated operation. It is possible to refuse to divide five blocks among four participants and consider them as a remainder. Here, the child divides not by eye, but he selects a certain figure, a tractor or a clock, which serves as a kind of measure, a unit. And if the unit consists of six blocks or cubes, then five blocks are a remainder, that is, a situation develops which is impossible with direct arithmetic. The transition from direct arithmetic to mediated, from a reaction by sight to a reaction that resorts to the tractor, clock, or wands as an auxiliary means is the most important instant in the arithmetical development of the child. The observations of Ranschburg and his students of severely retarded children showed that in these children it was difficult to elicit a transition from direct reactions to number to reactions that involved a figure that could serve as a unit, as a subsidiary means. It seems that our decimal system is difficult and almost incomprehensible for the severely retarded. Such a child does not go beyond the first ten. He cannot assimilate such a system. As Ranschburg says, there is an important symptom in the development of the child which can be used to predict how learning arithmetic will proceed. If the child does not resort to the devices of dividing of which I spoke, there is every basis to expect that he will be incapable of grasping cultural arithmetic. Basic culture in the development of counting consists of a transition from direct perception of number to mediated perception, to the child's beginning to compare numbers to certain signs and to operating with these signs. To conclude with preschool arithmetic, we must indicate the final stage through which its development passes. Quite soon, the older child is confronted with the fact that the method of dividing with the help of "tractors" and "clocks" distracts energy, attention, and time from the nonmediated task before him. The child is confronted with arithmetical difficulties, one of which is that the remainder exceeds the divisor. Then the child makes a transition to another, simpler form of operation. He begins to use as a basic auxiliary means not such concrete forms as "tractors" or "clocks," but certain spatial, abstract forms that correspond to the number and may by divided according to units. As far as we could trace this, it is evidently the last stage in the development of preschool arithmetic. We cannot say which paths further development would take if the child were left to himself, if he would not find himself in school and was not taught our system of counting, if he were to continue to develop along the natural, innate path. In practice, we cannot observe this. Almost always there are exceptionally crucial points in the development of the child, there is always a collision between his arithmetic and another form of arithmetic that adults teach him. The pedagogue and the psychologist should know that the child's learning cultural arithmetic produces a conflict. In other words, development here consists of a certain break, a certain collision, in a collision between the forms of operating with numbers that the child developed himself and those that adults teach him. Up to this time, psychologists and mathematicians are divided into two camps. Some say that the process of learning arithmetic proceeds more or less along a straight line, that preschool arithmetic prepares one for school arithmetic completely naturally the way the prattling of a child prepares for his speaking. The school teacher only corrects the child and pushes him further in the same direction. Other methodologists say that the process

Development of Arithmetic Operations

151

occurs in a completely different way. Between the preschool stage and the school stage, there is a certain break, a transition from one path to another. This displacement marks a turning point in the arithmetical development of the child. The child makes a transition from direct perception of number to mediated perception by experience, that is, to mastery of signs and ciphers and their correct meaning, to those rules that we use which consist of replacing operation with objects by operation with number systems. If we want to divide a certain number of objects among a certain number of participants, then at the beginning we count the objects and the participants. Then we carry out the arithmetical operation of division. The point at which the child makes the transition from direct reaction to number to abstract operations with signs is the point of conflict. It creates a collision between the former line of development with that which begins with the learning of school signs. We cannot imagine that development goes along a completely straight line. There are many leaps, breaks, and turns. It is interesting to select children for observation who, on the one hand, still do not know how to really count, but already have the rudiments of ordinary counting and, on the other hand, still have not completely abandoned "natural arithmetic." If we could do this, we would see that both methods of counting come into conflict, push each other away. Here we approach the basic, well-known methodological argument in arithmetic: how is counting to be taught-by way of number figures or by way of constructing a counting senes. We posed the problem of what is the relation between counting and perception in the child. We saw that at the first stage of development the fonn is an auxiliary means for counting. We gave a simple example where was it easier to note the absence of one person in a company of soldiers or in a crowd. The fonn, i.e., a certain ordering and correctness of the visual impression itself, is an important aid to correct perception of number. The first stage in the development of the childusing form and perceiving it-is most important; it is the stimulus for the development of perception of number. Take a simple domino game and you will see that the child, without knowing how to count, can play dominoes, matching the figures 2 and 2. It is completely clear that the ordered form stimulates to a high degree and assists the development of primitive, natural arithmetic. From this it follows that various objects can be used for various systems of counting. It is completely clear that it is not always possible to count with one and the same unit. To master number perception by counting is a function different from perception of form. Thus, if the child counts cubes distributed in a cross, he gets one cube more: he counts the one that is in the middle twice because it occupies a formal place in two rows, the horizontal and the vertical. It does not occur to the child to skip this cube, that is, to deviate from the form. Our colleagues carried out such research recently. The data they collected showed that the older the children were and the higher in cultural development, the fewer such mistakes they made. If a more complex figure was presented (a square of cubes and another square that intersected the first) it resulted in a number of cubes being part of both squares. Counting in this case was even more involved. As a general rule we can make the following statement: the child who mastered fonn gave a correct count, but the child who got lost in the fonn, got lost in the count. But we only had to present squares of a different color, only had to relieve the conflict to see a decrease in the number of errors the children make. In a child standing at the break between "natural" and "cultural" arithmetic, the relation between perceiving form and arithmetical operation changes radically. If fonn helped the arithmetical operation before, now it begins to hamper the op-

152

Chapter 8

eration. We are approaching the basic methodological argument that divides teachers and psychologists into two camps. Some authors say that the child's arithmetic develops from direct perception of number figures and this means that it is necessary to study with number figures-wands, points, ones, twos. Others say that the child must be taught not with number figures where the numbers are always given in certain forms, but must rather be freed from them to pass more rapidly to mediated counting. W Laj86 and other adherents of the method of number figures showed with many experiments that the child who carries out counting operations with the help of number figures, visual samples, masters these operations much more quickly. It would seem that the argument has been settled: experiments have shown that the visual method is easier and gives better results than other methods. Actually the argument only grew more intense after experimental evidence was presented. The problem has not yet been solved by the fact that the child learns more easily with one device than with another. He masters counting operations more easily because the whole course of prior development that was preparing the child also pertained to numbers. We say that all preschool arithmetic is, to a significant degree, arithmetic of direct perception of number, direct operation with numbers. But one may ask, does this device lead to the arithmetic that adults teach the child? This can only be resolved depending on a number of conditions. If we start from the point of view that school development of arithmetic is a direct continuation of preschool development, then the adherents of number figures are right. They say that what the child did when he learned "natural" arithmetic, he will do here also, with a certain systematic, methodological approach. If we start from the other point of view and take into account that preschool arithmetic differs from school arithmetic, that the child passes from direct perception of numbers to mediated perception, then something else will be clear: although operating with number figures is easier for the child, this is not part of cultural arithmetic, but deviates from it; it consolidates the close tie between number and perception of form from which the child must free himself; it keeps the child at a lower stage of development. Thus, the following is the main point: at a certain stage of development, the child comes to understand the limitations of his arithmetic and begins to make a transition to mediated arithmetic. "TI-actors," "clocks," and other figures appear in the role of numbers. A conflict develops between our counting system and direct perception of figures. School arithmetic is the turning point. Although preschool arithmetic conflicts with school arithmetic, this does not mean that the school approaches the child purely mechanically. In the collision, a new, further stage of the development of counting appears.

Chapter 9 MASTERING ATTENTION The history of the child's attention is the history of the development of the organization of his behavior. This history begins at the moment of birth. Initial attention occurs through hereditary nerve mechanisms that organize the reflex processes according to the principle of the dominant that is familiar in physiology. This principle establishes that in the operation of the nervous system, the organizing point is a dominant focus of excitation that inhibits the process of other reflexes and is augmented at their expense. The dominant nerve process is the organic base of the process of behavior that we call attention. The first chapter in the development of the child's attention is traced through the genetic study of the child's reflexes. The study establishes how new dominants appear sequentially in the child's behavior and how the formation of complex conditioned reflexes in the cortex of the brain begins on this basis. It is extremely important to note that the formation of conditioned reflexes depends on the development of an appropriate dominant. For example, genetic studies have demonstrated a definite dependence of the formation of an associative reflex on the development of dominant processes in the central nervous system, since an associative reflex, in the words of V. M. Bekhterev, can be formed only from the receptive surface from which a dominant functional affect arises in the central nervous system. The newborn has only two dominants: the feeding dominant and the position dominant that is manifested by a change in position. Bekhterev says that with these dominants, only an association between them can be formed: a conditioned reflex in the form of a feeding reaction that arises when the child is placed in a position customary for breast feeding. Before corresponding dominants develop, no other conditioned reflexes related to other perceived surfaces can be formed. Gradually, the child develops visual, aural, and other dominants, and only with their development is the formation of new conditioned reflexes related to the eyes and ears possible. Thus, the dominant process stands at the very beginning of the formation of new connections in the cortex of the child's brain and determines the character and direction of these connections. We term this period in the development of the child, which encompasses the natural maturation of separate dominants, the period of natural or primitive development of attention. This term is based on the fact that development of attention at this time is a function of a general organic development of the child and primarily of the structural and functional development of the central nervous system. Consequently, a purely organic process of growth, maturation, and development of the nervous apparatus and functions of the child form the base of the development of attention during this period. This process is analogous to the proc-

153

154

Chapter 9

ess of evolutionary development of attention from lower to higher organisms where it can be observed with greatest clarity. We do not want to say that the organic development of attention in the child is parallel to the process of evolutionary development of attention or repeats it to any extent. However, we want to stress that these processes are similar with respect to type of development: in both cases, the base for development of attention as a specific function of behavior is the organic development or maturation of the corresponding nervous processes. This process, which occupies a dominant place in the first year of the child's life, does not stop, does not end in later childhood or even in the subsequent life of the person. The relative equilibrium and stability that we observe in the adult in comparison with the child indicates essentially only an enormous retardation of the rate, and sometimes a change in direction of the processes, but not their cessation. These seemingly attenuated and retarded processes of organic change, however, affect the operation of our attention every day, and this dependency becomes especially perceptible and clear when these seemingly attenuated processes are revived when they change in morbidity. The significance of the organic process that is the basis for development of attention moves to the background very early in comparison with the new processes of development of attention that are qualitatively different in type, specifically the processes of cultural development of attention. As cultural development of attention, we have in mind the evolution and change in the devices of control and the operation of attention, the mastery of these and their subjection to the will of the person, that is, processes analogous in type to the cultural development of other functions of behavior which we spoke of in preceding chapters. Consequently, psychological study shows that in the development of attention we can also definitely see two basic lines with which we are already familiar. We can identify the line of natural development of attention and the line of cultural development of attention. We will not consider the relation that exists between the one line and the other in the development of attention because this problem has already been adequately elucidated in preceding chapters. Our task is to trace and graphically note the path of the second line, that is, the history of the cultural development of attention. Strictly speaking, cultural development of attention also begins at a very early age, with the first social contact between the child and the adults around him. Like all cultural development, it is a social development. The cultural development of any function, including attention, consists of a person's developing a series of artificial stimuli and signs in the process of mutual living and activity. The social behavior of the personality is directed by means of these and they form the basic means through which the personality masters its own processes of behavior. In order to trace the history of the mechanisms of attention genetically, we proceeded in the same way as in the study of other processes described above. We tried to create a situation experimentally in which the child would be confronted with the task of mastering processes of his attention with the help of external stimuli-devices. Implementation of this can be found in the work of our colleague, A. N. Leont'ev,87 who developed a method of double stimulation applicable to the study of mediated processes of attention. The essence of the experiments consists of the child's being confronted with a problem that requires a long, exerted attention, a concentration on the given process. The experimenter plays a game with the child, "Questions and Answers," a game of the "Forfeit" type, with the following stipulation: "You must not say 'yes' or 'no,' or 'black' or 'white.'" The child is asked questions to some of which he

Mastering Attention

155

must respond by naming a certain color. For example: "Do you go to school? What color is the desk? Do you like to play? Were you ever in a village? What color is the grass? Were you ever in the hospital? Did you see a doctor? What color is his smock?" etc. The child must answer the questions as quickly as possible observing the instructions that he must not name the two forbidden colors, for example, black and white or red and blue, and not name the same color twice. The experiment is set up in such a way that meeting the requirements is quite possible but requires constant attentive effort on the part of the child. If the child breaks a rule and names a forbidden color or repeats the same color twice, he pays a forfeit or loses the game. An experiment set up in this way demonstrated that the task is extremely difficult for a preschool child and quite difficult even for an eight- or nine-year-old who cannot carry it out without error. Actually, the situation requires concentration of attention to an internal process on the part of the child. It requires him to control his internal attention and is beyond his ability. The course of the experiment changes radically when the child is given colored cards as an aid: black, white, cream, red, green, blue, yellow, brown, gray. The child immediately has an external auxiliary device for solving the internal problem of concentrating and intensifying attention, and he makes a transition from direct to mediated attention. As we have already said, the child must master his internal attention, but he works with external stimuli. Thus, the internal operation is carried out or at least connected with the external operation and presents us with the possibility of studying it objectively. A double-stimulus type of experiment unfolds before us. Two series of stimuli confront the child. The first is the question of the experimenter, the second, the colored cards. The second series of stimuli is the device that helps fix attention on the correct response. The result of introducing the auxiliary series of stimuli usually is very quickly apparent and the number of incorrect responses decreases rapidly, which indicates the increased stability of attention and the fact that the child masters these processes with the help of the auxiliary stimuli. Let us consider the development with age of both forms of concentration and the establishment of attention in the double-stimulus experiment. In the preschool child, both forms of attention are usually close to each other. Their divergence increases markedly during the first and especially during the second school year and becomes insignificant again in adults. 'fiacing the development of attention from the preschool child to the adult, we reach a basic conclusion. The difference in activity of mediated and direct attention increases beginning with preschool age, reaches a maximum during the second year of schooling, and then exhibits a tendency toward leveling off. Subsequently, we can easily see in the two curves that show the basic genetic law of development of attention, a picture that is essentially similar to the parallelogram of the development of memory, which we shall try to elucidate in the next chapter. In order to explain the sequence in the development of mediated attention, we must briefly trace the course of the experiment at various age levels. Here we will note mainly that in the preschool child, the difference between the number of errors with the one method and the other of directing attention is negligible and the introduction of a new device does not essentially change the course of the whole process. The preschool child does not use the stimulus-device that he is given to any significant degree. He frequently plays with the cards as if they had no connection to the task at hand and sometimes selects one randomly and is guided in his response by the suggestive influence of the card. The child who carries out the task most successfully begins to make partial use of the auxiliary device. He isolates the forbidden colors, say white and black, puts them aside and uses the colors of

156

Chapter 9

the cards that he kept. But once the color has been named, the child does not remove that color from the cards that he has. As a rule, it is only at school age that the child makes full use of the device he is given. The internal operation becomes external, and the child controls his attention with the help of the external stimuli-devices. The cards begin to be clearly differentiated into "possible" or "impossible" colors, as one of the subjects of the experiment put it; the used colors, those already named, are added to the forbidden colors. The school-age children clearly exhibit a dependence on the device by attempts to mechanize the whole operation, which frequently results in mindless responses since the children display a tendency toward being guided only by the color of the card but not by the sense of the question. Thus, in the school-age child, resorting to the stimulus-device quickly increases efficiency of internal attention, but essentially reduces the quality of the response and moves in this way toward an inexpedient use of the device he is given. Older school children use external devices most fully and most adequately; they no longer exhibit complete dependence on the cards as do the younger children. The number of errors decreases correspondingly. In the preschool child, mediated attention scarcely decreases the percentage of errors; in children in the lower grades, the percentage of errors drops by half, and in the older school children, by a factor of ten. Thus we have a seemingly sequential picture of the development of mediated attention: the processes are mastered gradually and attention is subjected to the will. Only in adults do we note again an extremely insignificant drop in number of errors when they resort to the cards. To elucidate this fact, which plays a central role in the process of development of voluntary attention, we turn to experiments in a special series that show that in an individual child, the curves of development of both processes of developing attention are similar in form. If we continue these experiments with a preschool child for a long time, within the framework of this operation, the child generally follows the same course relatively quickly. The child's behavior during the experiment will pass through the following stages sequentially: (1) incomplete and inexpedient use of the cards; (2) transition to vigorous use of the cards and complete subjection to the external devices, (3) expedient use of the cards for solving the internal task with external devices, and finally, (4) transition to the type of behavior that adults exhibit. As strange as this may seem at first glance, in our experiments, in the transition to using cards, the adult behaves very much like the preschool child if we are to judge by external appearance. The adult also makes little use of the cards; his operation has the character of using a semi-external device and he makes notes "in his mind" of the forbidden and already used colors, but does not touch the cards. Observing this behavior, which we saw in the long-term experiment with a child, we have a firm basis for assuming that this is due to a conversion of the processes, that is, to a transition from an externally to an internally mediated process.
