If we understand will in the genetic sense that we give this term, specifically as the stage of mastery of the processes of one's own behavior, then, of course, most characteristic in the mental underdevelopment of the atypical child, including the idiot, is, as we have already indicated, the divergence between his organic and his cultural development. The same lines of development that coincide in the normal child diverge in the abnormal child. The means for cultural behavior have historically been devised in terms of normal psychophysiological organization of man. Specifically these means are not suitable for a child burdened with a defect. In the deaf-mute child, the divergence due to absence of hearing is consequently marked by a purely mechanical retardation along the path of speech development, but in the retarded child, the weakness is in the central apparatus: his hearing is preserved, but his intellect is so underdeveloped that the child does not have all the functions of speech and, consequently, the function of attention. On the basis of the law of conformity between fixation and apperception, we can determine the capacity of the Idiot to learn according to eye fixation on some object. On this basis, all idiots can be considered as being incapable of any education and completely not receptive to any therapeutic-pedagogical treatment. We have already seen that the capacity to pay attention requires a natural apparatus for catalyzing any perceived sign. If this process itself is absent, if visual dominants do not develop at all, then, as we have seen from the studies of V. M. Bekhterev, there can be no closure of any conditioned reflex from this organ. An imbecile who is capable of fixation on an object has passive attention and, consequently, is capable of training. A further decisive step is the transition from passive to active attention; according to Heller, the difference between them is not in kind, but in degree. One differs from the other in that active apperception occurs in a field of attention

Mastering Attention

177

containing several perceptions that conflict with each other and the child chooses among them. The presence of choice defines the instant of transition from passive to active attention. Only at this higher stage are volitional actions connected with choice in the true sense of the word possible. In this connection, Heller recommends training retarded children to apply the method of selection by presenting them with a number of objects and requiring them to choose and indicate an object named by the instructor. We also ascribe great psychological significance to this method because we see in it only a continuation and augmentation of the indicating function of the word, which in the normal child occurs quite naturally. We would like to note the general artificiality and the child's lack of interest in this task. This point is more a technical than a basic difficulty. The selection reaction put into play becomes a potent means through which we begin to direct the attention of the child. A further development of this method when it is applied in practice may be that the child says the appropriate word to himself and then selects the required object, in other words, the child learns to stimulate his active attention himself. From the outset, the imbecile has spontaneous attention directed to various objects, but in him this function is, as a rule, extremely weak and unstable, and for this reason the usual state which we call inattention or absentmindedness in the normal child is a characteristic trait of imbeciles. Finally, mild retardation, the least serious form of retardation, is characterized by underdevelopment of thinking in concepts, which we use to deduce abstractions from a concrete perception of things. This defect may be established with experimental precision in the slightly retarded and may, in this way, indicate not only the incapacity for direct attention, but also the inability to form concepts. Let us recall, however, our experiments demonstrating how essentially significant directed attention is for processes of abstraction, and it will become clear that the mildly retarded find it impossible to form concepts mainly because of an inability to follow the course of their own attention along the very complex paths indicated for them by words. The higher function of the word, connected with development of concepts, is inaccessible for them mainly because their higher forms of voluntary attention are underdeveloped.

Chapter 10 THE DEVELOPMENT OF MNEMONIC AND MNEMOTECHNICAL FUNCTIONS In the area of memory, psychology has long ago learned to distinguish between two basic lines-the natural and the cultural-which we have tried to trace over the whole course of our research. Very long ago, psychology began to consider memory as an organic function and came very early to formulating physiological bases for this function. As E. Meumann correctly points out, in traditional psychology, memory was studied mostly and specifically as a physiological function and psychologists began very early to relate to memory the more general properties of organic material. As E. Hering claimed, memory represents a basic property of all organized matter. Actually, the plasticity of our nerve substance is expressed in its capacity to change under the influence of external actions and to preserve a predisposition toward their repetition. This was the basis for the graphic comparison between memorizing and establishing nerve pathways by a process similar to wheels making a rut in a road or to a crease formed in a sheet of paper when it is folded. A. Semon introduced the special term "mnema" to indicate the organic bases of memory, but as frequently happens in comparing psychological and physiological concepts, he himself began to consider this concept as a certain mental function, that is, an ideational function. It seems to us, however, that the word "mnema" can best be used to indicate the totality of organic functions of memory that are manifested depending on certain properties of the brain and nervous tissue. In this sense, many psychologists now speak of mnema or the mnemonic functions, isolating natural or innate memory in this way. At the same time, psychology recognizes what has for a long time been called technical memory or mnemotechnique, which is understood as the art of mastering the processes of remembering and controlling them through special technical means. Initially, mnemotechnique developed as a practical art that had the most various tasks and applications. But theoretical study of mnemotechnique was haphazard and most psychologists could not distinguish in mnemotechnique the real and true principle that is the basis of all cultural development of memory from the random and distorted form in which this principle appeared at the hands of learned scholastics and professional magicians. For this reason, we would like to propose that under the designation mnemotechnique, we understand all those devices for remembering that include the use of known external technical means and are directed toward the mastery of one's own memory. Thus, we will subsequently use these two terms, mnema and mnemotechnique, long applied in psychology with a somewhat different meaning, to designate natural 179

180

Chapter 10

or organic functions of memory, on the one hand, and cultural devices for remembering, on the other. The inadequate separation of mnema and mnemotechnique had a most unfortunate effect on the working out of the problem of memory, and the inadequately studied function of mnemotechnical remembering led many psychologists and philosophers to a completely false formulation of the problem of double memory. Thus, psychologists who did experimental studies of the processes of thinking concluded that there two kinds of memory: concept memory, on the one hand, and thinking memory on the other, and that the two kinds of memory are subject to different laws. H. Bergson,95 in his well-known research on matter and memory, concluded that there are two memories: brain memory and mental memory and each of these has its own laws. Finally, Freud also concluded that the activity of our memory may be explained only if we assume that it consists of two separate but interconnected constituent parts of the system. We believe that only research carried out scientifically which separates mnema from mnemotechnique will succeed in turning this confused problem of the two kinds of memory right side up and will provide a scientific explanation for it. We find this kind of stumbling block also in the genetic studies of memory where, regardless of much experimental work, the principal question remains unexplained: does memory develop at all significantly in childhood, does it stand still during all of childhood exhibiting insignificant fluctuations in one direction or another and, finally, as much data indicates, does it deteriorate, undergo involution and, in a certain sense, decline as the child grows and matures. It seems to us that this basic controversy can be resolved only if the two lines in the development of memory of which we have been speaking are isolated. In our studies, we tried to compare directly the two types of memory, the two methods of remembering, and to elucidate by comparative analysis the elementary composition of both operations, their structures and genesis. In experiments, we asked a child to memorize a series of words (for the most part nouns, names of concrete objects). We used standard procedures such as are used in studies of memory in experimental psychology except that we tried to make it clear to the child that it would be impossible to remember the whole series in the given order. Thcn we introduced a new method of memorizing: we gave the child a number of cards from picture lotto or specially prepared cards either of separate pictures of the concrete objects or geometric figures, lines, dotted lines, etc. We added this auxiliary material in different series in various ways. Sometimes it was presented simply with the hint: "Perhaps these cards will help you remember?" but with no explanation as to how they would help. In other cases, we gave a short instruction (we explained to the child that he must try in some way to connect the words he was to memorize with corresponding cards) and we even gave a demonstration. The different methods were intended to show how the transition to a new method of memorizing occurs, to what extent it must be an independent device and to what extent imitation, what role understanding plays in this process, etc. We shall go into this later; now we will only say that the child did make a transition from natural, innate remembering to mediated or mnemotechnical remembering. The whole character of his operation changed instantly; every assigned word now elicited reference to a picture. The child established a connection between the word and the picture, then moved on to the next word, etc. After we finished the whole series, the child, looking at the cards, reproduced all the words that he was asked to memorize and explained what connection he

Development of Mnemonic and Mnemotechnical Functions

181

made between the word and the picture. We used two different methods of presenting the material: (1) the pictures were presented to the child in a completely ordered form corresponding to the words so that each word matched the picture previously selected by the experimenter; (2) the pictures lay scattered before the child and the operation was complicated by the fact that the child had to select a picture that he thought was suitable for remembering a given word. Then, by varying the difficulty of the words, the degree of their similarity to the pictures and the pictures themselves, we had the opportunity to trace the course of the child's process of remembering. The studies showed that even at preschool age, the child is capable of mastering the operation of using auxiliary pictures for memorizing and applying them correctly. Whoever observed the direct transition from the natural method of remembering to the mnemotechnical cannot help but have the impression that he has seen a seemingly experimentally elicited change from innate to cultural memory. The process of remembering was restructured instantly in such a way that remembering any given word was accomplished through the picture that played the role of a sign. The movement of the nervous processes in such remembering can be presented graphically in the form of a triangle (Fig. 2) showing that if in natural remembering a certain connection is made between two points, mnemotechnical remembering introduces a certain new, initially neutral stimulus-card that plays the role of a mnemotechnical sign and directs the course of nerve connections along a new path replacing one nerve connection with two new connections. The whole advantage of identifying the nerve connection lies in that we master a new way of remembering and can subsequently recall the appropriate connection as we wish when we need it. However, as the experiment has shown, the graphic presentation does not correspond to the complexity of the process that was actually observed. We can easily see that the formation of a new connection is never limited to a simple associative approximation of the word the object and the sign, but assumes an active creation of a quite complex structure in which both stimuli are parts. Thus, when the child selects a picture of a camel for the word "death," he creates the following structure: ':A camel in the desert is a wanderer and dies of thirst." From the point of view of associative psychology, it would be completely impossible to explain why such relatively complex structures are remembered much more easily and stably than simple associative connections between two elements. If we assume that in this case there is a renewal of older connections and a support of the new remembering by old connections, then we might expect to find the same thing occurring in all other structures. But experiments indicate something else. They show that in a great majority of cases, the child begins to create completely new structures and does not renew the old. For example, when the child remembers the word "theater" with the aid of a picture of a crab at the shore, he creates a special auxiliary structure. "The crab is looking at the stones on the bottom, it is beautiful, for him it is a theater." If in the first example, we can also say that the child reproduces a story that he has heard many times, then in the second example, the child undoubtedly compares the "crab" and the "theater" for the first time. He created this structure exactly for remembering. From the associative point of view, it would be exceptionally difficult to explain how this complex image is remembered more easily than a simple associative connection. The heuristic significance of all our experiments consists in the following: they support indirectly the correctness of the structural law of memory in contrast to the law of association, which says that a connection is created by a simple coincidence or juxtaposition of two stimuli. The law of structure says: a connection is formed only with the development of structure, that is, a new whole in which both

182

Chapter 10

elements entering into a connection are functional parts; the better the structure is, the more easily and well it is remembered. Both cases of remembering with the aid of pictures that we used made it possible to distinguish completely precisely the two instances to which we refer. In making a selection, the child usually finds and uses the old structure; he selects the picture that most resembles the word he is given. He depends on connections established in past experience since all the pictures call forth old structures and the best of these persist. From the psychological aspect, the process of selection itself is a recollection of an old structure. If we were not afraid of the paradoxical meaning of our idea, we might say that in this case, remembering is essentially recalling if by the latter we mean the renewal and restoration of an old structure. Thus, in selecting the picture of a camel for the word "death," the child recalls a story which contained both these elements. The situation is entirely different when the child must remember a given word with the help of a given picture, when he is not permitted to make a choice, and when recalling is actually impossible. Then the child begins to create new structures actively and this is the basis of the process of mastering memory. For this reason, from the psychological aspect, in such experiments it is not memory that is studied but the active creation of structures. This is borne out also by reproduction errors. These are most frequent in free selection and, what is most important, in selection that leads to recalling. In the same experiments, for the word "to shoot," the child selected a picture of a lion, forming the structure "They shot the lion." From the same structure, in repeating, he referred to the word "gun." Such errors are very rare when a structure is created: here, as we see, new factors enter into action, specifically direction of the whole structure toward the word that the child must remember. We shall speak about this later. Experiments provide an exceptionally important indication of the essence of the change that occurs in the process of cultural development of memory: some mental operations are replaced by others, replacement of functions so characteristic for all of the development of higher mental functions. The whole second operation of remembering externally retains the same form and results specifically in reproducing the given word. But the paths by which the child comes to the result are profoundly different. If in the first case, we dealt with the effect of the mnema, remembering in the organic sense of the word, then in the second case, the child replaces direct remembering with such operations a~ comparison, isolating the common factor, imagination, etc., which leads to the creation of the required structure. The child makes up little stories or imagines something new while looking at the picture. All of these new functions serve remembering, replace its simple forms, and a clear distinction can be drawn between the operation of direct remembering and other ancillary operations that replace it. Ancillary operations may be reestablished more or less fully according to the structure that they generate, but the problem of remembering as such is not yet solved by this. It is not enough to reestablish the whole given structure-it is important that one know how to find the word in it that was to have been remembered. Isolating a given word in a structure and the ability to control the whole operation of remembering are also inherent functions of memory. We can say that analysis of mnemotechnical remembering discloses three basic operations which combine to produce a complex mnemotechnical operation. The first consists of what can conditionally be called an instrumental act: this is general control of the operation, using a sign as a means in the operation of remembering. Then there are various and alternating operations of creating a new structure or simple remembering and isolating a common trait or something else.

Development of Mnemonic and Mnemotechnical Functions

183

Finally, the third and most important operation: isolating within the new large structure the word that must be remembered and reproduced. Strictly speaking, this is already the function of indicating, or attention in the true sense of that word, since here the whole structure is reproduced in its entirety and finding the required word at both the moment of remembering it and the moment of reproducing it can only be done by turning one's attention to it. The required word is as if marked by an X, by an indicating sign that places it in the center of the field of attention. We have the following evidence that all three parts enter into the make-up of the rnnemotechnical operation: each of them can exist without the other two. Thus, very frequently, the full mastery of the first operation occurs without the subject's knowing how to form a structure in a given case. Generally, the child understands very well that he must remember with the help of a card and he has done this successfully many times. In this case, he also resorts to the picture first of all, but for the child the process itself of creating a structure is impossible at this point. His capabilities for making combinations, his imagination, his abstraction, thinking, and other functions refuse to operate, as we have often observed, as soon as the relationship between the word and the corresponding picture becomes very complex. Frequently, the state of the problem and not knowing how to set up the appropriate connection are the point of departure for the formation of a structure. Specifically, the absence of a connection begins to serve the connection. What is completely incongruous, what has nothing in common is remembered specifically by this trait, and a structure of the absurdity type is formed. As one of the subjects said, "I remembered this like a fish at a funeral." We noted that it was specifically the distinct turning of attention to the given word and not knowing how to turn away from it, to broaden its meaning or isolate some detail in it that are the impediments to the formation of the necessary ancillary structure. It is enough only to nudge the child's stuck attention that is fixed on the given word and move it to related words or to a part of the object itself and the necessary structure will be formed. In such cases, we elicit experimellaliy the dropping out of a link in rnnemotechnical remembering. We frequently observe the opposite. The child himself is capable of forming structures and forms them surprisingly easily especially when we question him, but he does not have the first point of the operation: he does not understand that such a formation of structures can be used for remembering and he does not know that two parts of the structure are interconnected in such a way that one can reestablish the other. For this reason, he does not guess that the picture may be used as a sign. This is particularly clear when the child, led to the idea of imagining structures, creates a story himself, but does not bring a picture into it and does not know how to use it as a sign. Here we have the first link experimentally isolated. Finally, we can also isolate the third link experimentally: the function of indicating that plays a most important role in voluntary reproduction which consists of the fact that a selection must be made from among the mass of emerging images. We believe that here a special direction or sign indicating how the required selection must be made is helpful. Examples of absence of selection are the reproductions that miss the mark, about which we have already spoken. The child reproduces some word related to the given structure, he may even reproduce the structure as a whole, but not the word that was given. In the problem of memory in child psychology, the basic path of development of this function has not yet been elucidated. Does memory develop during childhood at all significantly or not? Available results give no unequivocal answer. They led psychologists to the most contradictory conclusions.

184

Chapter 10

Thus, A. Bain96 believed that maximum efficiency of memory occurs at ages six to ten, after which memory no longer develops but regresses. Others maintained that a child's memory improves continuously. Finally, a third group of authors, such as Meumann, tried to separate the very concept of memory into various functions and to show that while one function, specifically the ability to memorize develops rapidly, another ability-for remembering directly-deteriorates. When the question is formulated in this way, the problem itself of the development of memory is divided into two separate channels, each of which takes its own course. The problem can be resolved scientifically only if it is broken down. It seems that a child's memory evolves in certain respects and undergoes involution in other respects. The memory curve separates into two branches, one going up and other down; memory improves and deteriorates simultaneously. All the facts collected by researchers are a basis for the assumption that memory as such, its organic base, evidently does not develop in childhood at all noticeably; it is possible that it even contracts. However, the ability to memorize increases extremely rapidly and very remarkably in the child. The essence of the change, the rapid and vigorous rise in the development of memory, consists of the fact that memory becomes active, voluntary, that the child masters his memory, that he moves from instinctive, mechanical memory to memory based on intellectual functions and develops voluntary memory. Thus, psychology has for a long time been indicating the two basic directions in which memory development proceeds during childhood. The first-the intellectualization of the mnema-and the second-the active or voluntary character of remembering. However, no psychologist was able to determine more precisely that either the one process or the other is taking place in memory. All studies bring us to the conclusion that the key to the development of memory in childhood must be found in the change in methods of remembering. Our task will be to trace the evolution of these methods and to show that they occur through the four basic stages that we noted above. In our studies, we tried to trace the comparative development of the three methods of remembering in childhood. As has been said, we presented a series of words of comparable difficulty to be remembered. These had to be remembered by three different methods: the simple, natural method of retention and the rnnemotechnical method with mandatory and with voluntary selection of a picture. The data collected showed that all three methods of remembering exhibit a very characteristic and unique evolution at different ages. If we agree to call thc coefficient of natural memory the average percentage of retained words in direct remembering and the coefficient of rnnemotechnical memory, the average percentage of words retained with the second method and try to trace the relations of the coefficients in the process of the development of the child, we will establish the following basic fact that IS obtamed consistently under various research conditions: the relation between both coefficients is not a constant value but varies during the course of the child's development and changes strictly according to pattern. During preschool age, both coefficients are close to each other; the difference between them in methods of remembering is insignificant and the transition to mnemotechnical remembering does not noticeably increase the efficiency of memory. The child using a sign remembers approximately as much without the sign. At an earlier age, there is even the opposite interrelation of coefficients; the transition to remembering with the help of pictures only confuses the child, diverts him, since it presents a problem of considerably greater complexity. The child's attention is divided and the pictures confuse him. In this case, behavior is often observed that is characteristic for an earlier age. The picture is included passively in the associative

Development of Mnemonic and Mnemotechnical Functions

185

series and the child sometimes finds a connection between the word and the picture, but looking at the picture, he does not produce the given word, but the name of the picture itself or some other word associatively connected with it. We can trace how the improperly used sign diverts the child from correctly recalling the word and makes it more difficult or inhibits his work. The mnemotechnical coefficient is lower than the mnemonic coefficient. However, if we trace subsequent relations of the coefficients, we see that the curves begin to diverge as the child grows. While direct remembering (as was established by previous research) increases very slowly and displays a tendency toward almost horizontal movement forward, mnemotechnical remembering evolves rapidly, and the curves that represent the height of both coefficients diverge sharply. Their clearest opposition is apparent usually in the preschool years; later both curves again change direction. The lower curve that represents processes of direct remembering begins to rise quite rapidly and sharply toward the upper and to grow, while the upper curve, conversely, rises more slowly and gradually levels off. The relation of the curves changes radically after the transitional point. Instead of the ever increasing divergence that we observed earlier, we now again see a trend toward convergence of the curves due to a retardation of the upper curve and a sharp rise in the lower curve. We can again speak of the tendency toward convergence of the curves toward a single point. In this way, both curves, exhibiting a development of natural and cultural remembering, form an exceptionally unique relation. It is this: the two convex curves are turned toward each other with their concave sides and approach each other at their lower and upper points. This arbitrary graphic representation, which we call the parallelogram of the development of memory, establishes to a high degree what we believe to be an important basic genetic law of memory. This phenomenon was first noted by A. N. Leont'ev in the processing of experimental data and he was the first to formulate it as a law. Since the data was collected on a very large number of children and adults, we are justified in concluding that it represents the genetic curve in the development of memory and specifically, of its two basic methods. 97 We are justified in assuming that each specific child or each specific group of children will, in subsequent growth, exhibit the same characteristics in the evolution of both methods of remembering that was exhibited in the general mass of older children. In other words, we are justified in seeing in these curves the path that the development of a child's memory takes in the process of his developing into an adult. The parallelogram of the development of memory remains to be explained at least hypothetically. An explanation in the light of what we know about the development of higher mental functions of the child consists of the following: mnemotechnical memory must be considered as a process of mastering memory with the aid of external stimuli-signs. This operation becomes possible for the child only over the years, only gradually, only with the increase in his cultural development; the divergence of both curves in the left half of our diagram* can be explained by the fact that increase in cultural memory with a more or less unchanged size of natural memory consists in ever greater mastery of these processes. The child's memory grows rapidly and this means that the child quickly learns to master the processes of remembering, to improve them and control them. Both forms of 'Editor's note: Concrete data and corresponding diagrams of the discussed parallelogram of development are presented in A N. Leont'ev: Problems of Mental Development, Moscow, 1959, pp. 346, 347, 348 (Figs. 39, 40, 41).

186

Chapter 10

remembering reach maximum divergence when the child is of school age, when he has advanced significantly in cultural development, but has remained at an earlier stage with respect to direct retention. Subsequent movement of the curves must be explained in part by specific conditions under which the experiments were done. The total amount of material produced by remembering was so sparse that a school-age child came close to reaching the maximum. To a significant degree, this explains the fact that the curve of mnemotechnical memory exhibits a continuous deceleration in rate of increase. However, if we were to present more material for memorizing consisting of several dozen or even hundreds of words, we would easily see, as our subsequent experiments will show, that the curve would again exhibit a sharp upward trend. Most interesting and remarkable at first glance is the rise of the curve of direct remembering at an age when we might least expect it. We are inclined to explain this phenomenon preliminarily by the fact that the rapid rise in direct remembering is evidence of profound internal changes that occur in direct remembering affected by mediated remembering. We are inclined to think that there is a kind of revolution of mnemotechnical devices for remembering, that the child turned from external to an internal use of a sign and that in this way, direct remembering actually became mnemotechnical remembering, but based only on internal signs. A threefold reason convinces us of this. First, there are the experimental data that confirm our hypothesis indirectly from various aspects. This pertains particularly to the observation we have made many times: in direct remembering, the child, and especially the adult, resorts to mnemotechnical remembering using objects in his environment as well as internal connections and interpretation. Everything that in previous experimental studies of memory was represented as a broad use of auxiliary means pertains to this also. G. Miiller correctly indicated that natural auxiliary means are based on the same principle as mnemotechnique and may be termed natural mnemotechnique or internal mnemotechnique arising on the basis of external mnemotechnique. The next reason suggested by experiment is that the youth, and especially the adult, does not resort to remembering with the aid of external stimuli but uses internal means, actively makes connections between the memorized words and the content of a preceding experiment and organizes the word into one pattern or another, etc. Finally, data from experiments with the selection reaction and other experiments, mentioned in part above and cited in part below, show that in the process of the experiment itself, the child makes a transition from using external means to using internal means. Finally, a whole series of experimental ideas completes the special study that had as its goal tracing the process of such a turning inward, for example, training internal mediated remembering in a given concrete situation. The results of the study showed that in a specially organized series, the child actually moves in a quite short time from external mnemotechnical remembering to internal remembering. The curves of these experiments are very much like the curve of the genetic parallelogram of memory. Based on the data obtained, we can assume the following: what occurs in the child in the process of trained turning inward with respect to the special task also occurs with respect to the development of memory in general in an infinitely expanded form. Now, of course, we can speak only of a hypothetical elucidation of the development and we cannot yet say how this process, symbolically represented in our diagrams, actually takes place.

Development of Mnemonic and Mnemotechnical Functions

187

Does something occur that is actually somewhat similar to what we see in the specially organized experiment, that is, does the child make a transition in each separate situation in solving each specific problem from the external to the internal sign, and does his memory develop through an accumulation of separate turning inward of signs and by accumulating and combining separate points? There is much to support such a hypothesis, since observation shows that, on the one hand, in various situations the child solves different problems in different ways. In other words, from the genetic aspect, the child's memory is not at a single level everywhere but represents a kind of complex geological layering of various epochs of development. On the other hand, however, we can scarcely assume that the child is limited in the development of memory to the processes indicated and that these processes specifically play the decisive role. It is most probable that such points very likely prepare the process of memory development rather than complete it, that the development itself probably is completed by vigorous steps and that a turning in of a structural type occurs here while the device itself, the operation itself, is developing and the prolifically developing internal experience presents an already prepared and diverse system of so-called representations or trace stimuli that may be used as signs. In this way, we might present the development of memory according to a genetic diagram that we noted above for the development of higher mental functions in general. According to this diagram we might be able to sequence the change in method of remembering indicated by the actual data. We might say that at the beginning of the development of memory there is a purely mechanical remembering corresponding in our diagram to the primitive stage in the development of any function. It is precisely primitive memory that can explain the productive memory of children markedly apparent in initial motor habits, the easy assimilation of speech, retaining of a great number of objects, that is achieved in the first years of life and in comparison with which all subsequent accumulation of memory seems attenuated at best. This is followed by a stage of naive psychology in the application of memory, a stage which can be traced experimentally with greatest clarity and which consists in that the child, once he is convinced that the picture helps him remember, tries subsequently to resort to the picture again, but still does not know how to do it. An example of this may be cases from our experiments cited below, but we were not completely successful in observing how development of memory actually occurs in the second stage. Next follows the stage of internal rnnemotechnical remembering that alternates with the stage of turning inward or logical memory. Here, as the experiment shows, the line of memory separates into two: from the external mnemotechnical sign there are two paths leading to further development of memory. The first consists in the transition from the external to the internal process of remembering, that is, in the development of so-called voluntary memory. In our experiments we were able to observe that a child creates yet another path for the development of memory. He begins to develop systematic devices of "recording" or writing. Psychological studies have shown that writing developed as an auxiliary means for memory. The initial incisions made on trees, the knots that were tied for remembering were also the forebears of our modern writing. The same thing happens in the child. We ask the child to memorize a series of numbers using the natural method at first, the method of direct retention, and when he cannot do this, we give him an abacus for remembering. The child makes a transition from direct remembering to recording the numbers on the abacus and instantly the whole system of his internal operation changes.

188

Chapter 10

Experiments showed that natural remembering, of a series of five or six numbers is impossible for young children. But by using abacuses, every number mentioned is placed in a separate row and then is reproduced according to the number of beads. With a little difficulty, the zero is noted by one bead and the child distinguishes it from one by its place in the series. Two-digit numbers are placed in two rows and the child does not divide the number into tens and units but in reproducing them, takes into account both numbers in corresponding groups. We note here a very interesting relation between natural and cultural remembering, since one bead on the abacus can represent a unit, a zero, or a part of a two-digit number. The child differentiates either according to the location of the bead in the row or according to natural memory that is combined with the cultural memory, and the child, already quite competent in various methods of remembering, says: "I remembered it this way." Also, the order on the abacus does not always correspond to the order of counting, but in reproducing it, the child reestablishes the required order with the help of natural memory. Thus, making use of notation on an abacus for fixing the series of numbers leads to combining natural and cultural devices for remembering. Natural memory participates in differentiation of units, zero and eleven, in reproducing order, and in reading two-digit numbers, and cultural memory, in notations for recording zero and in recording numbers greater than ten. Here the general basic law that we are inclined to extend to all cases of memory that uses recording is apparent: natural processes are a function determined by cultural devices. Natural processes are applied where there are no cultural devices and are directed toward promoting the use of the latter. The use of cultural devices lightens the load for natural activity, eliciting in the child a positive reaction, and drastically changes his relation to the work. With further complication of the number series, the system of recording becomes more and more complex and requires special effort of natural memory for distinguishing the different signs. We observed these kinds of natural processes for inception of writing from tasks of memory in other experiments. For example, if we give a child a number series, we can observe how the child, guided by our suggestion or independently, involves completely peripheral, neutral material in the operation of remembering and begins to make special notes or a special record that helps him remember. In the experiment, the child creates tallies, that is, numerical records such as are widely used by people, without knowing how to count. Cards, chips, strings, and cubes lie before him and he discovers the required functional meaning of these objects. The child guesses, makes marks, and usually moves to "recording" knots, to removing or adding cards, to making tallies, etc. Most essential is the fact that the child carries out a series of operations externally in order to solve the internal problem of remembering. This result, which seems banal at first glance, which we all seem to know, which consists of remembering with the aid of writing, is disclosed in the experiment as a genetic fact. We are now able for the first time to pinpoint the moment of transition itself, the moment of inventing writing, and second, to explain at once the profound change that takes place in the child in the transition from direct to mediated remembering. Therefore, we can consider as established the two basic lines in the subsequent development of cultural memory. One leads to logical remembering and the other, to writing. We will try to trace the latter line in detail in the chapter on writing. The middle ground between the one line and the other in the development of memory is occupied by so-called verbal memory, that is, remembering with the aid of words.

Development of Mnemonic and Mnemotechnical Functions

189

Psychology noted long ago that language, according to the expression of G. Kompeire, is a mnemotechnical tool that introduces substantial changes into the process of remembering, and for this reason verbal memory is essentially mediated memory or memory aided by signs. When we remember not the things or experiences, but their verbal record, we abbreviate, order, and abstract and, using the word, profoundly change the form of the material that is to be remembered. We tried to compare experimentally the description of an eidetic image and a verbal description of the same picture with exactly the same kind of reproduction. It is quite difficult to detect the difference between the two methods of remembering because the description of an eidetic image also consists of words, that is, it is given in essentially the same kind of record, but it is possible, nevertheless, to see what the degree of difference is in remembering in words and direct remembering. The presence of verbal memory indicates that each person, in the expression of Binet, is his own mnemotechnician. In experiments on remembering with pictures, we very frequently came upon cases where the child remembered not the picture, but its name, its verbal record. We had only to change the name of the picture during the reproduction and the given word was not recalled. One and the same picture that was initially termed "tiger," which the child then called "lioness," prevented reproduction of the required word. But if the child were reminded of the initial term for the picture, then he succeeded in the operation. By deliberately presenting a picture with two possible names or arbitrarily changing the name of one and the same picture during the experiment, we were convinced repeatedly that in addition to the two stimuli-the picture and the word-a third stimulus, the name of the picture, developed. We tried to isolate the role of the name of the picture experimentally, asking for it during training and reproduction, giving a false name, changing it, presenting pictures with a double name, etc. For example, the child was given the word "fight." He selected a picture of two oxen; then the child recalled the words "to work," pointing to the picture and explaining: "This is a plowed field." Previously, the picture was described as "two cows walking," which was linked to the word ''work.'' Now the new name for the picture, "plowed field," was linked with the new word "work." Bergson worked out this thesis in his important study of matter and memory, showing that two memories theoretically independent of each other must be differentiated: mechanical, motor memory that results in the formation of a habit and mental memory linked to voluntary activity that takes the form of independent or spontaneous memory. Bergson gives an example that makes it possible to integrate the one with the other: Imagine that we are studying some lesson and must repeat something many times. As a result of multiple repetitions, we form the ability to recall the lesson as if by motor habit in which the other repetitions are erased, are summarized, are submerged in the total result. This is the first type of memory. But in addition to this result of remembering, I have another memory of the lesson. I can recall how I studied it the first time, the sixth time, or the last time. I can specifically remember a given single episode of learning, and this remembering represents the second type of memory. Bergson considers the first memory a function of the brain, and the second, a function of the mind. What is most remarkable here is that with respect to the difference between one type of memory and the other, Bergson points directly to the coincidence between the second device for remembering and rnnemotechnical devices. He suggests that an analysis of the devices devised by rnnemotechnology would show that these devices are especially useful in spontaneous remembering, which is cryptic, and put it under our control as active memory.

190

Chapter 10

Which device does mnemotechnology recommend? Each of us selects these devices more or less unconsciously. The talent of the rnnemonist consists in capturing in a segment of prose the outstanding ideas, short phrases, or simple words that call forth whole pages. Here we approach a most important point from which a whole philosophical perspective opens. In the present research we will not attempt to delineate it completely. In passing we will say that another great philosopher, Spinoza,98 saw evidence for constraint of our spirit specifically in memory. He said that we can do nothing with respect to spirit if we do not remember it. Actually, the decisive role of memory in the study of intention shows to what degree all of our intentions are linked to the recognized apparatus of memory which must subsequently realize them. Not in vain did Meumann, in analyzing a voluntary action, come to the conclusion that it very often resembles a mnemotechnical operation. H. H0ffding, citing the words of Spinoza, indicates that each intention is distinct because memory participates in it. "Intention is only a slave of memory," writes H0ffding, citing Shakespeare. But we shall discuss this question in greater detail in the chapter on will. In conclusion, we will touch upon the question of the development of memory in the abnormal child. The experiments we discussed above were also carried out with the abnormal (retarded) child and showed that in such a child, mechanical, primitive memory is dominant over mediated memory. For this reason, he experiences greater difficulty in making the transition to higher forms of remembering, but in principle, his remembering does not differ from the remembering of the normal child. Thus these studies show complete agreement with what we know in general about memory of retarded children. The material collected establishes three basic positions. First, among retarded and primitive children, eidetic memory is more widespread than among normal children. Second, the function of active remembering in retarded children in general is inferior to that of normal children. Third, thus far the fact that very frequently retarded children have exceptional ability for rnnemotechnical remembering has hardly been explained. There are outstanding artists with respect to memory whose memory is limited to some narrowly specialized area. Every institution in which many such children gather will, without fail, point to a child gifted with exceptional mechanical memory. These situations can be scientifically explained if we are able to distinguish the two lines in the development of the retarded child: the natural and the cultural, the separation of which is the basic idea of this entire chapter.

Chapter 11 DEVELOPMENT OF SPEECH AND TIDNKING The subject of this chapter-the development of speech and thinking, especially development of higher forms of thinking during childhood-is difficult and complicated. For this reason I will allow myself to begin with what is simplest: with the best known concrete facts, so elementary that I am afraid I will be reproached for extreme simplification of a great problem. But I see no other way to approach the enormous and complex problem immediately from the theoretical aspect. I would like to begin with a well-known experiment-with the attempt to determine the main stages in the development of a child's thinking according to the story he tells about a picture. We know that the device that Binet proposed, which was widely used by W Stern, is extremely simple and clear. A simple picture, for example, of a city or country family, or prisoners in a jail, is presented to a child of 3, 7, or 12 years, and an explanation is given of how each of them describes the same subject. The researchers say: since all the children were given the same subject to think about, it seems that thinking develops at early very important stages since it is evident in the story of the child. We also know what conclusions have been drawn from such an experiment. These are the conclusions on which much in the psychology of thinking has been based. What happens is that children of early preschool age name the individual objects and from this the conclusion is drawn that the preschool child thinks of the world as a system of separate things. The school-age child establishes some simple actions carried out by the people or functions of the objects in the picture and from this the conclusion is drawn that the school-age child thinks of the world as a system of acting objects and people. Finally, we know that the older school-age child makes a transition to the stage of signs and later to the stage of relationships and that he understands complex relations between separate objects. From this, the conclusion is drawn that the older school-age child perceives the world as a system of complex relations in which people and things interact. The central fact that is of the essence for the psychology of thinking is a review of the positions which we have just indicated. Doubts concerning the significance of the data obtained in the experiment with the stories about pictures arose a long time ago, and it was necessary to raise these doubts in people who approached the problem very simply. What does the experiment actually say? At first the child perceives objects, then actions, later, relations, that is, the connection between things. Is this really similar to what we know about the development of the child in general? Let us try to extend this series further back and we will see how the child perceives the picture or the world at an even earlier age. Obviously, he must perceive not only objects or things, but the smallest properties and qualities of things because a thing itself is quite a complex connection of separate traits and relations. 191

192

Chapter 11

We might say directly that everything that we know about the child contradicts this notion. Everything that we know about the child says that the child of early and preschool age perceives things as a segment of reality with an extremely concrete connectedness of these things. The initial perception of separate objects that we ascribed to the child on the basis of the experiment with the picture is actually a much later stage that appears in the child's subsequent development, and everything that we know about the development of thinking in the very young child favors the idea that this experiment as it continues leads by some miracle to a wrong notion, that is, exactly to an opposite process of development of thinking in the child. The child thinks rather in whole connected clusters. This is termed syncretism. Syncretism is the feature of the child's thinking that makes it possible for him to think in whole clusters without breaking them up or without separating one object from another. The syncretic nature of the child's thinking, that is, thinking in whole situations, whole connected parts, is so strong that it persists even in the area of verbal thinking in the school child and is a reorganizing form of thinking in the school-age child. Specifically the inability to isolate the individual thing, to name it, is very clearly apparent in two examples that I borrow from Piaget. The child is asked, "Why is the sun hot?" He answers, "Because it is yellow, because it is high up and stays high up." For such a child, "explaining" means bringing in a series of other facts and properties, impressions and observations that are directly connected with a single impression, a single image. That the sun stays up and does not fall, that it is yellow and hot, that there are clouds around it, all of this is what the child sees, what is linked together; he does not separate one thing from another. In the older child, syncretism results in a shifting, that is, a combining of everything with everything that only seems superficially united. This persists in the schoolage child in speech: the child operates with such syncretic wholes. Blonskii correctly calls this property unconnected connectedness of thinking. "Unconnected"-this is understandable: of course the child thinks, pointing out that the sun does not fall because it is hot. In this, much seems unconnected. Also, this is correctly called "connectedness," because the child connects what we, the adults, never fail to separate. For him, the facts that the sun is yellow and that it does not fall are merged into one impression that we divide. Thus, syncretism consists of an unconnected connectedness of thinking, that is, in a dominance of a subjective connection, a connection that arises from a direct impression, over an objective connectedness. Unconnected ness and subjective general connectedness are derived from this. The child perceives in such a way that for him everything is connected with everything. From the objective aspect, this means that the child assumes the connection of impressions to be a connection of things. What is for the child a connection of impressions, he perceives as a connection of things. What happens in this case in the brain of the child is relatively well known from the physiological aspect: this is very well reflected in Pavlov's interesting point on irradiation, that is, an initially dispersed, diffuse stage of excitation which accompanies a first impression that gives rise to a whole complex connected with this impression. How did psychologists of previous times, psychologists-subjectivists, imagine the development of thinking to be? They indicated that the state of a newborn child could be represented as chaos of some kind of sensations, primarily a chaos of unconnected things, for where would a connection arise when there is no experience? The child never saw objects, let us say, a bed, a person, a table, chair. If only organs of sensation function, then naturally the child must experience chaos

Development of Speech and Thinking

193

of what he sees, a mixture of hot and sweet, black and yellow, various unconnected sensations and properties of objects. Gradually sensations accumulate and groups are formed from separate sensations. From this, things develop, then things are organized into groups and finally, the child makes the transition to perceiving the world. However, experimental studies show that things go quite the opposite way. The very young child perceives the world as syncretic-whole large groups or situations. Still another physiological consideration speaks in favor of this. Pavlov studied the properties of the so-called complex of stimuli and demonstrated that a complex of certain stimuli elicits a different reaction than does each stimulus taken by itself, separate stimuli or taken together. In Pavlov's laboratory, initially work was done with separate stimuli and then with a complex of stimuli. Thus, in laboratory practice, an experiment with separate stimuli is set up first, then an experiment with a complex of stimuli. And what happens in the child's life? I think that at first the child deals with a complex of impressions and objects, with the situation as a whole. The mother feeds the child; this means that the mother is the stimulus, her clothing, face, voice; then the child is picked up and placed in a certain position; there is satisfaction with feeding and the child is put to bed. This is the whole situation that unfolds before the child. For this reason, Pavlov says that while in the laboratory we arrived at a complex of stimuli later, in life, genetically for the child, the complex of stimuli comes first, and the child initially thinks in terms of a complex and later in terms of separate objects. It is easy to see, however, that the experiment with perception of pictures says the opposite. Still another factual consideration. The experiment with the pictures indicates that a child of three sees separate objects and an older child thinks of the world as a system of effects. It develops that if one and the same picture (let us say, the prisoner in jail) is shown to a three-year-old, he will say, "a man, another man, a window, a mug, a bench," but for a preschool child it would be: '~ man is sitting, another is looking out the window and a mug is on the bench," We know that the opposite is true: both the three-year-old and the very young child resolve all the separate objects according to their functions, that is, they determine them through actions. For the child, it is actions that are primary. When we try to find the first, primary word, then we find that this is the name of an action and not an object; the child names a word that signifies an action, then a word that signifies an object. Summarizing the data, we come to this conclusion: a fatal contradiction has arisen between the development of thinking as presented by the story with the picture and everything that we know about the development of thinking from life. In both cases, the relations seem to be inverted. It is curious that all of these notions can be confirmed by experiments and facts. We can take a thousand children and show yet again that this is the case with the picture. It is an incontrovertible fact, but it must be interpreted differently. We will make one of the simplest observations that we can explain, and this will indicate the path to a new interpretation. If everything that we know about the thinking of a child contradicts what the story with the picture presents, then everything that we know about the child's speech is confirmed by it. We know that the child uses single words at first, then phrases; later the child accumulates a series of word fragments and facts. A five-year-old establishes a connection between words in a single sentence, and an eight-year-old uses complex, additional sentences. A theoretical assumption arises: can the story about the picture describe the child's thinking? Can we believe that the child thinks the way he

194

Chapter 11

speaks, in naive expressions? Can the matter be different genetically perhaps: the picture establishes only the fact that the child creates phrases from isolated words, then connects the words more and more into sentences and, finally, makes a transition to a connected story? Perhaps the child does not think of the world initially as separate things, then actions, and later traits and relations? Perhaps the child speaks initially in separate words, then simple sentences, and later combines these sentences? Let us conduct an experiment because only experiments can give a definitive answer. There are several simple ways to do this that seem to me to be extremely clever. We will try to exclude the child's speech and we will try to get responses to the picture by some other means and not through words. If the proposition is true that the child does not think of the world as separate things but can say only separate words and cannot form their connections, then we will try to get along without words. We will ask two children not to tell a story, but to perform what the picture shows. It develops that the children's play about the picture sometimes lasts 20 or 30 minutes, and primarily and most of all in the play, those relations are captured that are in the picture. To put it more simply, if the child is asked to dramatize the picture and not to tell its story, then, according to the experiments of Stern, the four- or five-year-old child dramatizes the prison picture the way a twelve-year-old tells it. The child understands very well that the people are in jail: here the complex narration about how the people were caught, how they were taken, that one looks out the window, and that he wants to be free is added. Here a very complex narration is added about how the nanny was fined for not having a ticket on the trolley. In a word, we get a typical portrayal of what we see in the story of the twelve-year-old. At this point the psychologist's eyes are opened to the process of the child's thinking, to the history of the development of the child's thinking as the story about the picture shows and as he displays it in dramatization. Let us move on to another aspect of this experiment. We will try to do what other experimenters have done before us. We will try to show whether it is true that a three-year-old does not perceive relations, but perceives separate things or objects and that the connection between them is established later. If this is so, then we would expect that if in the experiment we let the child operate with things which are only slightly connected, then the child will not see the connection and will use the objects as separate, unconnected objects. Eliasberg worked on this and developed a method for substantiating the special experiment. This is its essence. A number of bright pieces of paper are placed on the table and the child is brought to the table and given no instruction, but sometimes his attention is called to the papers lying before him. The papers are of two colors: bright red and blue. The child reaches for the papers and turns them over. A cigarette is pasted under one of the papers (under the blue). The child turns his attention to it and tries to pull the cigarette away. What is he going to do next? If the child is at the stage which was evident in the test-with the story about the picture-then we might expect that next the child will work with the separate papers or at least with several papers and will not establish any connection or relation between the objects. The experiment shows the opposite. The child, beginning at the age of one and a half, two, and, as a rule, three, always forms a very ordinary connection between the blue paper and the finding of the cigarette. When the papers are placed randomly, the child opens only the blue and leaves the red. If the color of the papers is changed after the first trial and the red and blue are replaced by orange and brown, the child proceeds in the same way. He opens the orange and opens

Development of Speech and Thinking

195

the brown in which he finds the cigarette and again establishes a connection between the color and the cigarette. It is very interesting that he establishes the connection much more quickly than the older school child for whom the experiment and the whole situation are much more divided into separate things that have nothing in common with each other. An adult establishes the connection even less successfully than the older school child. For this reason, Eliasberg believes it improbable that the child who easily establishes the relation between the things would, in the simpler experiment, think of the world as consisting of separate objects and would not be able to imagine the connection-a man standing at the window-and would see only a man and a window. For Eliasberg, the experiments with nonverbal children, with mute and deafmute children, were of decisive significance. Many experiments with analysis of nonverbal behavior lead us to conclude that the very young child may be thought to have a tendency to connect everything with everything else; as the experiment has demonstrated, for him the extraordinary difficulty lies in breaking the connection, in being able to isolate separate instances. For this reason, the common notion that the child does not connect actions with each other is dispelled. The correctness of the genetic curve in the development of thinking produced by the story based on a picture has been in doubt for a long time. Stern called attention to the fact that if the task of thinking is difficult for the child, he drops to a lower level. If the child sees a very complicated picture, the twelve-year-old will begin a narration like that of a seven-year-old and the seven-year-old, like that of a three-year-old. To demonstrate this, Stern asked a child to write a story about the picture he was looking at. Again all the children forced to use written language to tell the story dropped down to a lower level still. This experiment was a triumph for Stern. The experiment demonstrated that when the task became more complicated (if we study reconstructing the picture from memory), the quality of the narration declined. Consequently, from the superficial impression that formed about the process of thinking, it might be assumed that initially there was thinking about separate things, then about the actions of these things, next about traits and, finally, about connections between the things. But there is still another group of experiments that overturn all of Stern's constructs, and one can only wonder how it was that these experiments did not lead to a review of the problem from this aspect. The first experiment consists of the following. Among children from different social strata-peasants and cultured city children, retarded and normal-we find that in telling a story from a picture, the peasant child living in Germany drops back in development at the transition from stage to stage in comparison with children from other social strata. When Stern compared the thinking of children from cultured and noncultured strata, he found that in everyday thinking, there was almost no difference between the children of the two different strata, and in many cases there was almost no difference in the content of their thinking. Conversely, the experiment analyzing speech of children from various social strata showed complete agreement with data on the development of speech; for example, observations of development of verbal speech and syntax of peasant children show that the child describes the picture in the same way that he ordinarily speaks. A simple conclusion might follow from this, and if psychologists could be convinced of it then no further experiments would be necessary. But psychologists adhere to a different view, believing that the story about the picture is not an indication of how the child speaks in everyday life, but how he speaks under specific experimental conditions. Another experiment, to which Blonskii called attention and which also led to a review of all experiments with stories about a picture, shows that if we ask a

196

Chapter 11

child to write a story rather than tell it, then we see immediately that a twelveyear-old tells the story like a three-year-old. The written story of a twelve-year-old resembles the verbal story of a three-year-old. Can we really believe that it was only that we gave the child a pencil that made the task of thinking more difficult? If the child writes poorly, does that mean that in thinking he immediately drops from the stage of relations to the stage of objects? This is not true. But that a child at twelve writes like a child of three speaks is a fact. To put it more simply, this means that the story based on a picture yields an inverted picture of the child's development of thinking. Actually the story reflects the stage at which one form or another of the child's speech is; if we move to written language, then the experiment reflects specifics of the child's written language. The confusion in child psychology arose because psychologists could not distinguish the development of speech from the development of thinking-this is the most important conclusion from which a theoretical consideration of this problem begins. In an analysis of the test using a picture, we showed that if the test is not used sufficiently critically, it can lead us into error, that is, it may falsely show a path of development of the child's perception of the world and the child's thinking about the world. Moreover, experimental confirmation of a very young child's perception, excluding his speech, shows that the child does not perceive the world as a number or sum of separate things, that his perception is syncretic in character, that is, is integral, more or less combined in groups, that his perception and representation of the world is situational. If we approach these facts from the point of view of the development of the child's speech, we see that at an early age, the child really does use separate words, then a connection between two words develops, and later sentences with a subject and predicate. This is followed by a stage of development in which the child speaks in complicated sentences and finally makes a connection between the separate elements of a main and a subordinate clause. Analysis of the experiment with a picture mainly makes it possible therefore to separate the development of thinking and speech in the child and to show that the development of thinking and the development of speech do not coincide but proceed along different paths. We shall try to correct the misunderstandings that might arise in the interpretation of the facts obtained. The first misunderstanding may be as follows. We confirmed that a child of three describes a picture in the same way that he speaks, but perceives and thinks of the picture differently. Consequently, if we should want to represent symbolically the curve of development of speech and thinking, then the separate points of these curves would not comcide. But does this mean that development of speech and thinking are completely independent of each other? Does this mean that in his speech the child does not exhibit a certain degree of development of his thinking? This misunderstanding must be clarified. We must show that although the child's development of thinking and speech do not coincide, they depend closely on each other. The task of this chapter is to show that the development of speech in the child affects his thinking and restructures it. We will begin with the second, the simpler task. In order to analyze it, we must first establish that the child's thinking and a number of other functions begin to develop before speech develops. In the first years of life, development of thinking proceeds more or less independently, but in certain areas, it coincides with the

Development of Speech and Thinking

197

curve of development of speech; even in adults the function of thinking may remain independent to a certain degree and not connected with speech. We know about certain experiments, for example, the experiments of Kohler in animal psychology. These experiments established the pre-speech roots of thinking. There are studies by other authors in the area of child development, for example, the experiments of Tudor-Hart and G. Gettser with a six-month-old child. These authors observed the child's turning toward objects and could see the preliminary stage or rudiments of thinking with which the child operates in a concrete situation manipulating objects and using them as tools. Rudiments of thinking are more definitely evident in a child of 10 months. In addition to instinctive, innate reactions and conditioned reflexes, a child of nine to twelve months already exhibits habits that are developed at an early age. He has a quite complex apparatus of adaptation to new situations. For example, in using tools, the child picks up the basic relations between objects mainly in their simpler forms. All 42 ten-month-old children observed by Tudor-Hart and Gettser proceeded as follows: when a rattle with a string attached fell, they perceived the connection between the rattle and the string and after vain attempts to get the rattle with their hands, pulled the string and tried to get it that way. Also, observations showed that a child of this age is not only capable of picking up simple relations between objects, is not only capable of using an object as a tool, but in order to push another object toward himself, he creates a connection and complex relation between objects. The child tries to use one object as a tool for controlling another object much more frequently than the objective situation warrants. The youngster tries to move one ball with another ball not just when the ball is nearby and reachable with his hand, but also when the ball is several meters away and when there is no contact between the tool and the object. Germans call this Werkzeugdenken in the sense that thinking is revealed in the process of using simple tools. In a twelve-month-old child, thinking is revealed much more fully and precedes the formation of speech. Therefore, these are the pre-speech roots of the child's intellect in the true sense of the word. Recently we got hold of extremely valuable experiments with so-called conceptions. We know what a conception is in the old psychology: it is the traces of stimulation which originates in the environment that, from the subjective aspect, consists of all the objects acting on us and which, closing our eyes, we frequently, more or less actively, reproduce as an internal image. From the objective aspect, we still do not precisely know the mechanism of representation; evidently we are speaking of regenerating trace stimuli. Experiments with so-called eidetics made it possible to experiment with representations. Eidetic representation is the degree of memory development that in genetic relation occupies the midposition between perception on the one hand and representation, in the proper sense of the word, on the other. Since on the one hand, representations are memory in the sense that a person sees an image when the object is not before him, we are dealing with representation as with the material of thinking. On the other hand, since a person localizes images seen previously on the screen and these images are subject to the principal laws of perception, we can experiment with these images as with a perception; we can move the screen closer or farther away, illuminate it differently, introduce various stimuli and look at the results. During the last few years, E. Jaensch99 did experiments of this kind: he took 14 eidetics and conducted an experiment with them in the following situation. He showed each of the subjects a real fruit, then after a certain interval, he showed them a cane and a hook. When the objects were removed, the eidetics saw images

198

Chapter 11

on a screen: the fruit and the cane and the hook. When the subjects were asked to think about how good it would be to eat the fruit, 10 of the 14 responded in the same way: if the cane and the hook were isolated in the field of vision, then after the instruction, the cane and the hook moved toward each other in the field of vision and assumed a position that would actually make it possible to get the fruit with the cane. Any distraction of attention from the cane disrupted the connection being formed and the cane moved away from the hook. We know that in our perceptions, separate objects appear to be mobile and change very easily in size and location depending on the attention paid them. In observations of eidetics, mobility of images is extreme. Thus, Jaensch succeeded in showing that in both conceptions and trace stimuli, direct visual merging of separate objects occurs. These experiments were the basis for assuming that Jaensch obtained a model of how not only animals in Kohler's experiments, but also children who cannot speak, solve problems by thinking. This occurs in the following way. If no objects are close to each other in the field of eidetic vision, then in the conceptual field, in the field of trace stimuli, there is a special combining of the objects that corresponds to the task, the situation in which the child finds himself at that certain time. This form of thinking is called "natural" because it is innate, primary. This thinking is based on certain primary properties of the nervous apparatus. The natural form of thinking differs first in the concreteness of what is before the child, in the connection in more or less ready situations and, second, in the dynamics, that is, eidetics create combinations and transpositions of certain images and forms. In other words, they create in a sensory field the same changes that hands make in the motor field when a person takes a cane and moves it in the required direction. The connection that is made in the motor field is also made in the sensory field. I think that this experiment, whose physiological implications we do not yet know, does not contradict what we know about the working of the brain. We know that it does not have two centers that work independently of each other; on the contrary, as a rule, any two centers that are simultaneously excited in the brain have a tendency to form a connection with each other.
