He understood that what is unfolding is, in the words of Biihler, the greatest drama of development-a collision of the innate and the social-historical. Previously, when we spoke of the development of speech and thinking and tried to disclose the errors of the naive psychological theory that tried to draw a curve of the development of the child's thinking on the basis of a test with pictures, we saw what a great distance there is at that age between the curves of deVelopment of thinking and speech, what serious dialectical contradiction there is between the method the child uses for thinking and those with which he speaks. We have seen that this type of thinking, which is a matter of habit for the cultured adult, is created and developed only as a result of a long struggle and adaptation of natural forms of thinking to the basic higher forms; psychologists considered this type of thinking to be a finished product of development and believed it to be primordially given and natural, but genetic studies present it as a result of the complex process of development. As examples, we have such processes as the replacement of natural arithmetic by school arithmetic when new forms of adaptation to numbers displace the old, or transition from primitive to cultural arithmetic which occurs in the form of a major collision between them. We can observe step by step the same kind of transformation of innate or natural forms of behavior into cultural or social-historical in all the other chapters of our study. We can say that all chapters lead us to establishing the basic points that have deep significance for the problem of cultural rearing of the child. The first consists of a change in the concept of type of development: in place of the stereotypic, established development of natural forms that resembled the automatic replacement of forms characteristic for the child in the womb, a living process of formation and development occurs that goes forward in a continuous contradiction between primitive and cultural forms. This living process of adaptation, as we have said, can be considered analogous to the living process of evolution of organisms or to the history of humanity. In this sense, Buhler rightly calls the process of child development a drama, since its basis is a collision, a struggle, a contradiction of two basic instances. Through this, into the concept of rearing, the concept of the dialectical character of the cultural development of the child is introduced, the concept of the actual adaptation of the child growing into a social-historical environment that is completely new to him, a concept of historically higher forms and functions of the child's behavior. If earlier we were able to assume with a certain naivete that the child's thinking develops from the stage of the object to the stage of action and then to the stage of quality and relations just as a petal emerges from a bud, now the processes of the development of the child's thinking appear to us as an authentic drama of development, as a living process of development of social-historical forms of behavior. It is natural that together with the change in the basic theoretical point of view, the concept of cultural rearing changes radically. This change affects two basic points. Previously, psychologists studied the process of cultural development of the child and the process of his rearing in a one-sided way. Psychologists posed the

Cultivation of Higher Fonns of Behavior

223

problem of what kind of natural given conditions promote the child's development, on what kind of natural functions of the child should the teacher depend to introduce the child into one cultural sphere or another. For example, a study was made of how the development of speech in the child or teaching him arithmetic depends on his natural functions, how it is primed in the process of the child's natural growth, but the opposite was not studied: how acquisition of speech or arithmetic transfonns the natural functions of the child, how it profoundly reorganizes the whole course of natural thinking, how it interrupts and displaces old lines and trends of development. Now the teacher begins to understand that when the child enters into culture, he not only takes something from culture, assimilates something, takes in something from outside, but culture itself profoundly refines the natural state of behavior of the child and alters completely anew the whole course of his development. Differentiating the two plans of development in behavior-the natural and the cultural-is the point of departure for the new theory of rearing. The second point is even more important, more essential. It introduces the dialectical approach to child development into the problem of rearing for the first time. If previously, in differentiating the two plans of development, it was possible to naively present the cultural development of the child as a direct continuation and result of his natural development, now such a concept is impossible. Former researchers did not see the profound conflict in the transition, for example, from babbling to the first words or from perceiving numerical figures to the decimal system. They believed that the one is more or less a continuation of the other. New studies have shown, and this is their inestimable contribution, that where formerlya smooth path was taken for granted, there was actually a discontinuity; where there seemed to be a successful movement along a smooth surface, there were discontinuities instead. Speaking more simply, new studies noted turning points in development where the old assumed straightforward movement; in this way, the new studies illuminated the important nodal points of the child's development for rearing. Naturally, together with this, the old concept of the nature of rearing itself also disappears . Where the old theory could speak of cooperation, the new speaks of conflict. Actually, of course, for rearing, it is not an indifferent matter whether the child is brought from babbling to the word or from perceiving a number to the decimal system along a straight line or with breaks, discontinuities, and reversals that the child must make. The first theory taught the child to take slow, smooth steps; the new theory must teach him to skip. The radical change in the view of rearing that arises from a review of the basic problems of the cultural development of the child can be illustrated decisively for each methodological problem with every chapter of our research. Let us take a simple but, from our point of view, eloquent example that we borrow from Thorndike's psychology of arithmetic. Thorndike gives a picture of a first lesson in arithmetic. The child must master the number one and its shape. How would the old textbook present this? It would have a picture of one object, one mug, as Laj does, or one figure of a child, an animal, a bird, and alongside would be the number one and he would try to unite the natural concept of a single object with the cultural concept of one. This is how the methodology of teaching proceeded when teachers believed that the cultural development of the child could be nurtured simply by continuing directly from the natural perception of numbers. This was the basis for the serious mistake of Laj and his school. They thought that in order to understand one, it is necessary to see one since they assumed that the arithmetical concept of one arises on the basis of perceiving a single object.

224

Chapter 13

Thorndike proceeds differently. He goes over one with a picture of many objects, a whole landscape that in perception has nothing in common with one. Visual perception must be overcome, must be refined, broken up in order to make the transition to the arithmetical sign. The picture contains two girls, two dogs and a tree. Questions are posed that divide the visual perception separating the girl on the swings from the one standing on the ground. Thorndike brings the child to a real understanding of one. In this somewhat simplified example, the methodological change of which we spoke is clearly apparent. In contrast to Laj, Thorndike leads the child from perceiving numbers to a number series not along a direct path, but over a seeming barrier that forces the pupil to jump over the barrier. This contains and graphically expresses the basic problem of methods of teaching. What is the transition from perceiving one object, that is, the transition from natural arithmetic to Arabic numerals, that is, to the cultural sign? Is it a step or a leap? Laj believed it was a step, Thorndike showed that it is a leap. If we were to express the change in a general form, we could say that the new understanding involves a radical change in the concept of the relation between rearing and development. The old point of view had only one very important watchword: adapting rearing to development and that alone. It assumed that it was necessary to adapt rearing to development (in the sense of time, rate, forms of thinking and perception proper to the child, etc.). It did not pose the question dynamically. For example, from the correct rule that memory in the pupil is still concrete and his interests are emotional, it reached the correct conclusion that work in the lower classes must be emotionally saturated and presented in concrete, graphic form. It knew that teaching is strong only when it is based on natural laws of child development. This was the wisdom of that theory. The new point of view teaches that there must be opposition to be overcome. It takes the child in the dynamics of his development and growth and asks where must the teaching bring the child, but it answers the question differently. It says that in lessons with pupils, it would be folly not to consider the concrete and graphic nature of their memory, which must be relied on; but it would also be folly to cultivate this type of memory. That would be to keep the child at a lower step of development and to fail to see that the concrete type of memory is only a transitional step to a higher type, that concrete memory must be overcome in the process of teaching. In the chapters on written language, memory, arithmetic, and voluntary attention, everywhere we established the same principle which, if we think it through and work it out to the end, must bring us to a profound reorganization of all pedagogical and methodological study in the area of the cultural rearing of the child. We recall as an example the well-known and still unresolved methodological argument about arithmetic: how must a child be brought to acquiring the ability to count-through counting or through number notation? We remember why it was that the problem could not be solved. On the one hand, Laj's position that perception of written numbers is easier, more natural, more proper to the child and leads to a better and more rapid result in his training could be considered as being experimentally proven. On the other hand, it ostensibly adds nothing to the teaching of arithmetic in the true sense of the word. In our view, which we tried to present in the chapter on arithmetic, this paradoxical position is resolved simply and clearly. We cannot but admit that written numbers, with which Laj worked, actually corresponded to the development of arithmetical operations at preschool age and for this reason were easiest and most accessible for children entering school. For this reason, undoubtedly true is Laj's

Cultivation of Higher Forms of Behavior

225

conclusion that school training that takes into account the special quality of child development cannot help but consider this circumstance. For the child, the path to mastery of numbers lies through the perception of numerical images. And no matter how much Laj's opponents might maintain that a chasm lies between school arithmetic and the perception of a numerical image, that they are separated by essential traits and that the numerical image in itself even if developed to its maximum is not capable of bringing the child to the perception of the elementary, arithmetical unit itself, all of these claims, regardless their validity, cannot shake Laj's basic position: the child approaches arithmetic through perception of the numerical image and school training cannot bypass this path. The crux of the matter is that the break, the chasm, the principal difference between the perception of a numerical image and a number series lies in the development itself of the child. The child must cross over it in order to find himself on the other side; consequently, he must come directly to the chasm and somehow get beyond it. A more important mistake that Laj made was to affirm that the path through the numerical image could lead the child to understanding numbers and modern arithmetic. To do this, Laj had to develop a purely a priori understanding of the number and operations with it. His second mistake was that he ignored the main difference between the one arithmetic and the other and in constructing cultural arithmetic along the pattern of the primitive, he depicted it as being a priori. Here all the legitimacy of his opponents comes forward. They recognized this difference, they understood that as long as one goes further along the path of perceiving numerical images, no matter what, one will never get to cultural arithmetic but, on the contrary, will move away and aside from it. Their mistake was in a one-sided rejection of the partial truth that Laj's position contained. Finally, the eclectic point of view of Meumann and others who tried to unite both methods is actually the right one, but theoretically is completely insupportable because it provides no basis for the way in which the two methods must be united. The problem is solved purely eclectically, and the internal contradiction of both paths for growing into arithmetic not only is not surmounted, but appears with even greater force. Meumann tries to unite the two contradictory elements: without indicating a theoretical basis for uniting the two contradictory methodological devices, he indicates that it must conform to the dialectical contradiction in the development of the child himself. Thorndike notes correctly: Laj and his followers have a reached a wrong conclusion that numerical forms are the best method of teaching arithmetic since they help the children most to perceive numbers. This is supported by the fact that certain numerical images are easily evaluated quantitatively but this does not at all mean that they are the best means of teaching; they may be easier because they are more customary. Laj's conclusion would be correct only if his way resulted in better results 1Il teaching arithmetic m general under other equal conditions. With Laj's method, it is necessary to measure all results, not just time and ease. From our point of view, the problem that is actually resolved genetically can be formulated as follows. As we have seen in the development of arithmetical operations, when the child enters school, he undergoes a break, a transition from primitive to cultural arithmetic. There is no straight line in the child's development, there is a discontinuity, a replacement of one function by another, a displacement and conflict of two systems. How can the child be carried through what is a dangerous point for him? Genetic research indicates that for this, both teaching and genetic devices adapted to the nature and the features of the given stage of development are necessary. This means that in no case can we ignore all the features of primitive arithmetic of the preschool child. They are the point of support from

226

Chapter 13

which the leap forward must be made. But neither can we ignore the fact that the child must decisively give up this support and select the new point of support contained in the number series. For this reason, it is impossible to reject the method of numerical images in teaching arithmetic and to replace it immediately with the method of mastering the number series. We must depend on the numerical figure specifically for the child to master the number series. It is necessary to depend on the numerical image in order to overcome it. As we have seen, this is the methodological device in the training of the child in cultural behavior: to depend on the primitive function in order to overcome it and lead the child further. In a more general form the same situation arises every time we speak of the cultural rearing of the child. The element of contradiction, an internal overcoming, must always be included in every methodological device, and rearing can never ignore the fact that the transition from primitive to cultural forms of behavior is a change in type of the child's development itself. From this point of view, the principle of convergence in child development established by Stern can be seen in a new light. As part of this principle, Stern understands the basic position that the line of natural organic development of the child coincides, crosses over, meets with the line of the effect of external conditions that affect the child and only from the convergence or coincidence of these lines can the real process of his development as a whole and each of its stages be elucidated. The principle was formulated somewhat generally and does not essentially give us any means for explaining it, does not disclose further what the process of convergence or coincidence specifically consists of, what kind of meeting occurs between the two lines and what occurs at the meeting site itself. This principle is essentially unreliable and says nothing. It becomes unreliable if it is used, as Stern uses it, as an explanatory principle. Actually even the child's development in the womb, his nutrition, teaching him to read, and inculcating ideals-all of this appears as a result of convergence of internal and external conditions. But the problem of scientific research is to know specifically how to answer the question of what kind of meeting is occurring in each case, what the processes that develop here are, what the role of each of the factors is and what the result of their combined effect is. The task of scientific theory is to review the main types of relations that exist between the environment and the organism. However, in the area that interests us, Stern's position expresses the factually true idea that the development of the child in every cultural epoch more or less coincides at certain points with the line of his natural development. Thus, if we consider matters phenotypically, it actually seems that at a given stage of development of the brain and accumulation of experience, the child acquires human speech, at another stage, he masters the number system, and still later, under favorable conditions, he enters the world of algebra. Here apparently there actually exists a complete coincidence, a complete agreement of the lines of development. But this is a deceptive point of view. It conceals a serious discrepancy, a complex conflict in which these conditions never coincide since the line of natural development of the child is actually subjected to its own logic and never makes the transition to the line of cultural development. The conversion of innate material into the historical form is never a process of a simple organic transition, but is a process of complex change in the type of development itself. The basic conclusion that can be drawn from the history of the cultural development of the child with respect to his rearing is that rearing must exert itself every time whenever it had been following a smooth road before, that it must take a leap where before it seemed that it could limit itself to a step. The

Cultivation of Higher Forms of Behavior

227

primary merit of the new research is specifically that it disclosed a complex picture where formerly we saw a simple one. The new point of view introduces a real revolution into the principles of rearing when we approach the rearing of the abnormal child. Here the matter is something essentially different from rearing the normal child. The whole apparatus of culture, both external and with respect to forms of behavior, conforms to the normal psychophysiological organization of man. All of our culture is meant for a person who has certain organs, the hand, the eye, the ear, and certain functions of the brain. All of our tools, all our technology, all signs and symbols-all are meant for the normal person. From this arises the illusion of convergence of a natural transition from natural to cultural forms which cannot actually exist in the nature of things and which we have just attempted to disclose in its true scope. As soon as a child appears who is different from the normal human type, weighed down by a defect in psychophysiological organization, then even in the eyes of a casual observer convergence is replaced by serious divergence, that is, by deviation or a noncorrespondence in the lines of natural and cultural development. Left to himself and his own natural development, the deaf-mute child will never learn to speak and the blind will never learn to write. Here training helps by providing an artificial cultural technique, a special system of cultural signs or symbols adapted to the features of psychophysiological organization of the abnormal child. Thus, in the blind, visual writing is replaced by tactile; Braille makes it possible to produce a whole alphabet from various combinations of six dots and for the blind child to read by feeling the raised dots on the page and to write by using a stylus to pierce the paper to make convex dots. In just this way, for the deaf-mute, dactylology or the hand alphabet replaced the sounds of speech with optical signs and various positions of the hand and produced a special writing in the air that the deaf-mute child reads visually. rraining goes even further and teaches the deaf-mute child to speak since his speech apparatus is usually not defective. A child who is only deaf from birth is mute because he is deprived of aural perception. rraining teaches this child to understand speech by reading lips, that is, replacing speech sounds with visual images, the movement of the mouth and lips. The deaf-mute learns to speak using touch, a visually perceptible sign, or kinesthetic perception. Specially constructed indirect ways of cultural development of the blind and deaf-mute child and specially created written and oral language are very important in the history of the cultural development of such a child in two respects. The indirect ways are a kind of natural experiment of nature that shows that cultural development of behavior is not necessarily linked with one organic function or another. Speech is optionally linked with the sound apparatus; it can be translated into another system of signs just as writing can be diverted from the visual path to the tactile path. These cases permit us to observe most clearly the divergence of cultural and natural development that occurs in the normal child as well, but which, in this case, is manifested most markedly specifically because it is here that we note the most striking divergence between the cultural forms of behavior meant for normal psychophysiological organization of man and the psychophysiology of a child burdened by one defect or another. But most important is the fact that these cases indicate the only true method for rearing the abnormal child. Creating indirect ways of development where no direct ways are possible is the method. Written speech for the blind and writing in the air for the deaf-mute are indirect psychophysiological ways

228

Chapter 13

for cultural development in the most literal and materially precise sense of the word. We have become accustomed to the fact that man reads with his eyes and speaks with his mouth. Only a great cultural experiment which showed that it is possible to read with the fingers and speak with the hand discloses the whole conventionality and mobility of cultural forms of behavior. Psychologically these forms of rearing can do what is most important: specifically, they can accustom the deafmute and the blind child to speaking and writing. It is important that the blind child reads just as we do, but this cultural function is facilitated by a completely different psychophysiological apparatus than we use. In the same way, for the deaf-mute child, most important from the point of view of cultural development is the fact that universal human speech is facilitated for him through a completely different psychophysiological apparatus. Thus, the first thing these examples teach us is the independence of a cultural form of behavior from one psychophysiological apparatus or another. Another conclusion we must reach from these examples pertains to deaf-mute children and the independent development of cultural forms of behavior. As we have seen in the chapter on the development of speech, deaf-mute children left to themselves develop a complex mimetic language, a complex peculiar speech that they master with no training from those around them. A peculiar form of speech is created not for the deaf-mutes, but created by the deaf-mutes themselves. A unique language is created that differs more profoundly from all modern human languages than do these languages from one another. Thus, left to himself even with no training, the child begins his cultural development; in other words, the child's natural psychological development and his environment contain all the necessary factors for self-starting of cultural development, for the child's independent transition from natural to cultural development. Taken together, these points bring us to a radical reevaluation of the current view of rearing the abnormal child. The traditional view was based on the idea that the defect represents a minus, a gap in the child's development. A negative view of this developed, that is, a position that his development was characterized primarily from the aspect of the lack of some functions. For this reason, the whole psychology of the abnormal child was usually constructed by subtracting the missing functions from the psychology of the normal child. This conception is replaced by another conception that considers the whole dynamic of the development of the child with an organic defect; this new conception is based on the key position that the defect has a double effect on the child's development. On the one hand, the defect is an inadequacy and acts directly as such, causing disruption, gaps, and difficulties in the child's adaptation. On the other hand, specifically because the defect presents an impediment, causes difficulties in development, and disrupts normal equilibrium and adaptation, it serves as a stimulus for the development of indirect, alternate ways of adaptation, of replacement functions or additions to functions which serve to compensate for the defect and bring the whole system of disturbed equilibrium into a new order. In this way, the new point of view prescribes consideration not only of the negative characteristics of the child, not only his minuses and deficiencies, but also strives for a positive analysis of his personality which mainly presents a picture of complex indirect ways of development. From this, it is clear that organic overcoming of the defect and direct organic compensation is, to a high degree, a narrow and limited path. The development of higher mental functions of the child is possible only along paths of the functions' cultural development and it doesn't matter if the cultural development proceeds along a line of mastering external means of culture

Cultivation of Higher Forms of Behavior

229

such as speech, writing, and arithmetic or along the line of internal refinement of mental functions themselves, that is, developing voluntary attention, logical memory, abstract thinking, formation of concepts, free will, etc. Research shows that the abnormal child is usually retarded specifically in this respect. But this development is not directly related to the organic defect of the child. This is why the history of the cultural development of the child is a basis for the following thesis: cultural development is the main sphere in which compensation for defects is possible. Where further organic development is impossible, an immense path of cultural development opens. In the chapter on giftedness, * we shall consider especially how culture levels the differences in giftedness and erases, or, more properly, makes it historically possible to overcome organic underdevelopment. It remains for us only to add that with respect to cultural development of internal means of such types of behavior as voluntary attention and abstract thinking, the same kind of technique of particular indirect ways must be created as exists with respect to the development of external means of cultural behavior. For the development of higher mental functions of attention and thinking in the mentally retarded child, something like Braille for the blind or dactylology for the deaf-mute must be developed, that is, a system of indirect ways of cultural development where direct ways are cut off for the child as a result of his natural limitation.

'Editor's note: The manuscript does not contain material on giftedness.

Chapter 14 THE PROBLEM OF CULTURAL AGE At this point, we approach a problem which can be called the problem of cultural age. We know the conventional concept of intellectual age that is sometimes introduced in psychology in other countries together with chronological age. Neither intellectual nor cultural age are chronological concepts. When we speak of physiological age or of intellectual age, we assume that the process of development consists of certain stages that in a certain regular pattern of change follow one after another; the beginning of these stages never agrees completely precisely with the chronological passage of time. For this reason, we know that a child of a certain chronological age may be either ahead or behind that point with respect to both his physiological and his intellectual age. In this connection, we have come immediately to the problem that might be called the problem of cultural age. We will proceed from the hypothesis that was being developed in the preceding chapters: cultural development of the child represents a special type of development, in other words, the process of the child's growing into a culture cannot be equated, on the one hand, with the process of organic maturation and on the other, it cannot be reduced to a simple mechanical assimilation of certain external habits. If we take the point of view that cultural development, like all other development, is subject to its own patterns, its own stages, then the problem of cultural age of the child seems completely natural. This means that with respect to every child, we are justified in asking not only what his chronological age is, what his intellectual age is, but also at what stage of cultural development he is. It is easy to see that just as two adults, so two children of the same chronological and intellectual age may belong to different types of cultural age. Conversely, two persons with comparable cultural age may differ in intellectual and chronological age. One circumstance has for a very long time interfered and interferes even now with elucidating the problem of cultural age: cultural development, as they say, levels giftedness to a great extent. Experiments were recently done on animal giftedness. Unexpectedly, the experiments showed that intellectual giftedness in animals varies to a much greater extent than in man. Specifically, the experiment of Kohler, Jaensch, and Katz 106 with domestic chickens showed that intellectual giftedness in solving certain problems varies more than giftedness in solving the same problems varies in very young children. Kohler's experiments with chimpanzees showed that variations in giftedness among chimpanzees in the area of solving intellectual problems was greater than variation in solving similar problems in very young children. In other words, there is some tentative basis for assuming that there would be a difference, while not completely established, in giftedness with respect to higher mental functions; sometimes even basic functions in some animals exhibit greater variation than in the very young child. From this, psychologists reached the con231

232

Chapter 14

clusion that differences in organic functions must be more significant than differences in giftedness in cultured man. Other observations show that cultural development to a certain degree levels the difference in giftedness in the same sphere; however, talent results in much rarer forms of development than leveling. Let us imagine primitive arithmetic and cultured arithmetic. If we compare all of us with respect to arithmetic culture, then we would find that everyone who went through school, who has more or less mastered useful habits in the area of solving problems, is equipped with these habits in an equal measure and for this reason there will be no great difference in functions in the area of cultural arithmetic. But if we test each of us for development in primitive arithmetic, then we will find that both our actual potential and the dynamics of our development vary much more than common cultural forms of behavior that we have assimilated. This can be explained by the fact that each form of cultural behavior is in a certain sense already the product of historical development of humanity, an adequate form of adaptation in the given area of behavior. And to the extent that each of us grows into the specific forms, to that extent, there is naturally a leveling of giftedness as an index of the common cultural level that we attain. On the one hand, this is the case. On the other hand, cultural development in some cases affects giftedness, that is, it is a process critical for the state of giftedness of the child. We know from pedagogical observations of mentally retarded children that they are not ready to master writing although the path for mastering writing is within the range of school age that is systematically subject to teaching and cultural development. This is completely clear and understandable. Let us imagine the course of cultural development. As we know, it greatly extends natural potentials. Differences in giftedness that are insignificant in natural, practical behavior are converted into very different forms of adaptation by the powerful aid that cultural development gives to mental functions. I would say that cultural development can increase the scale of divergence in the differences of natural giftedness. Let us assume that we test musical giftedness in a number of people. We will find significant variation, but it will be within a relatively limited range. But let us assume that each of the people tested that ranked the same at the moment of testing had modern musical training, let us say, played complex musical compositions on some instrument. How greatly potential varies, how greatly the divergence in results increases in which giftedness is apparent! Thus, the relation between cultural development and giftedness is very complex and double-edged; on the one hand, cultural development has the tendency to level individual differences in natural giftedness, and on the other, it tends to increase the scale and range of various degrees of giftedness. Specifically because the relation between cultural development and development of general giftedness is so complex, scientific investigation is faced with the problem of isolating, as far as possible, the process of cultural development and considering it more precisely. Thus, as cultural age, we will take that stage of cultural development of the child that he has reached approximately and will correlate cultural age on the one hand with chronological age and, on the other, with intellectual age. The question arises of means for determining cultural age. Modern devices for measurement in the area of child development and current state of diagnostics of child development illuminate this problem only in rough approximation. We must get to know the first attempts to measure cultural age of the child in order to show that this measurement, like every new measuring device, does not at all change or contradict the basic forms of study of mental development of the child that are

The Problem of Cultural Age

233

available to science. But this approach makes it possible to look at the process of child development from yet another point of view, to see the side that is of great significance for the development of both the normal and the abnormal child. The basic measurement of cultural development is closely tied to the scientific approach to special giftedness. We know in general what constitutes the problem of special giftedness. The very concept of general giftedness is to a certain degree a misunderstanding that arises from an incorrect translation of a foreign word. 'fraditionally, we translate the word "giftedness" incorrectly, since this term in French, English, and Latin and in the international psychology lexicon means "intellectuality" and those using it usually have in mind intellectual giftedness. For this reason, when we ask the question about special giftedness in general, it does not mean the same thing in Russian: here, we must have in mind intellectual giftedness, since general giftedness distributed over all aspects of the personality equally does not exist. This we know very well. With respect to mental, intellectual giftedness, the simplest observations, particularly observations of outstanding geniuses, leave the impression that human giftedness as a system of a person's intellectual functions is something singular, fundamentally simple. We know, however, that even the least complicated apparatus of metabolism is not at all a simple, homogeneous mass of tissue that carries out its task with respect to the organism as a whole through completely homogeneous functions. The same can be said also of observations of apparent giftedness, of geniuses or talented people. Here we are always surprised by the following. For example, if L. N. Tolstoy, the genius novelist, might want to work on mathematics, might want to take up medicine or even chess, there would probably be an enormous discrepancy between his abilities as a novelist and his potential for mathematics or chess. If we were to take an outstanding chess player or master of classical dance, then we would probably get an increase in the coefficient with respect to chess or classical dance and a significant decrease in the coefficient in all other respects. Experimental observations of normal and mentally retarded children teach us the same truth at each step: the human organism, the human personality, and the human intellect is a single whole, but not a simple whole, that is, not a homogeneous whole, but a complex whole comprised of a number of functions or elements in a complex structure and in complex relation to each other. Regardless of the high correlation of various forms of giftedness, we know not only from facts from life, but also from facts established experimentally that different curves in the development of the child almost never coincide of themselves. We know, for example, that intellectual giftedness and motor giftedness do not coincide with each other, and we know that retardation is possible in each area so that the curves for these areas do not coincide completely and even if retardation coincides in ordinary forms, each curve will still have its own unique dynamics. This situation creates a most important and most substantial turning point in the technique of modern psychological measurement, and the turning point inevitably leads to a transition from the group of superficial measurements to measuring giftedness and to special research on specific features of separate functions. There are attempts, however, to unite the functions through a complex functional-structural connection. Recently, Thorndike, having carried out a great number of separate psychological and physiological studies, proposed a new project for reforming measurement of mental development of the child. He indicated the need for reviewing and evaluating the traditional methods of measurement if only according to the system of Binet, of which Thorndike justifiably says that in this sys-

234

Chapter 14

tem, we never know what we are measuring and how we are measuring or whether the measurements we have are reliable. Most of all, we do not know what we are measuring because we must add and subtract various operations of the child in a five-part form. One child did a test of three assignments, another made a map of city streets and houses of the town where he lives; the one and the other were scored according to 1/5 of their age. But it is clear that the product of these operations is not homogeneous and we cannot compare them and say that both indicate identical increase in intellect by 1/5. It is often necessary in such a case to add and combine different qualities, to add miles and pounds. As a result, we do not know what we are measuring and it is impossible to discern either the identity of the operation or the parity of the products obtained as a result of these operations. Most important, says Thorndike, is that we do not even know how we are measuring. Measurement is a basic arithmetical principle; measurement is counting with equal units so that between 1 and 2, 7 and 8, and 15 and 16 there is the same arithmetical unit; consequently, for measuring, we need a scale of equal units. What is the situation with Binet's measurement? Of course, there is no such single scale. If a child at 7 does not pass a certain test and passes it when he is 12, do we know that here too the advance is equal to a unit? Speaking generally, in a ranking of children, we can get the following. I studied five children and found their rank: A, B, C, D, E; but it may be that the difference between the first and the second child will not be the same, that for one, the coefficient will be 200 and for the other, 10 or 20 times lower. How can the meaning of the rank be understood if the differences are not identical? Finally, because of this, we are measuring things that are not the same with units that are not the same, and we reach the point where we do not know whether we are measuring reliably, whether we are actually getting a coefficient that corresponds to the state we are measuring. This happens because we are working with a scale that has no zero. In order to set up any scale, we must have a zero at the beginning, but here we do not know what to relate our unit to and from which point we began to count. Let us say that we began to count from zero and that Binet's scale is based on the year as a unit. This is one thing. Then the rank of the child who solves problems of a 12-year-old according to Binet is automatically raised and the increase will be evaluated as the ratio 3/2. Let us imagine that we did not begin from zero and that one year of development was equal to a score of 1000. * Then this would be an entirely different ratio, approximately 1012/1018, which yields an entirely different coefficient of the child's advancement. In order for us to know whether this ratio that we obtained with two measurements is reliable and whether we can compare these two measurements, we must take only the last two numbers, or all numbers as a whole, or the two last digits from a six-digit number. Thorndike says that to obtain a zero, we would have to grade intellectual giftedness from an earthworm to an American student, that is, we would have to take the whole range of development in order to present a ratio that we are measuring. Thorndike advances a solid position, and all methodology of measuring intellectual giftedness for children and all of his research point to the need for a transition to special measurements. First of all, we must know what we are measuring. For this, we need to know and measure the number of the same kind of units; to do this, we need to measure not intellectuality in general, but only a series of intellectual features of children and as far as possible experimentally analyze certain functions "Editor's note: arbitrary unit.

The Problem of Cultural Age

235

that would give us, under identical conditions of measurement, a product that could be compared. Thorndike proposes a system of tests for this that would measure intellect, specifically, a test of vocabulary, a test of arithmetic, etc. Moreover, he speaks of the inadmissibility of adding or subtracting the products of different tests. Thorndike concludes that we must carefully approach establishing methods that determine the unit of measurement for special functions, that is, a number of nonuniform processes. The second position. We must measure with equal units, therefore we must organize the apparatus for measurement adequately. For this, we must find in behavior qualitatively identical units or quantities; without these we cannot construct any measurement. On the basis of the indicated points the following necessity arises: we must not only get the facts, but also determine their proper interpretation. Binet's operation consists of the following. He takes a number of facts: the child solved this, he did not solve that, the protocol discloses the data, they are added, the arithmetical average is computed and a mechanical result is obtained. Actually, says Thorndike, it is naive to assume that between the intellectual functions at all ages of development there is this kind of arithmetical relation. If the matter were so simple, then we could take as an indicator a simple derivative from all theoretically taken five parts that we obtained in the preceding several years of development of the child. For this reason Thorndike believes that in similar cases science is obliged to divide strictly the two methodological operations-obtaining facts and interpreting them. How does a doctor proceed? He listens, measures temperature, takes an x-ray, and does not simply add up all these data; no, he interprets them on the basis of the whole system of medical knowledge and only then does he make a diagnosis. Here scientific processing consists of developing a solid basis for facts that can be interpreted by separating the two different things. In order to establish a proper position, we must establish a fact with maximal precision, and then each precise fact can be subjected to further interpretation. This is done in order to disclose the relation between separate facts since one and the same fact in a given grouping (the same temperature, the same cough or chill) do not mean the same thing as they would in a different combination: they have a different significance in a different combination. The psychologist must proceed in the same way. Instead of naively assuming that the relation between intellectual functions is simpler than between stages of tuberculosis, he must separate the facts from their interpretation and he must have a theoretically correct plan. Only then can he disclose the relation between these facts. Thorndike proposed a most interesting experiment that established precisely the plan for differentiating three plans or three studies of intellect: measuring by height of intellect, measuring by breadth of the intellectual area, and measuring by scope of intellect. We will not consider this in detail at this point. We shall return to the problem of measuring the cultural behavior of the child. First of all, it is necessary to indicate that in this area we have what is lacking in other areas of research-we have a zero established empirically although it is not ideal arithmetically. The zero had to be derived. Because of this, we can compare the different measurements. In some cases, the problem is solved without changing the cultural means and in others, by applying appropriate cultural devices. In this way we can compare two types of solving, and this comparison will give us a completely objective scientific criterion for measuring development. This convenient situation is due to the concept itself of cultural development, of what differs

236

Chapter 14

in cultural development from organic development. We can study a number of external transformations of organic development that are derived from adaptation of the growing child to the corresponding cultural environment. The same device of comparison or relation, as Thorndike correctly points out, allows us to approach closer to the scale of equal units and to make the transition to measuring relative indicators and then operating with correlations of coefficients. Having this kind of coefficient, we gain another advantage: we obtain actually equal, although abstract units. We will now confirm this. When we add data according to the system of Binet and identically consider as 1/5 the solving of a test from the three tests assigned for naming place, number, and year, we proceed in an unjustified way because we compare two qualitatively different things. But if we compare two identical relations consisting of identical units, then we get something uniform. Here we will not make a mistake. It is easier to count abstract units than concrete units and in this way, we overcome the difficulty. Finally, based on general knowledge of cultural development and its relation to organic development, we also obtain the reliable possibility of interpreting facts of which Thorndike speaks. We obtain a collection of data of a comparable character, with the same function in cultural and organic development. We have the possibility of disclosing the relation of symptoms, and this makes it possible to interpret fact scientifically and approach the measurement of cultural development just as a doctor approaches observation of symptoms. Using the examples, we will try to elucidate our situation. We will take the development of arithmetic in the child. How can we present and measure an operation with numbers in relation to its cultural development? There are many examples of measuring arithmetical development in the child, such as tests that pose the problem of taking into account the presence or absence of certain habits. For this, the subject is asked to solve special variants of a test in subtraction, for example, to subtract not only the lower number from the upper, but also the upper number from the lower. What can we expect in this experiment? Imagine that a given class solves the test in subtraction in a certain way and the pupils are distributed according to its solution also in a certain way. Imagine that we propose to do the same subtraction, but in reverse so that the subtrahend is written above and the minuend below. It would seem that if we introduced the same change for all children, then we might expect that the rank distribution of the pupils would remain the same as it was earlier; perhaps the amount of total time needed for solving the problem might change. But studies show that this is not the case. We introduced the same change for everyone, but the difference between the children in solving the new problem was very great. Thus, we are confronted by a fact that we must explain. Why do we get one distribution in the groups when we subtract in the normal order and a different dIstribution when the order is inverted? As always, in every concrete fact there is an interweaving of a great number of very complex reasons. In this case, a number of factors playa role: the moment of adapting to the new circumstances (different in different children), the moment when stability of complete mastery of the habit is attained, and the point in time that must be passed. But the basic point that has now been proven experimentally is that children that solve the test identically, that is, children that exhibit the same ability to use some habit, are genetically at different stages of development with respect to cultural arithmetic. One child has mastered a purely external mechanical device which he uses effectively. As soon as conditions for carrying out the operation change, he is no longer able to perform the operation and begins to make mistakes and for this

The Problem of Cultural Age

237

reason, the whole operation is disrupted. Sometimes the subtraction is not carried to completion, the principle of subtraction is violated, and the whole decimal system and the whole system of arithmetical operations is disrupted. All of this is broken down because of a simple change: the lower number is placed above and the upper number, below. Under the changed circumstances, the whole system seems improper. With another child, the operation is slowed, the number of errors changes, but the solution itself remains absolutely reliable. This means that the child assimilated the required structure of subtraction, that is, in cultural development, he not only acquired the external habit with which subtraction is usually done, but he actually developed an adequate method of behavior with respect to the given structure, and for this reason even in changed circumstances, his habit is more stable. Finally, between the two extreme cases where a child could not solve the problem at all and where a child was able to solve it but more slowly, there is the greater majority of the class and this is the pattern of the cultural development of this class. Let us imagine that 40 people who solve test problems in subtraction according to external signs are a homogeneous group, but as soon as we change the conditions, the same 40 people exhibit great differences. Here we have a device for research that can arbitrarily be called the method of shifts, or displacement of commonly accepted means, commonly accepted systems of measurement. In this way, we can test to what degree the child reacts mechanically to a given situation and to what degree he has assimilated the essence of the given operation, that is, to what degree he has assimilated the structure of the cultural operation regardless of the different changes. We will give one more device which Thorndike also adopted. Together with using a test in elementary arithmetical operations, he constructed a study using algebraic devices which allows taking into account the productivity of the pupil who has mastered one habit or another. In addition, says Thorndike, it is also possible to study more general psychological conditions that underlie the process of calculation. Thorndike's research is extremely simple. Let's say that we replace the ordinary arithmetic signs with letters. We will try to introduce a new arbitrary representation for writing division and multiplication signs, to introduce an unusual form instead of' the usual form, and we will observe how this affects the behavior of those solving the problem. Or even more simply. In order to learn how a child operates with symbols in general, specifically algebraic signs, Thorndike introduces a series of arbitrary signs. Preliminarily, instructions are given that one sign means one thing, another, something else, and a third, something else again and that one of four signs stands for a certain concrete thing. Then a system of signs is presented that the child must interpret. In this way, Thorndike tries to study to what extent in general a child mastered the structure of dealing with symbols and to what extent he can replace one symbol with another to solve an arithmetic problem. What does the research show? It would seem that there would be no difference if in an algebra problem the x were replaced by another sign. The principle does not change; a bar could replace the x. The meaning of the sign used remains unchanged. But such a change is not without consequences for any of the pupils. As a rule, the general character of solving the problem changes. Thorndike is correct in saying that the habit of using the unknown x has a substantial significance and changing the meaning slows the resolution of the corresponding operation; the new system of signs seems more difficult and, what is most essential, the rank order of pupils in the class with respect to solving the problem changes, that is, in other words, carrying out the task expressed in different signs yields a different distribution of the pupils in the class

238

Chapter 14

from the distribution that resulted when the problem was solved with the ordinary signs. As a result of the experiment, we get a picture different from the one we got before. Thorndike says that by this means of research, we disclose the discrepancy between arithmetical and algebraic knowledge and we obtain a general form of behavior which conceals the solution of every purely arithmetical problem in a general form. Consequently, the method of shifts makes it possible to gain access to genetic study of one habit or another, to disclose how stably a habit has been assimilated. This method opens the genetic approach to the system of schooling. Schooling and its effect on the pupil's development has until now been measured in two basic directions, which remain, of course, and which are most essential, but which do not solve the whole problem of schooling: on the one hand, we considered the system of school skills, habits, and knowledge, and on the other, giftedness of the pupil. However, a third important task remains-showing the reciprocal effect of the habits acquired by the pupil and the schooling on the form of cultural behavior and development of this behavior. Thus far, mainly the first two indicators have been studied in pedagogical practice. Moreover, we know that there is also a reciprocal, extremely strong influence of the acquired devices of behavior on the general development of the child. We know that without certain intellectual data, the child cannot master the required habits; a child-idiot cannot even master speech. But the other side is also significant for us: it is important for us to know not only to what degree thinking reorganizes the devices of behavior, but also to what extent the devices of behavior reorganize thinking and why specifically these two processes must be at the center of attention for the study of the cultural age of the child. Put more simply, the moment of mastering speech depends on the intellect of the children, and all studies lead to the conclusion that the study of speech is a very convenient indicator of mental potential. Consequently, one degree or another of giftedness is undoubtedly reflected in the nature of mastering speech. But, of course, the reverse is also true. If in the process of development, the child masters some language, it is interesting to see to what degree and how this reorganizes the whole natural process of his thinking. Let us recall the divergence of curves, that is, the mutual effect of natural and cultural development. Let us recall the example with the picture. If we try to draw the curve of development of the child's thinking taking into account its quality from stage to stage, we see that this curve does not coincide with the curve of development of thinking as a whole but coincides with the curve of the development of his speech. Two very important questions arise from this. First: to what degree is mastery of speech facilitated by intellectual potentials of the child? For example, why does a child who is at the stage of, let us say, a three- or four-year-old describe the picture with separate words although he thinks of it in whole images? Obviously, in order to master speech, one must know how to carry out intellectual operations of a more complex order than those that a three-year-old can carry out. Consequently, we can disclose the dependence of separate stages of development of speech on the degree of development of the child. Something else that is more important remains: the child thinks in whole images, but tells about the picture in isolated words, and in school the child experiences a conflict between the development of thinking and speech. Speech leads to the end of the formation of thinking, transfers it to a new track, converts direct, natural thinking into cultural thinking, verbal thinking, and in this way makes it more abstract.

The Problem of Cultural Age

239

The second question arises here: how does development of speech in the child change and transform the development of thinking? What kind of new forms of thinking arise in the child that depend on his using appropriate forms of speech? We tried to elucidate this question in preceding chapters. We tried to present certain points of view, certain theoretical data, a kind of genetic plan or methodological devices that have a common task: on the one hand to separate what in studies on child psychology was considered until now to be inseparable, that is, to separate the process of organic development from the process of cultural development, and on the other hand, to determine methodological ways to study the problems. On the basis of our results, we can take yet another step in research. Our work helped us see still another side of the process of child development, which until now was considered somewhat simplistically by many. If we will stop being satisfied with a somewhat one-sided approach to child development, then the task of the present work has been accomplished. In our research, we wanted to develop a more complex view of the process of child development, to present two lines of child development and as far as possible to enrich the methodological approaches to the study of the child. This was our basic purpose in the present work.

Chapter 15 CONCLUSION; FURTHER RESEARCH; DEVELOPMENT OF PERSONALITY AND WORLD VIEW IN THE CHILD Our studies were always conducted along an analytical path. We tried to isolate and trace the line of cultural development of separate mental functions, separate forms of behavior connected, on the one hand, with mastery of external means (speech, arithmetic, writing) and on the other, with internal changes in memory, attention, abstract thinking, formation of concepts. The chapter on will disclosed the general root from which all other forms of behavior spring. But synthesis must necessarily follow analysis. Since the course of exposition never coincides directly with the course of research, we first presented the general theoretical positions to which the study of separate functions led us, which we considered in three chapters. Their purpose was to explain the analysis, structures, and genesis of cultural behavior. In the synthesizing section, we attempted to gather general conclusions that the history of cultural development supported with respect to rearing and the methodology of school training. Then we tried to elucidate the problem of cultural age connected with the range of cultural development and with a method of measuring it. Finally, we come to the last task, the subject of this chapter, which consists of an attempt to present a plan or picture of the whole cultural development of the child, to review the cultural development of the child as a whole in its changes with age. We must note that at the present state of our knowledge and our research, the synthesizing chapter can be written only in draft form. In presenting the cultural development of each separate function, we used factual material and separate studies and observations. Now we come to projecting the further course of research. Only a detailed accumulation of primary factual material, followed by empirical and theoretical generalizations that show partial patterns and place them within ever broader laws, can lead to a complete resolution of our problem. As we have said, at present our studies do not give an adequate basis for this. However, even now we can try to draw a schematic of cultural development as a whole and illuminate in an age cross-section the more important points of the perspective that is opening up in connection with the facts presented earlier. We do this, first, because only facts taken in perspective can be finally understood and evaluated in all their significance. Second, because without a conclusive synthesis, even in a schematic form, many facts would be deprived of unity, would be disconnected and would lose much scientific significance. An attempt to cover cultural development synthetically must be based on the following basic positions. 241

242

Chapter 15

First: in content, the process of cultural development may be characterized as development of the personality and world view of the child. These concepts are insufficiently determined and precise scientific terms. They are being introduced into child studies almost for the first time. Perhaps in the course of further studies, they may be rejected entirely or replaced by other concepts, but even if they remain with the approximate meaning which we are now trying to ascribe to them, their content must be made more precise, determined and rigorously separated from similar concepts. At present, we are introducing them for preliminary orientation as general concepts that encompass two very important aspects of the cultural development of the child. Personality as understood here has a narrower sense than in the usual sense of the word. We will not include here all the traits of individuality that distinguish it from a number of other individualities, that make up its uniqueness or relate it to one specific type or another. We are inclined to put an equals sign between the child's personality and his cultural development. Thus, the personality is a social concept; it encompasses what is supernatural and historical in humanity. It is not innate, but arises as a result of cultural development because "personality" is a historical concept. It encompasses unity of behavior that is marked by the trait of mastery (see the chapter on will). In this sense, the correlate of personality will be the relation of primitive and higher reactions, and in this plan, the concept we introduced coincides with the concept that Kretschmer is establishing in the area of psychopathology. As world view, we are also not inclined to understand any logical, well-thoughtout views of the world and its more important parts, developed in a deliberate system. We are inclined to use this word also in a synthesizing sense corresponding to personality in a subjective plan. World view is that which characterizes a person's behavior as a whole, the cultural relation of the child to the external world. In this sense, an animal does not have a world view, and in the same sense, a child does not have a world view at the moment of birth. During the first years of life, sometimes right up to the time of sexual maturity, the child does not have a world view in the real sense of the word. Frequently this is more likely to be world action rather than world view. Thus, we imbue the term "world view" with a purely objective meaning of a method of relating to the world that the child has. We must make two more stipulations: first, the course of our research differs from a study of the course of natural development. Natural development goes forward not as development and change in separate parts that results in the formation of a total change of the whole. More likely, conversely, not one function of those considered above, whether it be speech or memory, develops at all independently and separately from others; in the process of close interaction, all aspects of mental life develop, advancing and supporting each other in every way. Personality develops as a whole, and only arbitrarily, only for purposes of scientific analysis can we abstract one aspect or another of its development. This is clear from what has been said above. The very character of cultural development, in contrast to natural development, brings about what neither memory nor attention can turn into processes of general cultural behavior if taken as such and left to themselves at any level of natural development at which they might be. Only when the personality masters one form of behavior or another does it bring them to a higher level. Thus, as in the area of organic development, the whole, in the expression of Aristotle, preceded its parts, the parts themselves and their action, that is, organs and functions, change depending on a change in the whole. In precisely the same way, the smallest step in the area of cultural development of any function assumes development of the personality if only in the most embryonic forms. The essence

Conclusion; Further Research

243

of cultural development, as we have seen, consists of man mastering processes of his own behavior, but an indispensable prerequisite for mastery is the formation of personality and for this reason, development of one function or another is always a derivative of development of the personality as a whole and is determined by it. From this point of view, we see that in our research on the development of personality and a world view, we accumulated significant material that must be collected and presented as a whole. If it is true, as we have said above, that the development of each separate function is a derivative of the development of personality as a whole, then it must follow that in tracing the development of each separate function, we will trace the development of personality. Personality was present as if invisibly and participated in the process of mastering one's own reactions; we touched on this above. Second and most important: we wrote not a single chapter that does not extend multiple threads into subsequent chapters. If we looked at the work as a whole, as they say from the height of a bird's flight, there would appear before us the very complex and confused threads that connect and interweave all the chapters together. Thus, speech, that principal means for developing personality, leads us to the basic form of mnemotechnical memory; it becomes clear only in the light of the pointing function of signs of attention. The word is a direct tool for the formation of concepts. Speech appears as the basic means of thinking and is connected with the development of the gesture, the picture, play, and writing. Attention also provides a real basis without which the development of concepts would be obscure, and we would never take up writing the history of personality and world view of the child if these intricately interwoven threads were not already noted in the whole preceding presentation. Third: with the current state of our knowledge we have a serious lack of important points for resolving the problem posed. Thus, we could say nothing about the very important connecting link between organic life and life of the personality, a link that lies in the cultural development of human emotions and desires. We could trace the development of social and cultural needs only conditionally and briefly in studying the development of motives. Let us move on to an ontogenetic consideration of cultural development as a whole. The newborn is probably the most primitive being that a person can be. Neither people at a low level of development, nor idiots who have almost no cultural development, nor deaf-mute children, nor an older child, let alone an adult, ever reach in their organic functions the stage of pure naturalness that characterizes the newborn. The newborn is a natural being in the full and precise sense of that word. For this reason, in the first period of the child's life it is easier than ever to observe natural forms of reactions. If certain primary forms of cultured behavior are apparent here, then they still have a semi-organic character and are coupled with an elementary reaction of the child to the human voice, to the appearance of an adult or other small children. In this period, most significant from the point of view of cultural development, the most important turning point, is the instant when the child uses his first tools. As systematic observations and experiments have shown, this aspect of natural thinking, independent of words, of which we spoke above, is observed in children at nine months. Specifically then the first complex mechanical and sensory-motor connections are formed. Preliminary intentions to do something with the aid of some object have been observed in a child as young as six months. At nine months, complex connections appear more definitely. When, after several attempts, the child cannot use his hand

244

Chapter 15

to get a toy that has fallen, he throws another toy after the first. We note that Kohler's monkeys and older children acted the same way when they failed in their attempts to get a ball lying beyond a net. But a child of ten months very often picks up a rattle that has fallen by its attached string, an operation that is impossible for an adult dog, as Kohler's experiments have shown. The child already comprehends certain mechanical connections. This period of instrumental thinking ("Werkzeugdenken") Buhler proposed to call the chimpanzee-like age. We observed how a child at ten months tugged at an adult by the hanging end of his belt or used another object to move an object that was not accessible. Researchers are absolutely correct when they say that with the use of a tool, a completely new period begins for the child. The principal change that occurs in the child is the following: at ten months, the child can use a string to get an object and remains at this level of thinking, independently of speech, till he is a year old. So we have before us a child whose behavior has developed along all three stages: instincts, conditioned reflexes, and simple forms of thinking. Establishing the fact that natural thinking is present in a one-year-old is a major achievement of modern experimental psychology. It shows that the genetic roots of thinking and speech do not coincide and that there is nothing more instructive than tracing month after month the development of reactions, thinking, and prattling in the child. But we will forget that we have before us the natural roots of thinking and speech. 'llacing the one and the other over the months, we see that in the child, there is no direct connection between the one and the other. The decisive significance of the transition to tools consists also of the fact that this moment is the turning point in the development of basic forms of adaptation of the child to the environment. Jennings indicates that the reactions of an organism represent not simply a disordered mass, but a system determined by the organization of the given being. An amoeba, he says, cannot swim like an infusorian, and the latter has no organs for flight. Man has a system of acts that limits the forms of his behavior; but in contrast to other beings, man extends the range of his actions with the help of tools. Man's brain plays a decisive role; among his organs, of decisive significance for implementing this are the hand, the eye, and the ear. The range of their activity is limitless owing to the use of tools. The natural inventory of this system of actions is possible for the human child beginning at the age of early use of tools. We will note that even here, it seems to us, researchers gave insufficient consideration to the natural character of the child's behavior. Following Hans Driesch,107 they tried to reduce behavior to elementary units, considering these to be not the simple reflexes that in pure, isolated form appear in the newborn infant as rarely as in the adult, but actions whose criteria, according to Driesch, are that the actions are determined by individual experience of a living being. For this reason, in determining actions, researchers introduced behavior that was actually or seemingly goal-directed. As these researchers say, an action is behavior directed toward definite success regardless if the striving is conscious or unconscious and regardless of what kind of success is intended. This point of view, like the whole teleological position, seems spurious to us in the sense that for expressing a well-known objective fact or relation, specifically a functional relation, the term "goal-directedness," pertinent to earlier stages, is used essentially subjectively and genetically. Also, the boundary for determining actual primary goal-directed actions when they first appear in the child is lost. Both thinking and speech of the child in this period are manifested in a natural form. Speech does not at all serve intended or consciously set goals,

Conclusion; Further Research

245

but appears as a simple activity of the child manifested in prattling, cries, and other expressive sounds. But the most decisive turn occurs in the sphere of activity of the child at the moment when he first begins to produce simple social reactions on the basis of speech. Social reactions absent in the first months of life begin with a cry inspired by the cry of another child, proceed to a glance at an adult, a calm smile after being spoken to, a cry when the adult leaves the child, and then a grasping of the adult and looking at another small child. In just six months, the child develops the reaction of prattling to call the attention of an adult and responds with prattling in response to the words of the adult, stretching out his arms to him, etc. Calling the adult's attention by movement, by tugging at his clothing in order to get his attention develops only at nine months. At ten months, the child can show the adult objects and at eleven months, he is capable of organized play and activity, getting the attention of another child by prattling, etc. Prattling very early begins to fulfill the elementary function of calling attention, but these means are still primitive and essentially do not differ from those we find in animals. Thus, ascribing a social function to speech (calling attention) and extending beyond the limits of natural organology through the use of tools are two very important instances that in the first year of life prepare for important changes that are the basis for further cultural development. If we should want to connect the common factors with what we know of the cultural development of separate functions, we would have to say that this whole period is a transitional period from the natural to the cultural life of the child. Every action of the child during this time has a mixed animal-human, natural-historical, primitive-cultural, or organic-personal character. We arbitrarily termed the transitional stage in the development of the child the magical stage. Actually, according to Piaget,108 who developed the most elegant and serious theory of the first year of life, the world view of the child at this level of development may best be described as magical. Piaget proceeds from this simple form of child behavior that is most characteristic for the first year of life, which Baldwin called a chain reaction. It lies at the base of motor experience of the infant and is the point of departure for all adaptation of a child of this age. The child's hands make random movements and if he gets any interesting result from this, the infant repeats the movement endlessly. This is how he begins to suck his thumb, grasp objects, pound on the table, etc. The chain reaction is exploitation of chance. Motor adaptation is continued in memory. As we know, memory in small children, beginning at the end of the first year, is different. Piaget observed that a child uses one and the same element of a chain reaction to affect the world that surrounds him.
