A researcher observed that at eight months, a child will rise, then drop with his whole body in order to move something that he sees at a distance. A somewhat older child screws up his eyes, looking at a lamp cord in order to light the lamp. The child does not distinguish which changes occur as a result of his own reactions and those that occur independently of him. In his words, Piaget's hypothesis states that all movements carried out by the infant in the environment and all movements subsequently coinciding with his efforts will be perceived as the same until the resistance of objects or people brings the child to separating several actual centers in the world; put more simply, to separating his "I" from other objects. We believe that Piaget's idea is quite correct that in the newborn there is not even the most primitive presence of "I"-the personality and world view-that is, relation to the external world and to others. The one and the other for him is

246

Chapter 15

therefore indivisible and he senses the chain reaction when he moves his arms in order to make a sound and when he squints at the lamp in order to light it. If we were to ascribe to the child an innate consciousness of his "I," says Piaget, then we could not at all understand why he begins to imitate others nor why he makes such strange movements in order to interact with the external world. We could say even more simply that the child experiences closure of an ordinary conditioned reflex to external actions that randomly coincide with some reaction of his. But, says Piaget, if some want to limit all of psychology to conditioned reflexes, then we must ask: what is the meaning of the circumstance that the child uses the same devices to affect things and himself? This seems to us to be the most clear indicator that formation of personality has actually not occurred in the child and that it is still completely merged with his world view as exhibited in his actions. Piaget correctly terms the undifferentiated stage of personality and world view as a paradoxical state of solipsism (not in the philosophical sense, but to indicate the fact that the child is, on the one hand, completely at the mercy of external things and on the other, that for him, external things are not at all distinct from the processes that are taking place in his own body). If we recall what we have said about separate functions, we will see everywhere this transitional or magical phase that is characterized by the lack of differentiation of the personal and the external, objective world. The different, natural memory of the child when he acquires important information on the qualities and states of the external world, the natural maturing of one dominant after another, the stage of forming concepts that, as Piaget correctly says, consists of distributing all objects acting on the child according to patterns of the five basic organs of perceptionthese are the characteristic traits of this stage. We shall consider two important examples that characterize it. The first is memory. Despite the exceptional strength of child memory, impressions of the first year of life are never retained and are not maintained for the duration of the subsequent life of the child. Why we do not remember anything about the first year or in general about the very early years of our life is a puzzling fact that has not yet been explained in psychology, but cannot, of course, be simply reduced to the fact that it happened a long time ago. In recent psychology we have two basic explanations for this fact. One was proposed by Freud, the other by Watson. Freud proposes that memories of early childhood are forced from consciousness because they refer to an organization of the child's behavior completely different from all the rest of his life. Watson identifies Freud's unconscious with non-speech behavior and explains this fact by saying that early impressions are accumulated without the participation of speech. Memory for Watson is the result of the verbal aspect of behavior. Watson's explanatIOn seems to us highly probable. From it, we can conclude that the first year of life in the development of the child is a kind of prehistorical epoch about which we remember nothing as we remember nothing of the prehistoric epoch of humanity that left no writing. Our speech is, in this way, a kind of unique writing of our past. In any case, the basic fact that we remember nothing about the first year of our life together with the other fact, specifically, the memory of the past as a basis for consciousness of our personality, allows us to say that the first year of life in a certain sense relates to later life just as development in utero relates to subsequent life. It is the second, seemingly prehistoric epoch in the cultural development of the child. The example in the area of development of concepts shows once again the extent to which the natural character of the epoch under consideration is connected with the absence of speech when, for the child, objects

Conclusion; Further Research

247

are divided into objects to recognize, to grasp, etc., that is, they are sorted according to different sensory patterns. In this way, in the plan proposed by Piaget, we see a kind of analogy to our concepts of natural behavior. We see how mistaken is the view according to which the child begins with recognition of single objects and only through generalizing from these comes to a concept. Actually, the child, like an animal, begins with the most general patterns; for him the five groups of things indicated exist and are not differentiated individually, but are perceived by him in accordance with the assimilation pattern. If the child could put his concept of things into words, he would need a total of five words and with these, he could convey the whole diversity of the world he knew. It is interesting to note that the affective theory of the origin of language also projects a total of several basic words for conveying separate meanings realized by humanity in prehistoric times. As Volkelt says, the pyramid of concepts is constructed simultaneously from two ends-from the specific and from the general. We could go even further and say that judging by the behavior of the child, before the age of one year, the pyramid of concepts is built specifically from non differentiation of the particular; the child goes from the general to the particular, gradually isolating ever smaller groups and the single object is evidently isolated later. This corresponds with what we know about the basic property of nervous activity, specifically about the radiation of nervous excitation that always results in the formation of generalized conditioned reflexes. Only later, as a result of differentiation, never happening instantly, does the child begin to isolate and distinguish objects. Thus, natural speech, natural memo!"'j, logical development of the dominant, and assimilation of a pattern instead of concepts-this is what characterizes the infant. The transition to tools, to socialized speech, marks the turning points that are the basis for further cultural development, but in themselves, they still belong to the natural period in the history of humanity. Speaking in the language of comparative psychology, they do not differ very much from the same forms of behavior in animals. The lack of differentiation of the personality and world view, merged into a single magical action, points to the transitional stage of cultural development. The correction that we must make in Piaget's theory consists of the fact that socialized speech and the use of tools are instances that drive a wedge that splits off this magical stage. Adaptation to the natural world and to the social world begins to branch here into two separate lines. Finally, the fact of absence of memory in our first years, which we have already noted, is evidence that at this time the personality of the child has not yet formed and the means have not been created that it would use to manifest itself in external and internal actions. The next epoch in the development of the child is characterized by two basic changes that have a decisive significance for subsequent development. The first change is organic and consists of the fact that the child learns how to walk. From this follow a radical change in his adaptation to space, an extension of his control of things, a freeing of his hands from the function of moving around, and an abundance of things that the child can now manipulate and control. Another change is cultural and consists in mastering speech. We have already indicated that this most important instance in the development of the child is accomplished as follows if we consider it schematically. At first, grasping movements dominate. Unsuccessful movements result in the stretched out hand remaining extended toward the desired object; the pointing gesture develops from this, the first precursor of human speech. Its function is to indicate, to call attention. This may be done directly by holding out the object itself or perhaps by indicating an object. The child extends

248

Chapter 15

his arms toward an object before the age of one year; during the second year, it is a pointing gesture. All speech of the child develops through gestures, and in this way what takes place here is a kind of roundabout and zigzag development of speech. Contrary to what Meumann expected, the first words serve not an affectiveexpressive function, but an indicating function. They replace or accompany the pointing gesture. Audible speech develops slowly and gradually and together with it, the basic apparatus develops for connecting with those nearby and affecting others. In the chapter on thinking, we showed that mastering speech leads to a reorganization of all the features of the child's thinking, memory, and other functions. Speech becomes the universal means for affecting the world. At this point, a new and unique form of the child's world view develops. Since the child acts on external things through adults, a shorter path between words and objects develops. Let us recall how in the experiments of Kohler and Buhler when a child could not get an object lying behind a net, he began to throw pillows, a belt and other objects at it. Now the child proceeds in the same way with words, and it is interesting to note that this feature is retained in the adult (swearing at inanimate things with which the adult cannot cope). It is most interesting that the child tries to affect things through words. Thus, in the experiments of M. Ya. Basov, the child turns to a toy, asking it to drop down. The child retains such a merging of affecting people and affecting things for a long time, but it differs greatly from the situation that obtained during the first year of life. Affecting people and affecting things proceed along different lines. The child has mastered the tool and the sign, at least the principle of their use, and applies each as appropriate. The decisive moment in the development of the child's personality in this period is his recognition of his "I." As we know, the child initially calls himself by his own name and acquires the personal pronoun with some difficulty. Baldwin correctly noted that the concept of "I" develops in the child from his concept of others. The concept "personality" is thus a social, reflected concept constructed on the basis of the fact that the child applies to himself the devices of adaptation that he applies with respect to others. This is why we can say that in us personality is social. For us, this conclusion is not at all unexpected: even in analyzing each separate function, we have seen that the child's mastery of one process of behavior or another is formed from the example of how an adult masters it. We have already seen, for example, that initially, adults direct the attention of the child to one thing or another and the child assimilates only those devices and means with which this was done. The same thing happens with speech. Initially, it fulfills the function of intercourse with others, then it becomes internal speech based on intercourse with himself. The personal name is frequently elicited in response to a question, as Delacroix correctly indicates, when children are asked: "Who wants this, who has this?" The personal pronoun, like the personal name of the child, is a pointing gesture to himself. J. Fichte 109 wanted to celebrate the baptism of his son on the day that he began to say "I." But, of course, the appearance of this pronoun says just as little about the appearance of the recognition of personality as the pointing gesture says about the objective meaning of a word. This can be seen very clearly in French in which there are two separate particles for expressing "I": "I" in the self-contained sense of the word replacing the noun (moi) and "I" as a pronoun used only with the verb (je). Delacroix proposes that in the child, "I" in the meaning of the noun precedes the "I" that appears only as a grammatical element.

Conclusion; Further Research

249

We know of a deaf-mute child who spoke freely from the age of seven, but only at the age of twelve started to used the personal pronoun. Before that, regardless of special schooling, he used his name instead of the word "I," for example: "Oleg has a father." In this connection, Stem's statement is interesting: in first-born children, the child's own name frequently precedes the personal pronoun, but in second and later children, the pronoun, "I" appears simultaneously with the name and not just as a grammatical element. It is difficult to find confirmation of the fact that the personality of the child at this stage is constructed according to social example and that the child makes the transition to recognizing the "I" just as others do in indicating themselves with the word. The next typical stage in the development of the child's world view we believe to be the age of playas a special form of child behavior, exceptionally interesting especially from this point of view. In play, the child, ascribing new meaning to toys and things, imagining himself to be a captain, a soldier, a horse, has, of course, gone beyond the magical stage when he did not differentiate between psychological and physical relations. Now, for him, a stick representing a horse is not a horse, he is under no illusion. As we tried to show above, this new (illusory) meaning often is not an arbitrary symbolic designation. The stick is no more a horse nor a sign of a horse, for example, than is a picture or a word. It is interesting to note that in the child's play, a picture or drawing rarely replace an unavailable object. Gettser noted such cases, but nevertheless, we are inclined to consider them extremely rare; they are the exception rather than the rule. The relation between objects and the meaning ascribed to them in a given case is very unique and corresponds to the stage of the child's world view that he has now attained. As has been said, for him, the stick is neither an illusion nor a symbol. We tried to show above that this meaning develops from gestures, that is, from that same common root from which the child's speech develops and from which the whole natural history of the development of the sign begins. The unsuccessful grasping that becomes extinct in the animal as goals are un attained, begins to fulfill a new function in man owing to his social environment and is, in essence, the true source of all of his cultural forms of behavior. It is primarily a plea for help, for attention, and consequently, is the first going beyond the boundaries of the personality, that is, a primitive cooperation in the psychological sense of the word. We tried to show that the stick acquires its meaning for the child owing to the gesture, movement, dramatization. Psychologists, guided by what the child himself said, were susceptible to illusion. They saw only the ready result or product of a certain process, but did not detect the process itself, which is that the initial meaning is inherent in the imitative gesture, the pointing of the child, riding the stick, etc. Something is required to serve as an object for full implementation of the gesture, and the meaning that this something acquires is essentially a secondary, derived meaning based on the primary meaning of the gesture. In this way, we see that the child at the stage of play very unstably localizes his personality and his world view. He can be somebody else just as easily as he can be himself, just as every thing can assume any appearance, but it is remarkable that with the general lability, the instability of the child's "I" and of the things surrounding him within each game, the child distinguishes not magically, but logically between treatment of things and treatment of people. It is remarkable that the child at this stage of development does not confuse activity in play with serious activity. He isolates the one and the other in separate spheres and he moves easily

250

Chapter 15

from the meaning of one sphere to the meaning of the other, never confusing them. This means that he already has control of both spheres. Only at school age does the child first exhibit a firmer, stable form of personality and world view. As Piaget has shown, the child of school age is also a much more socialized and a much more individualized being. What externally seems to us to be a contradiction is actually two aspects of one and the same process, and we believe that there can be no weightier evidence for the social origin of the child's personality than the fact that only with an increase, an intensifying and differentiating of social experience does the personality of the child grow, become formed and mature. A very important basis for this change is the formation of internal speech that now becomes the child's main tool for thinking. If at the stage of play, the child thinks and acts in a mixed way and thinking of some activity embodied in signs, moves directly to dramatization, that is, to a factual carrying out of the activity, then the thinking and action of the school-age child are more or less separated from each other. In play, we see a unique form of using signs: for the child, the process of play itself, that is, the use of signs itself, is still closely linked with entering into the meaning of these signs, into imaginary activity; in this case, the child uses the sign not as a means, but as a goal in itself. The situation changes decisively when the child reaches school age. Here his thinking and action are sharply divided. Piaget proposed that explanation of all the features of the school age can be derived from two laws. The first he calls the law of the shift or displacement. The essence of this is that the features in behavior of the child and his adaptation to the external world that were observed in preschool years in the sphere of action now shift, are displaced and transferred to the plane of thinking. Syncretic thinking and using it to explain visible phenomena, characteristic for the child's perception at the early stage of development, now appear in the form of verbal syncretism, examples of which Piaget presents in his experiments. This law can be formulated thus: the child of school age lives in a sphere of direct perception and action. The second law, which Piaget calls the law of recognizing difficulty, was established by Claparede. The gist of the law is that the child is conscious of his operation only to the extent of his unsuccessful adaptation, and for this reason if for a child of preschool age impulsive, direct, unconscious reactive activity is characteristic in general, then for the school-age child, an essentially different situation obtains. This child is already conscious of his actions, and he plans them through speech and can give an account of them; he has already isolated the higher form of intellectual imitation that we termed a concept, which can be connected to what the child isolates through internal speech as a kind of extract of things and their relations. But what is most important has not yet happened to the child, namely, consciousness of his own processes of thinking. The child does not take account of them, he does not react to them and often he does not control them. They flow with him just as actions flowed previously, that is, in a purely reactive way. Only gradually, only with the passage of years does the child learn to control the course of his thoughts and just as he earlier controlled the course of his actions, he begins to regulate and select them. Piaget correctly notes that regulating thought processes is a voluntary act, an act of selection, to the same extent as a moral action. Not without reason does Thorndike compare reflection with arithmetic based on selection of required associations, with selection in a conflict of motives, as this process occurs in real life. Only by the age of twelve, that is, by the end of ele-

Conclusion; Further Research

251

mentary school does the child completely overcome egocentric logic and move on to mastering his thought processes. The age of sexual maturity has usually been designated as the age at which two major changes in the life of the youngster are completed. It is said that this is the age of discovering one's "I," of forming the personality on the one hand, and, on the other, the age of forming a world view. No matter how complex the relations these two moments might have with respect to the basic transformation that is taking place in adolescence, that is, to the processes of sexual maturity, there is no doubt that in the area of cultural development, they are central moments, with greatest significance for everything that is characteristic of this age. For this reason, Spranger is fully justified in calling the transition age the age of growing into culture. When they say that the adolescent discovers his internal world with its possibilities, establishes his relative independence from external activity, then, from the point of view of what we know about the cultural development of the child, this may be designated as mastery of the internal world. Not without reason, the external correlate of this event is the development of a life plan as a certain system of adaptation that is first realized by the adolescent. Thus, this age crowns and completes the whole process of cultural development of the child. We have already indicated that we are forced here to limit ourselves to a cursory and schematic review of childhood ages, since research at its present stage cannot yet give a complete and differentiated picture of the age features of mental development.

THE QUESTION OF MULTILINGUAL CIDLDREN1 The question of being multilingual in childhood is one of the most complex and intricate in modern psychology <. . . >. We must mention the research of Epstein, the first both chronologically and logically, based on personal observations of polyglots, on data from questionnaires answered by a number of people who spoke several languages, and, finally, on some experiments on teaching various languages carried out by the author in Switzerland. Epstein bases his work on the principle that the mental base of language is the process of associative connection established between the sound complex and corresponding meaning, that is, the subject or idea named by the given sound complex. All further consideration of the question is based on this fundamental psychological premise. If the basis for language is nothing other than an associative connection between sign and meaning, then the problem of being multilingual from this point of view is extremely simple. Instead of one associative connection, we have here two or more completely identical associative connections between one meaning and its various sound designations in two or more language systems. Phenomena termed associative inhibition have been studied quite thoroughly in experimental psychology. In essence, they consist of several associative connections originating at one point that inhibit each other. If a certain idea is connected simultaneously with two sound designations, then both of these words have the tendency to appear in our consciousness immediately after the idea. A competition develops between the two associative tendencies as a result of which the stronger and more persistent associative connection prevails. But victory is the result of a conflict related to a slowing and disturbance of the associative process. For this reason Epstein proposes that two or more language systems may exist simultaneously as more or less autonomic systems without entering into direct connection with each other, but having an associative inhibition on each other. Epstein maintains that different languages may associate directly with an idea and function in all impressive and expressive forms regardless of the native language. However, between these systems, each of which is connected with the idea completely identically by an associative connection, antagonism develops that leads to conflict of various associative tendencies, to displacement of elements of one system by elements of the other, and to impeding and impoverishing not only the new, but also the native language. Thus, interference or a displacement and interaction of one system with the other develop together with associative inhibition. The negative effect of one lanIThis article was written in 1928. It was first published in 1935 in the book by L. S. Vygotsky: Mental Development of Children in the Process of Schooling, Moscow, Leningrad, 1935. The text of this article follows that of the first publication, with omitted material indicated by < ... >.

253

254

The Question of Multilingual Children

guage on the other is expressed in a feeling of difficulty, awkwardness, stylistic errors, replacement of words by words of the different language, etc. The harmful effect of one language on another is limited to this. The author states that polyglotism unavoidably impedes thinking. Due to competition of associative tendencies, an exceptionally complex interaction arises between them and a mutual negative effect of one speech system on the other develops. Because the different languages frequently lack completely identical words that correspond absolutely precisely to the words of the other language, and because there is always a certain difference not only in the languages, but also in the meaning due furthermore to the fact that each language has its own grammatical and syntactic system, polyglotism leads to serious difficulties in the child's thinking. Every nation, the author says, has its own special way of grouping things and their qualities, their actions, and their relations in order to name them. Terms that seem equivalent to us in different languages are so only partially. They have their shades of meaning and sense not directly translatable from one language to another. Difference in meanings is an extremely potent factor of interference in polyglotism. Not only a transfer of phonetic, or grammatical and stylistic features of one system are transferred to another, but also a certain erroneous identity of meanings. This difficulty is more significant than the difficulty arising from the differences in words. While including words from another language is found comparatively rarely, some displacement of ideas and meanings arises extremely frequently. Antagonism of ideas, says Epstein, is more intensive than antagonism of words. An even more significant factor of mutual inhibition of two language systems is the difference not only in ideas denoted by words of different languages, but also in the combinations of these ideas. In each individual, the native language establishes unique processes of linking ideas and their constructions that are expressed in syntactic forms. These forms acquire an exclusive associative rigidity, but are not the same in the various languages. Thus, interference of different principles of linking ideas arises and, together with mutual inhibition of words and meanings, a mutual inhibition of different methods of linking or connecting ideas also develops. On the basis of this theory, Epstein reaches the practical conclusion: least harmful is the passive use of several languages. In his opinion, all polyglotism is a social wrong and the whole task of the pedagogue is to reduce or ameliorate as far as possible the influence of this wrong on the development of the child. For this, the child must speak only one language since active mixing of two languages, according to Epstein's observations, is most harmful. That is why understanding and reading or passively using many languages is the practical conclusion that the author reaches from his studies. As he expresses it, there must be impressive polyglotism and expressive monoglotism. Further, according to these observations, the damage done by polyglotism depends not only on the active or passive form of using language, but also on the age of the child. Most injurious is the influence of polyglotism at a very early age when the child is formmg the first habits and forms of thinking, when associative connections between his thinking and speech are not yet firm and when, consequently, competition of other associative connections established in another language system is particularly harmful to the whole fate of speech and intellectual development < ... >. < ... > Many pedagogues-linguists, differing with Epstein, stated that the study of several languages differing from each other does not so much lead to inhibition of mental development as promote it and that the differences of two languages promote a better understanding of the native language. Evidence for this point of

The Question of Multilingual Children

255

view is usually the citation of the extremely interesting experiment of the French researcher-linguist, Ronget, who observed the development of his own child over a number of years. Ronget, the father of the child, was French and his mother, German. In the child's upbringing, an experiment was carried out strictly according to the principle: one person, one language. This meant that the father always spoke in French with his son and the mother, in German. Of all other people with whom the child came into contact, some spoke German and some spoke French, but they almost always observed the same principle: each person spoke with the child mainly in one language. The result of the experiment showed that the child acquired both languages in parallel and almost completely independently of each other. Parallel mastery of two language systems pertains to both the phonetic aspect of language and its grammatical and stylistic form. It is especially interesting that the sounds in different articulation systems were simultaneously acquired in the one and in the other. The history of speech development in the child could be observed as if it were separated and converted into two independent processes. All phases and stages that characterize the transition from the first sounds of prattling to formal, correct speech with all its peculiarities and different traits were observed in the same sequence with respect to both French and German, although at first, German, as the language of the mother, advanced somewhat more rapidly. But the most remarkable result of the experiment was the far-reaching independence of the one language system from the other that developed in the child relatively early. He completely mastered both languages, and very early, an exceptionally interesting speech phenomenon was manifested when the child had to express one and the same idea in different languages to his mother and his father. When the father, speaking in French, sent him with some message to his mother, the child expressed the sense of the message in pure German so that there was no noticeable influence of translation from French in which the child had just received the message. For example, the father sent the child from his room to another because the room was cold and said in French: "Don't stay here; it's a little cold here, go there." The child went to the other room and told his mother in German: "It was a little cold in father's room." The processes of using one language and the other go forward in the child without mixing or interference. Very rarely was there a transfer from one language to the other of word order and expressions and a literal translation of untranslatable words. Thus, alteration of the word order of an adjective following a noun that is characteristic for French was very rarely observed in the child. Of course, even here there is some mixing of elements of one language with the other, but especially important is the fact, established experimentally, that these mistakes and mixing are characteristic for children's language in general and are the exception rather than the rule. The child developed early a consciousness of having two languages. When both parents were present, he named separate objects in both languages and only later began to differentiate the languages, labeling them as speaking like mama and speaking like papa. To the question as to whether such parallel assimilation of two languages did not interfere with speech and intellectual development of the child, Ronget answers in the negative most categorically. Also remarkable is the fact that the child does double work in mastering both languages without any kind of delay in speech development and without any kind of noticeable additional work due to assimilating the second form of speech. As always, the experiment gives us clean results because of the artificial conditions in which our observations were made, and in this case, Ronget justifiably credits the success of the experiment to the strictly maintained principle: one person, one lan-

256

The Question of Multilingual Children

guage. Specifically this organization of speech activity of the child evidently protected him from interference and from mixing, from mutual damage to both languages. In another case of which Ronget told us, both the mother and father spoke to the child in different languages; this completely changed the whole course of speech development so that the child fully mastered both languages significantly later than normal children. Including speech in a definite and stable situation is, evidently, an essential trait that facilitates the study of the second language, as Stern correctly notes in analyzing this case. The question that Epstein raised is significantly broader than the answer that Ronget's experiment gives. In essence, he considers the problem from only one aspect: how does teaching a second language reflect in a favorable or unfavorable way on the development of the native language? But there is another no less important question that goes beyond language learning in the narrow sense of the word and pertains to the connection between the multilingual child and his thinking. As we have seen, in this respect Epstein reaches pessimistic conclusions. Polyglotism in his opinion is disastrous with respect to developing the child's thinking. This factor, inhibiting the mental development of the child, results in confusing concepts, in mistaken linking and combination of ideas, and in slowing and making the mental process as a whole more difficult. Authors who go into the problem more deeply from the theoretical aspect stop when they approach pathological disturbances in speech and the difficulties in speech and thinking that a polyglot experiences. Neuropathologists indicate very interesting phenomena that they observe in polyglots with aphasia. Sepp points to motor aphasia in polyglots as a notable example which leads to the conclusion on the dependence of localization of speech centers on the order of speech formation. A patient with damage to a certain segment of the cerebral cortex loses the ability to speak in his native language while he retains the ability to speak in a language that was used less and sometimes quite forgotten, which seems not only not to have faded away, but to be much more free and full than it was before the illness. He says that it is obvious that engrams of speech functions are localized in different places each time depending on the order of their formation. In these facts we see two points that are most interesting for us. First, indicating the different localization of different language systems and the possibility of retaining one language while losing the ability to speak another, that is, new evidence in favor of the relative independence of each of the different language systems and, second, indicating that one of the language systems that is little used and forgotten, as if displaced by the other, acquires the potential for free development when the first IS disrupted. Thus we come to the conclusion that confirms Epstein's position with respect to the autonomic nature of the systems and their direct connection with thinking and with respect to their mutual functional conflict. Many contemporary researchers point to a number of cases where a sharp transition from one language to another or learning several languages simultaneously resulted in a pathological disturbance of speech activity. < ... > However, these approaches to pathological disturbances of speech are only an extreme development from quite widespread studies that do not bring the matter to such an extreme, but nevertheless come to conclusions that are not heartening with respect to the effect of polyglotism on the mental development of the child < ... >.

The Question of Multilingual Children

257

Data presented thus far are a basis for a most important conclusion in regard to theory and practice. We see that the question of the effect of being bilingual on purity of development of the native language of the child and on his general intellectual development cannot be considered at present as being resolved. We see further that, in essence, it presents a very complex and controversial problem that will require special studies for its solution. Even now the development of this problem does not allow us to assume that it will have a simple and unequivocal resolution. On the contrary, all the data presented thus far indicate that its resolution will be extremely complex, depending on the age of the children, on the nature of the meeting of the one language with the other and, finally, what is most important, on the pedagogical effect on the development of the native and the foreign language. One thing that no one doubts is, specifically, that the two languages that the child masters do not collide with each other mechanically and are not subject to simple laws of mutual inhibition. < ... > The most serious deficiency of all the studies in this area done thus far, including Epstein's studies, is the methodological and theoretical insupportability of the premises in the light of which the authors pose and study this problem. For example, modern psychological studies in no way allow us to look at the relations between thinking and speech as the simple associative connection of two concepts for which the basic law is the law of mutual inhibition. Moreover, we must reject such an invalid premise since, together with it, Epstein's whole conception falls. The problem of thinking and speech leads the psychologist to conclusions that the great complexity of those connections and interdependences that are the basis of this higher and specifically human function are not measurable. Then the very complexity of this phenomenon must inevitably be taken into account. < ... > < ... > An important consideration that we believe is also required and which is also the result of the critical review of previous research is that the whole problem of being bilingual must be treated dynamically, not statically, from the aspect of the development of the child. We think that from the scientific point of view, posing the problem as it is posed in the work of Epstein and other authors is insupportable. We must not ask whether being bilingual is always and everywhere favorable or inhibiting under all circumstances regardless of the concrete conditions in which the child is developing and of the patterns of that development which change at each age level. For this reason, the transition to concrete study taking into account the whole aggregate of social factors of the child's intellectual development on the one hand, and the transition to genetic study that attempts to trace the fact with all its multifaceted qualities in the process of the child's development on the other-these are two rules that must obviously be mastered by our researchers. Finally, the prerequisite condition for setting up general studies is the need in studying this problem to descend from the surface, from taking into account external traits and indicators, and to penetrate deeply, to take into account internal structures of the processes that are directly involved in speech development of the child. In one respect, extending the breadth and depth of the problem in this way has already been done in the process of developing previous studies and we had the opportunity above to show how this problem developed beyond the narrow limits of the initial setting up of the question. The question of child polyglotism is now not only a question of the purity of the native language of the child depending on the influence of the second language. This question is only a part of a more complex and broader question that includes general teaching on speech development in the child in all the richness of the psychological content that is usually included in this concept. All the child's speech

258

The Question of Multilingual Children

development, not just the purity of his native language, all intellectual development of the child and, finally, the development of character and emotional development-all of this reflects the direct influence of speech. But if extending the problem in this direction is already recognized by former researchers, it is one of the areas of speech which still remains little explored and to which we would like to call attention in concluding this outline. It is the area of hidden speech influences. It seems a naive idea that speech participates only in the activity of such functions that involve an outwardly pronounced word. All so-called verbal tests contain either verbal formulation of the problem presented or a solution that requires verbal formulation. These tests are usually contrasted with so-called mute or nonverbal tests that do not contain spoken instruction, or use it minimally, and the solution of which consists in a certain compensation of actions without outward use of speech. This naive notion assumes that when we exclude the outward use of speech by purely external means, we can exclude all influence of language on intellectual operations of the child and obtain intellect in its pure form not obscured by words. Our studies have shown that this naive point of view cannot withstand experimental criticism. Actually, the solving of so-called mute tests requires as an indispensable internal condition the participation of speech in a double form. On the one hand, we have before us speech that is simply internal that replaces external speech. In solving the problem, the child is silent, but at the same time, he does not solve it without the help of speech. He only replaces the processes of external speech with processes of internal speech that, of course, differ qualitatively from external speech, but are still a more complex and higher degree in its development. Thus, the researcher, in introducing a mute test and thinking that by doing so he removes the participation of speech from the child's operation, actually without noticing it, introduces this speech in a cryptic form, in the form of internal speech, that is, in a form that is more difficult for the child. Thus, he is not facilitating, but making more difficult the speech part of the test, not eliminating the effect of speech, but setting higher requirements for the child's speech development, since it is more difficult for the child to solve the problem with internal speech than with external speech since internal speech is a higher level of speech development. Another form of cryptic effects of speech is even more interesting. The mute test that requires logical, considered, complex action of the child may not include unfailing participation of internal speech or may involve it in the least significant degree. Nevertheless, the test places such requirements for this action which may be carried out only on the basis of a high development of the child's practical intellect. Studies in turn show that the development of a child's practical intellect occurs with the help of speech, and so if speech does not participate in solving a problem presented by a mute test immediately and directly at the time the problem is being solved, it participated in the past because it provided the conditions necessary for the development itself of the practical intellect of the child. We must not forget the position that is basic to modern psychology of thinking that one researcher formulates in the following form. He says that knowing how to think like a human being without words is, in the final analysis, made possible only by speech. Thus, it is not easy to eliminate the factors of speech: when we push speech out the door, it comes in through the window, and researchers must not ignore the whole multifaceted and qualitative uniqueness of these various forms of the participation of speech in the intellectual operations of the child. But the matter is not limited only to thinking and the practical intellect of the child. We have already spoken of how closely speech is connected with the rightor left-handedness of the child. It is possible that such dependences may also exist with respect to emotions and even to character. Previous investigators indicated

The Question of Multilingual Children

259

the dependence on speech of certain changes in emotional and character development in the child. There is every factual and theoretical basis for stating that not only intellectual development of the child, but also the formation of his character, emotions, and personality as a whole depend directly on speech and, consequently, we must disclose in one form or degree or another the connection between being mono- or bilingual and the child's speech development. Thus the problem becomes broader and assumes the following form: being bilingual must be studied in all its breadth and in all its depth as it affects the whole mental development of the child's personality taken as a whole. Only such an approach to the problem of being bilingual is justified with the present state of the theory on this question.

EPILOGUE The third volume of The Collected Works of L. S. Vygotsky contains the basic theoretical studies on the general problems of the development of higher mental functions during childhood. It includes both the published first five chapters and unpublished material from his monograph, "The History of the Development of Higher Mental Functions." The part that was published earlier contains solutions to general problems in the development of mental functions, including a basis for the problem of development and methods of research and analysis of their general structure and genesis. In the chapters published for the first time, the general theoretical positions are specified using material on development of separate mental processes: attention, memory, thinking, development of speech and arithmetic operations, higher forms of voluntary behavior, and development of personality and world view of the child. Realizing the general theoretical positions, Vygotsky considers the development of concrete mental functions and forms of behavior as a dramatic process, converting innate, natural forms into cultural forms that develop when the child socializes with adults, mediating this process through the sign aspect of speech. 1

The central problem is formulating the subject of studying higher mental functions. Determining the subject of study requires changing the traditional view of the process of mental development of the child. The difficulty "consists not so much in underdevelopment and novelty of the questions entering into it as in the onesided and erroneous formulation of these questions, affected by all the factual material accumulated over the decades and by the inertia of erroneous interpretation ... " (p. 2). In this basic and pointed way L. S. Vygotsky poses the problem of development of higher mental functIOns of the child. What was the main error, the one-sidedness of his predecessors? It consists "primarily and mainly in an inability to look at these facts as facts of historical development, in the one-sided consideration of them as natural processes and formations, in merging and not distinguishing the natural and the cultural, the essential and the historical, the biological and the social in the mental development of the child; in short-in an incorrect basic understanding of the nature of the phenomena being studied" (p. 2). Thus, Vygotsky places the general problem of the mental development of the child in the context of the relation of biological and social, natural and historical, and innate and cultural, that is, in that context in which even now many problems still remain unresolved definitively. In such a setting up of the problem, Vygotsky sees not a special aspect of studying the problem of mental de261

262

Epilogue

velopment in children, but the general subject of research, the study of higher mental functions. Correspondingly, it might be said that real, innate, natural, and biological processes belong to lower mental functions and cultural, historical, and social processes belong to the higher. At the stage of research under consideration, for Vygotsky it was important to separate and even oppose the processes and phenomena described as differing in nature, as opposed to each other. This kind of formulation of the problem ensured its polemicism and distinctness. Sometimes it acted as a position directed against the innate, natural, biological, and real in human mental development. Children's speech and drawing, reading and writing, developing mathematical operations and logical thinking, formation of concepts and world view of the childthis is far from a complete list of mental functions that belong to the higher mental functions and which, before the studies of Vygotsky, were considered in the works of psychologists to be complicated and natural. Vygotsky sees the principal deficiency of the works of his precursors to be that in these works, the enumerated functions were considered mostly from their "innate aspect, from the aspect of the natural processes that formed them and were a part of them" (p. 2). Vygotsky notes as a deficiency the tendency to reduce more complex phenomena to simpler and more elementary phenomena. "Complex formations and processes were partitioned into component elements and no longer existed as wholes, as structures. They were reduced to processes of a more elementary order occupying a subordinate position and fulfilling a definite function with respect to the whole of which they were a part" (p. 2). As Vygotsky believes, with such an approach, there is inevitably a reduction of the complex to the simple, an unavoidable loss of specifically psychological, integral formations, of their unique patterns. We must keep in mind that specifically during the period of work over the manuscript, there was an intensive self-determination of psychology as a science, its separation from related sciences, a self-determination of materialistic psychology in a number of different schools of psychology. For Vygotsky, these situations took on a sharply different aspect in formulating and solving the general problems of the mental development of the child. Characterizing the traditional point of view of mental development, Vygotsky formulates three basic positions on which it was based: a study of higher mental functions from the aspect of natural processes comprising it, reducing higher and complex processes to elementary, and ignoring specific features and patterns of cultural development of behavior. The main problem and basic criticism of Vygotsky with respect to traditional associative and objective (most of all, behaviorist) psychology is that the automatic formulation of tl)ese schools made it essentially impossible to study higher mental processes as would be appropriate to their psychological nature. It is very important to understand that when Vygotsky compares higher and lower forms of behavior, he is looking for relations and transitions between them in the mental development of the child. " ... The very concept of development of higher mental functions is foreign to child psychology ... of necessity, it limits the concept of mental development of the child solely to the biological development of elementary functions that are directly dependent on maturation of the brain as a function of organic maturation of the child" (p. 6). In this situation, the idea of the need for "something other" than a biological understanding of the development of higher mental functions in man is clearly expressed. However, isolating theoretically the problems of studying higher mental functions, Vygotsky, in his polemical position, places in opposition the development of elementary and higher functions, of biological and cultural bases. Meanwhile, in a concrete analysis, he notes their mutual interchanges in all cases.

Epilogue

263

Beginning his own analysis, Vygotsky indicates the achievements in the direction of distinguishing and studying phenomena and processes that were called higher mental functions. He notes that in objective psychology (that is, in behaviorism and reflexology) there is only one differentiation of lower and higher forms of behavior: as innate and as acquired reactions. The latter are considered as habits. Empirical psychology presented every mental function as including "two stages." "Together with mechanical memory, logical memory was differentiated as its higher form, voluntary attention was added above involuntary attention, creative imagination was added above reproductive imagination, thinking in concepts rose as a second story above figurative thinking, lower feelings were symmetrically supplemented with higher feelings, impulsive volition, with foresight" (p. 6). This was the material for the point of departure that was reached in the preceding psychology which is the subject of Vygotsky's analysis. In order to present the theoretical position and the basis of his own formulation of the problem more precisely, Vygotsky brings together three fundamental concepts previously considered to be separate: the concept of higher mental function, the concept of cultural development of behavior, and the concept of mastering the processes of one's own behavior. Thus, Vygotsky not only poses a new problem, but at the same time proposes a way of solving it that includes analysis of cultural development of behavior as historical and based on mastering the processes of one's own behavior. We must note that in a number of studies on Vygotsky, only one of the ways of solving the problem of development of higher mental functions- their cultural, historical development-was analyzed and emphasized; mastering processes of one's own behavior, which is another way connected with the first, was studied to a lesser extent. Another point must be made. Vygotsky considers and analyzes not only the simpler, but also the higher mental functions as forms of behavior. This kind of use of concepts corresponded to the total scientific context of the epoch in which the scientific conceptions of cultural-historical development of higher mental functions were being developed. In formulating the problem of the development of higher mental functions as a new problem, Vygotsky was clearly aware of the complexity of its solution. He saw the primary difficulty specifically in the separation that developed and in trying to compare two types of sciences, the natural and the humanistic, which, respectively, study the lower and the higher mental functions. "The dualism of lower and higher levels, a metaphysical division of psychology into two levels, has its most extreme expression in the idea of dividing psychology into two separate and independent sciences: into physiological, natural-science, explanatory, or causal psychology6 on the one hand and interpretive, descriptive or teleological psychology of the mind 8 as a basis of all humanistic sciences on the other" (p. 7). Vygotsky was clearly aware of the noted dualism and the presence of the opposite tendencies in science and tried to find ways to overcome them, but could not do this on the basis of the data of contemporary science. Vygotsky considered the position in psychology that developed at that time in the form of contrasting the indicated trends as a psychological crisis which led idealistic philosophy and the psychology based on it to reach a number of erroneous conclusions. Dualism and the clearly expressed trends of the crisis in psychology even now divide modern psychology in other countries into two parts, in which psychology as a science of the mind opposes itself to psychology as a natural-science discipline. Subsequently we will trace how and along which basic lines Vygotsky seeks to solve the problems posed and to overcome those contradictions that we noted between the two directions in psychology as a science of higher mental functions.

264

Epilogue

Thus, on the basis of theoretical analysis, Vygotsky carefully formulates his own historical approach to the study of higher mental functions; his task is to show the origin and development of these functions. In the general concept "development of higher mental functions," Vygotsky includes two groups of phenomena. "These are, first, the processes of mastering external materials of cultural development and thinking: language, writing, arithmetic, drawing; second, the processes of development of special higher mental functions not delimited and not determined with any degree of precision and in traditional psychology termed voluntary attention, logical memory, formation of concepts, etc. Both of these taken together also form that which we conditionally call the process of development of higher forms of the child's behavior" (p. 14). The two indicated basic parts of higher mental functions, which, from Vygotsky's point of view, comprise the higher forms of behavior, have both common and special features of development. This necessary differentiation of two groups of phenomena is rarely made. Most often, the development of higher mental functions is considered as being accomplished according to a single common path that is most characteristic for the first group of phenomena, including writing, arithmetic, etc. The absence of this kind of differentiation may lead to errors in understanding Vygotsky's scientific views. In solving the general problem of the historical approach to research, he included two aspects of individual, genetic development of the child, biological and cultural development, and in history, presented as two "stages" of the development of man, the biological, including evolution, which ensured the appearance of man as a special biological type (Homo sapiens), and the historical, which continued the preceding stage on the basis of its own historical laws. Differentiating the two stages and types of development indicated as fundamental, Vygotsky also depends on the idea that biological development of man as a separate species is fully completed and all subsequent progress is accomplished according to different laws. (Contemporary biology leaves this as an open question and considers the process of biological change of man to be continuous.) Defining the essence of this position, Vygotsky notes: '~ wholly different type of adaptation in man, the development of his artificial organs, tools, and not a change in the organs and structure of the body, is of primary importance" (p. 16). Vygotsky sees the general idea of comparing biological (evolutionary) and historical (cultural) development as two historical stages as a way to differentiate and uniquely contrast them as two types of development in ontogenesis as well. At the same time, Vygotsky distinctly realizes that under conditions of ontogenetic development both lines are in complex interaction. All the uniqueness, all the difficulty of the problem of development of higher mental functions of the child lies in the fact that both of these lines are merged in ontogenesis and actually form a single, although complex process. Continuing the same line of discussion, Vygotsky cites the concrete and quite common example of the development of activity in the child in which such a major change in development as the use of tools occurs. Formerly, child psychology, including that of Buhler, noted this fact and the transition as a major modification of behavior. But for Vygotsky, the most important point in this transition was not the transition itself but the fact that it did not change the preceding form of activity, but merged with it. '~l the uniqueness of a transition from one system of activity (animal) to another (human) made by a child consists in the fact that the one system does not simply replace the other, but both systems develop simultaneously and together: a fact which is unlike any other in the history of the development of animals or in the history of the development of man" (p. 21).

Epilogue

265

"The process of cultural development of child behavior on the whole and development of each separate mental function is completely analogous to the example given in the sense that each mental function in its time goes beyond the limits of the organic system of activity proper to it and begins its cultural development with limits of a completely different system of activity, but both systems develop jointly and are merged to form an interlacing of two essentially different genetic processes" (pp. 21-22). Thus, for Vygotsky the main problem and subject for study in order to understand the "interlacing" of the two types of processes is to trace their concrete uniqueness at each step of development, to disclose the age and individual-typological picture of development at each of the stages and with respect to each higher mental function. The difficulty for Vygotsky is not to trace and understand the separate process of cultural development, but to understand its features in a complex interlacing of processes. Particularly significant is Vygotsky's determination of the relations between the biological and the cultural at different stages of ontogenesis and in concrete manifestations, for example, in various cases of deviation from normal development, that is, with one defect or another in "biological" development. To solve this problem, Vygotsky proceeds from the idea of the historical development of higher mental functions in which both processes are the result of a common historical development of humanity and the higher processes and functions are a unique continuation of preceding "natural" forms. 2

For Vygotsky, determining the subject of study was inseparably connected with searching for a new method of experimenting and determining its structure and separable units of analysis. The problem of research method occupied Vygotsky in solving every problem that confronted him and was resolved from various aspects: the historical, genetic, experimental, and didactic. We are now interested in the most adequate description of this method as it is presented by Vygotsky himself on the basis of the classical examples he presented which form unique models for studying the development of higher mental functions. The development of a new research method as found in the thinking of Vygotsky was accomplished by means of contrasting it with the common method of experimentation based on theoretical positions of classical behaviorism and expressed in the theoretical and experimental pattern of stimulus-response. The method that Vygotsky considers as universal was equally accepted in various divisions of psychological science and was actually used in experiments in all basic schools of experimental psychology. It was used in psychophysics and psychophysiology, in animal psychology and child psychology, and in various divisions of general psychology for the study of perception and attention, memory and thinking. Concrete specific variations of this method as an experimental model consisted only in a sequential increase in complexity of the stimulus series and analysis of a correspondingly increasing system of reactions that grew into various types of behavior. As Vygotsky noted, in this respect, psychology did not know the principal difference between the method of studying lower, elementary and higher, complex processes and functions. The first and most general feature of higher mental functions is that they represent another class of activity of the subject characteristic only of man. This class of activity differs, according to Vygotsky, by the fact that it assumes mediated in-

266

Epilogue

teraction between man and nature and includes real activity not elicited by a stimulus alone. The most convincing model of this type of activity that characterizes the appearance and realization of higher mental functions is the "situation of Buridan's ass." This situation, described by Vygotsky, is classical in many respects. First, it presents the possibility of comparing a simple "two-stimulus" situation that determines some act of behavior with another, strictly intellectual situation, which psychologists now describe as an indeterminate situation or as a problematic situation. Second, this situation presents the possibility of intellectual behavior that includes a transformation of it so that it becomes a solved situation that ensures adequate behavior. In this situation, Vygotsky is interested mainly in the system of means that make it possible to transform (solve) the situation that developed. According to Vygotsky, the means that man uses to transform (solve) the given situation are dice. The essence of the change that occurs in the structure of behavior consisting of a transition from a lower to a higher form is that man introduces into a complex stimulus situation a new system of neutral stimuli that play the role of stimulimeans. Thus, the main characteristic of the principal change that takes place, which is a model of research of higher mental functions and the principal way in which they develop, is that the lower mental functions begin to be mediated by a new system of stimuli-means that carry out a new function in the organization of behavior. We note that at this stage of development of psychology, Vygotsky himself, deviating from the model of stimulus-response as being inadequate, could not finally reject it. He was forced, like many of his contemporaries, to think and to conduct research within the framework of this same original model, changing it in the indicated link: the means of transforming the situation (stimulus situation) and the means of controlling reactions. This formula, traditional at the time, allowed Vygotsky, on the one hand, to pose substantial problems of its transformation and to introduce limitations into it and, on the other hand, acted as an obstacle to further development of his own theoretical conception. Vygotsky undoubtedly took an important step forward toward disclosing the specifics of higher, uniquely human forms of behavior that allowed him even on the indicated limited basis to present completely anew one of the aspects of human behavior. Thus, two important positions characterize the model of higher behavior under consideration: first, the creation and inclusion in behavior of artificial, special stimuli-means, and second, the use of these means for controlling one's own behavior. The step forward consisted in introducing stimulimeans for controlling behavior, but it was taken on paths using a common pattern of stimulus-response. In the same way, Vygotsky analyzes the second principal case, the use of auxiliary means ("knots for memory") for voluntary remembering. In contrast to involuntary memory, voluntary memory is characterized by the fact that the person uses special auxiliary stimuli-means in order to remember and reproduce, that is, to control the process of memory. "If in the first case, remembering was wholly determined by the principle of stimulus-response, then in the second case, the activity of the man hearing the speech and memorizing it by means of notches on wood is a unique activity consisting in creating artificial stimuli and in mastering his own processes by means of the notches; it is based on a completely different principle" (p. 51). Together with the general principle inherent in the first and second models, there is a difference between them: in the first case, the stimulus-means (in the form of a die) is introduced to control behavior and in the second, to control process (memory). This difference is important in subsequent analysis of transitions

Epilogue

267

from natural to higher mental functions and for understanding the main structure of higher mental functions. Artificially produced stimuli-means used by a person to control his behavior differ mainly from all other types of stimuli that directly elicit and determine behavior. They differ from other stimuli also in origin and in function. For a more precise definition, Vygotsky calls them signs. Thus, stimuli-means acquire not only a new verbal designation, but also a new psychological characteristic. '~ccording to our determination, every conditioned stimulus created artificially by man that is a means of mastering behavior-that of another or one's own-is a sign. Two points are therefore essential for the concept of a sign: its origin and its function" (p. 54). Together with including the sign as a means of denotation and a means of controlling behavior, the structure of higher mental functions begins to be subject to a new principle, signification, together with the principle of signaling (stimulation). "Man introduces artificial stimuli, signifies behavior, and with signs, acting externally, creates new connections in the brain. Together with assuming this, we shall tentatively introduce into our research a new regulatory principle of behavior, a new concept of determinacy of human reaction which consists of the fact that man creates connections in the brain from outside, controls the brain and through it, his own body" (p. 55). The appearance and development of this second principle of regulating behavior is based on two main prerequisites: first, on the signalizing mental regulation that appeared in the course of development, and second, on the system of sign regulation of behavior that appeared in the course of historical development. Vygotsky systematically stresses two positions that he finds significant: the social aspect and the sign aspect of the new principle of regulating higher mental functions. From Vygotsky's point of view, the new principle of behavior that developed not only is a means of regulation, but also acts as a principle of "social determination of behavior carried out with the aid of signs" (p. 56). Thus, the sound trend of Vygotsky toward disclosing specific human methods of regulating activity and behavior led him to a one-sided presentation of sign determination, and as a result of this, the general problem of factors of social determination in the development and structure of higher mental functions of man was expressed through their sign mediation. Naturally, in this case, other factors of social history formed in Marxist philosophy were still not recognized and realized by Vygotsky in his theory on the origin and development of specific human higher mental forms. We must, of course, remember that Vygotsky notes and stresses the role of society in determining mental development of man, but in this case, what is meant specifically is the indicated form of realization of community and the social determination of the origin and development of the mind of man. "Not nature, but society must, in the first place, be considered as a determining factor of human behavior. This is the whole idea of cultural development of the child" (p. 59). The most important significance of the models of the structure of higher mental functions considered above consists not Just in the potential for external control of one form or another of behavior. There is another aspect of this process that has a more important significance for understanding the conditions and form of mental development of higher mental processes. The essence of the change achieved at the level of another type of mental regulation consists of "taking" it outside the mental process, implementing it with the aid of other means (signs) in an external form. " . . . Man, in essence, constructs externally a process of remembering, an external object compels him to remember, that is, he reminds himself through an external object and, in this way, carries out a process of remembering as if externally, converting it to external activity" (p. 59). In a commentary on Vygotsky's

268

Epilogue

studies, it is right to stress the significance of transforming the external into the internal considered as a process of interiorization of the forms of activity that have developed. The position cited above shows a somewhat different aspect and a somewhat different sense in the relation between external and internal forms of mental processes. Making the process external indicates accomplishing the process with the aid of other means, auxiliary stimuli-means, signs that mediate the process of remembering and of subsequent reproduction. The external form of accomplishing the process is accomplishing the same process with the aid of auxiliary means. Thus, external and internal accomplishing of one process or another differ in the means of its accomplishing and not by the presence or absence of the process as a mental process. The process accomplished externally is the same mental process (memory, thinking, etc.) but is done with the help of additional, auxiliary means. 3

In formulating the principle of studying higher mental functions, Vygotsky simultaneously formulates the principles of scientific analysis of psychological facts. This sets the approach to the development of the problem of scientific psychological thinking. Three basic principles characterize and comprise the more important requirements of scientific psychological thinking: analysis of the p~'Ychological as a process; explanatory (causal-dynamic) and not descriptive analysis; genetic analysis that allows reestablishment of any form of behavior or function that develops. These principles together comprise a unified approach to studying and interpreting higher mental functions. At the same time, they act as principles of genetic and experimental study of higher mental processes. Each of the indicated principles is seemingly merged with the others and in this way ensures the possibility of genetic, process-related, causaldynamic analysis of higher mental functions. The need to formulate special principles for analysis of higher mental functions is stipulated by the fact that, with respect to them, either methods of analysis and study of simpler mental processes were applied without a basis, or scientific analysis was limited to describing these higher processes, which actually led to denying understanding the mental as process and rejecting study of the causal dependence of higher mental processes and functions. Each of the noted requirements of scientific analysis is essential. Moreover, we may note that for Vygotsky, all of these requirements were connected primarily with the genetic principle. For this reason, Vygotsky's own internal striving was to understand both the process and the causal-dynamic relations through genetic relations that provided understanding or experimental reproduction of the original (not the derivative) processes, through a genetic reestablishment of primary causaldynamic relations that determined the appearance and development of certain psychological structures and forms of behavior. " . . . The method we use may be called an experimental-genetic method in the sense that it artificially elicits and creates a genetic process of mental development. Now we may say that the basic problem of the dynamic analysis that we have in mind is also contained in this. If we replace analysis of things with analysis of process, then the basic problem for consideration naturally becomes the genetic restoration of all the instances of development of the given process. Here, the principal task of analysis is restoring the process to its initial stage or, in other words, converting a thing into a process" (p. 68). Vygotsky notes that the task of such analysis is to present every higher form of behavior as a process of its establishment in order to find not its separate elements, but to disclose all the fundamental instances of the establishment of the higher form of behavior as a process.

Epilogue

269

Describing the second principle of analysis, the need to disclose and study the causal-dynamic relations that ensure the possibility of explaining and not just describing some higher forms of behavior that develop, Vygotsky compares the descriptive approach with the phenotypic and the causal-dynamic (explanatory) with the genetic. "Under genetic consideration of the problem, we will understand disclosure of its genesis, its causal-dynamic bases. Under phenotypic consideration, we will understand analysis that is based on directly presented traits and external manifestations of the object" (p. 69). Meanwhile, the method of psychological analysis formulated does not reject and does not eliminate the need for analysis and explanation of the phenotypic features and patterns of the mental process that developed. What is new is that these features now must be understood as determined by the origin of these processes, that is, as dependent on their genesis. Vygotsky links the third principle, his genetic principle of scientific analysis, with the need to disclose the origin of historically developed forms of behavior and mental processes. This includes primarily such processes as are described in psychology as automatized. In this case, relations between the form of implementing processes of different levels most often seem to change places: voluntary processes precede involuntary. What has become automatic appears here as the result of preceding development. To illustrate, Vygotsky cites the example of converting voluntary attention to involuntary attention, considered by Titchener to be a third stage in the development of attention. At this stage of his research, Vygotsky does not pose the question of psychological patterns or mechanisms of transition from one form to another; the main thing for him is the principle itself that requires understanding the transition from the third phase to the first.
