We know that most often in analyzing similar positions of Vygotsky, only two phases (or two stages) of transition are considered: from the external, voluntary, to the internal, involuntary. From Vygotsky's point of view, this is a process of transition to the same type of mental process that is accomplished according to the laws of the primary forms. These positions of Vygotsky have not yet been adequately analyzed. How can the form of reacting that has developed be transformed into a process? For example, how can the automatic selection reaction or any other complex reaction be analyzed into a process? The question Vygotsky poses has important significance for experimental psychology and for a more adequate understanding of the way in which the scientist approaches the development of experimental study of the mental process. For these purposes, Vygotsky considers it possible to use two basic devices: the device of difficulty, which prevents the implementation of some activity or carrying out a reaction automatically, and the device of pointing out, which includes means for carrying out the reaction or implementing the action. It is remarkable-and Vygotsky notes this-that in the case of difficulty, the process begins and is carried out as a search or as recollecting and it may be implemented with the help of questions asked of the experimenter. This process of "seeking" to reestablish the initial link of the reaction or action has not yet been sufficiently studied. It is possible that this is due to the fact that Vygotsky to a large extent and in great detail considered the second link of this process, which included using auxiliary means, and the first link was just noted and left without adequate analysis.

4 Vygotsky may have focused his analysis of the traditional point of view of the problem of construction and development of higher mental functions on isolating

270

Epilogue

the mediating quality (the tool or sign aspects) of higher mental functions and on their social origin and development through interiorization. From the very beginning, Vygotsky considers it necessary to reestablish the basic definition of the lower (natural) and higher mental functions. "We will term the first structures primitive; this is a natural psychological whole that depends mainly on the biological features of the mind. The second, arising in the process of cultural development, we will term higher structures (p. 83) ... New structures that we contrast with lower or primitive structures differ mainly in that direct fusion of stimuli and reactions into a single complex seems to be disrupted .... Between the stimulus to which behavior is directed and the person's reaction, a new intermediate member intervenes and the whole operation assumes the character of a mediated act" (p. 84). Because of this mediation, there is a mastery of processes of one's own behavior, that is, a mastery of those processes that formerly were of a natural character. In the case under consideration, "man himself creates the connection and ways for his reacting; he reconstructs the natural structure; with the help of signs, he subordinates to his will processes of his own behavior" (p. 86). This determination and differentiation of lower and higher mental functions appears as most essential in an analysis of the processes of transition from the one into the other. Based on isolating the sign as a main element that ensures the possibility of reconstructing the lower function into the higher, Vygotsky formulates with complete clarity the position of development that he defines as the natural history of signs. Here, neither the approaches in which the process of mental development included the possibilities of subjective discoveries nor the approaches that proposed the formation of habits were considered as ensuring the possibility of understanding and analyzing cultural development. The problem of development of higher mental functions in this case currently appears in a different form: as a process of assimilating forms of behavior that developed and were cultivated in the history of society and a process that acts primarily as a "history of the development of signs." Based on Janet's conception of mental development, Vygotsky considers this process as the most general law of mental development of the child. "The essence of this law is that in the process of development, the child begins to apply the same forms of behavior to himself that others initially applied to him. The child himself assimilates the social forms of behavior and transfers them to himself" (p. 102). Later this law of development is formulated completely precisely: "If we take this law into account, it becomes absolutely clear why everything that is internal in higher mental functions was formerly external. If it is true that the sign is initially a means of socializing and only later becomes a means of behavior of the individual, then it is absolutely clear that cultural development is based on the use of signs and that including them in the whole system of behavior occurred initially in a social, external form" (p. 103). Formulating these positions on the social determination of the mind as being sign-mediated allowed Vygotsky to mark the line of studying the operational structure of higher mental functions, but limited the possibility of studying mental processes, states, etc. Thus, with respect to analysis of the mental development of the child, Vygotsky formulates the position that the process of this development is accomplished as a transition from the external (social) to the internal (mental); the means of socializing appear as means of behavior and the structure of higher mental functions corresponds to the structure of relations between people, "verbal thinking represents the transfer of speech inward" and "deliberation is a transfer of argument inward."

Epilogue

271

The common psychological "mechanism" for forming and developing higher mental functions is imitation, which ensures assimilating separate functions among people. Considering this aspect of the social determination and the structure of higher mental functions in detail, Vygotsky formulates the positions stated as a law of cultural development of higher mental functions: "Every function in the cultural development of the child appears on the stage twice, in two planes, first, the social, then the psychological, first between people as an intermental category, then within the child as an intramental category" (p. 106). '~l higher mental functions are the essence of internalized relations of a social order, a basis for the social structure of the individual. Their composition, genetic structure, method of action-in a word, their entire nature-is social; even in being transformed into mental processes, they remain quasisocial. Man as an individual maintains the functions of socializing" (p. 106). The development of higher mental functions is not only socially determined, but higher mental functions in their content are social and individual-this is an assimilated social quality. Concretely, with respect to individual mental development of the child, this means that psychology must study not individual development of the child in the group, but the transformation of group relations into individual, personal characteristics of the child. "The usual question is how does one child or another behave in a group. We ask how does the group create higher mental functions in one child or another" (p. 107). Stressing this sociogenetic aspect that is fundamental to the position he developed, Vygotsky contrasts sharply the relations between the social and the individual, the internal and the external. With this sharp contrast, which suits Vygotsky's style of scientific polemic thinking, these positions themselves begin to be controversial since they express to a large extent one of the main aspects, but not the real complex process of the development of higher mental functions. As we have noted, Vygotsky presents this process of development of higher mental functions as a process of mastering one's own behavior. In the case under consideration, this process appears not only as assimilating "external" forms of behavior, but also as mastering one's own processes and forms of behavior. " .... At the higher stage of development, man begins to control his own behavior, subjects his own reactions to his own control. Just as he controls the actions of outside forces of nature, he also controls his own processes of behavior on the basis of the natural laws of behavior" (p. 112). Thus, in studying the structure and development of higher mental functions, Vygotsky formulated two basic principles: (1) higher mental functions arise as a result of their interiorization; (2) they develop as mastery of natural, innate processes and forms of behavior in accordance with their own laws. Correlation and realization of both principles pose significant difficulties. The difficulty arising in the theory is simultaneously a contradiction, insufficiently realized by Vygotsky himself, that, when considered with one-sided emphasis on one aspect or another, subsequently led to sharp discussions of the positions of the author in solving the problem of development of higher mental functions. This contradiction was also sharply expressed in the solution to the problem of development of concrete mental processes and forms of activity and behavior. 5

The section on the development of separate mental processes and forms of behavior contains concrete material that continues analysis of the problem of the

272

Epilogue

development of higher mental functions. This material makes possible a more complete presentation and understanding of the realization of general positions of the study as well as the formation in training and cultivating the higher forms of behavior and mastering the basic mental processes that Vygotsky considered in greater detail. The material published for the first time can be arbitrarily combined in three basic sections: development of speech and arithmetical operations; development of cognitive processes, including attention, memory, and thinking; and development of higher forms of behavior, world view, and personality. Development of speech and arithmetical operations. Development of oral and written language is a special subject of study. In a more complete and precise form, it permits the expression of basic characteristics of the process of development of higher mental functions. Speech is a higher mental function that serves as a means of communication and acts as a major social link mediating all higher mental functions and forms of behavior in development. At the same time, speech in individual development passes through very typical stages: from a reflex cry to extended forms of oral and written speech and later, internal speech and direct understanding of text presented in the act of reading. Carefully tracing the development of speech, Vygotsky tried to show both special as well as general features that characterize the development of higher mental functions. The process of developing oral speech begins with an innate, natural reaction. It appears and develops according to the general type of development of the reflex form of behavior. However, even during the first year of life, vocal reaction begins to fulfill two basic functions: expressive, characterizing a change in emotional state of the organism, and the function of social contact. The second function leaves its mark on all subsequent development. One of the more important aspects of this development of speech is the relation of speech to thinking. At the first stage of development, speech fulfills only the expressive function and is reflexive; it develops "independently" of thinking. "Thus, there is no doubt that the first phase in the development of children's speech is not related at all to the development of children's thinking; it is not connected with the development of the intellectual processes in the child" (p. 123). Similarly, thinking in the child develops at an early age regardless of speech. When the child is one and a half to two years of age, there occurs a kind of intersection, a meeting of thinking and speech. After this, "speech becomes intellectual, connected with thinking, thinking becomes verbal and connected with speech" (pp. 123-124). Following Stern, Vygotsky notes three points as the main bases for such a singling out and characterization of the new stage in the development of speech and thinking: a jumpwise increase in vocabulary, the appearance of first questions, and the beginning of active extension of vocabulary. The child seems to discover for himself the relation between sign and meaning; he establishes that every object has its own name. Here, as Vygotsky believes, "the word standing for the thing becomes as if the property of the thing itself" (p. 126). In this way, Vygotsky, in considering the child incapable of making the indicated "discovery," contradicts Stern. In all subsequent cases, Vygotsky will object sharply to "discoveries" by a child, considering that discoveries are possible only at quite high stages of the development of thinking. He considers the extension of vocabulary at this stage as a sequential assimilation of it. The development of written language is even more representative of a process of assimilation accomplished under conditions of and with the help of special training. The greatest difficulty and main inadequacy in teaching written language is reducing this process to training in which the written language itself is considered only as a complex motor habit. Vygotsky believes that "the development of written

Epilogue

273

language belongs to the first, most obvious line of cultural development because it is connected with the mastery of an external system of means developed and created in the process of cultural development of humanity" (p. 133). Specifically for this reason, from the very beginning, Vygotsky considers mastery of written language as a process of mastering a social form of behavior developed during the history of man. With this goal, Vygotsky carefully traces the connections of written language with other similar means that preceded it. These include the gesture of the child. It is Vygotsky's opinion that two instances connect the gesture with written language: children's scribbling and the symbolic function of objects in children's play. "In this way, a child's symbolic play may, from this point of view, be understood as a very complex system of speech aided by gestures that supplement and indicate the meaning of individual toys" (p. 135). Vygotsky calls this use of gestures in play object speech. Further development includes sequential transitions and replacing some methods of designation with others. "The child gradually converts the nondifferentiated marks into indicating signs; the symbolic lines and scribbles are replaced by figures and pictures and the latter yield to signs" (p. 141). However, for transition to written language, the child must attain "symbolism of the second order which consists of using written signs for oral symbols of words" (p. 142). Written language comprises an important link in the general speech system. Mastering written language assumes the establishment of complex connections of written language with oral language and the establishment of a two-sided connection, writing-reading. Corresponding to the conception he developed, Vygotsky considers the process of formation of writing and reading genetically, including in the process the transitional link that comprises abbreviation of extended forms of reading and which ensures the possibility of "silent" reading. "Understanding written language is done through oral speech, but gradually this path is shortened, the intermediate link in the form of oral speech drops away, and written language becomes a direct symbol just as understandable as oral speech" (p. 142). Thus, establishing the higher forms of reading, including seeming direct understanding, is accomplished through abbreviation (dropping out) of intermediate links that were necessary during the process of assimilation. It must be noted that Vygotsky did not interpret this form as "revolution"; he analyzes it as a shortening of auxiliary links, their exclusion from a complex process as a result of which the process of understanding acquires the form of a simple direct act. As in the process of acquiring speech, in the development of arithmetical operations, the main question is studying the patterns of transition from "natural" to "cultural" arithmetic, from direct to mediated counting. In this case, Vygotsky also notes that there is "a transition from direct perception of number to mediated perception, to the child's beginning to compare numbers to certain signs and to operating with these signs" (p. 150). On the whole, in solving problems on psychological patterns of development of arithmetical operations in the child, Vygotsky sequentially and precisely shows the need for "converting" the child from "natural" to "cultural" arithmetic, the need for mastering number systems that do not coincide with natural conceptions of number and methods of counting. Analysis of the development of speech and arithmetical operations showed ways of realizing the general positions of the cultural-historical approach to understanding and studying these processes in the child. What these processes have in common is the need to make a transition from natural reactions to historically developed, cultural reactions that assume the use of sign forms of mediating speech and counting. The process itself of developing new cultural forms passes through specific stages that include not only mastering behavior through mediated methods, but also subsequently excluding these forms, which results in the process of reading

274

Epilogue

itself acquiring the form of a direct process from which the system of sign mediation is excluded. The development of cognitive processes. In contrast to processes of assimilating specially developed means of behavior (speech, counting), cognitive processes (attention, memory, thinking) do not have similar equivalents precisely represented in social life. For this reason, specifically in analysis of the psychological structure of mental processes themselves, Vygotsky presents a more precise formulation of his theoretical position and approach to understanding their development in childhood as mastery of these processes: "mastery" of attention, memory, thinking. The transition to higher levels of development of mental processes occurs in this case not only as assimilation of "cultural" forms of behavior, but also as sequential change in type of mental regulation in the mental processes themselves. As in the analysis of development of other higher mental functions, Vygotsky isolates their "natural" and "higher" form. The basis of "natural" forms of attention, according to Vygotsky, is the separate dominant foci of excitation that determine the primary directions of behavior. Corresponding patterns that determine the appearance, functioning, and development of attention are especially characteristic for the very early age, but also appear at all subsequent stages of human life. Another line is the line of cultural development of attention that includes mastering the process with the aid of stimuli-means that make it possible to transfer the internal process of attention to external behavior and to control the process in this way. These cases of transition to a higher level of attention are also described as an intellectual method of carrying out tasks that are placed before the child. In all cases of doing complex problems that require voluntary attention, the child is actually required to solve the problems as intellectual problems. It is completely natural that he solves them using intellectual, thinking methods and means. As in other mental processes, in the development of memory Vygotsky also isolates two basic forms, the real (natural) and the cultural. He writes: "In our studies, we tried to compare directly the two types of memory, the two methods of remembering, and to elucidate by comparative analysis the elementary composition of both operations, their structures and genesis" (p. 180). According to patterns, natural memory coincides with physiological processes, and cultural memory is assimilated in the process of developing a system of means of remembering. In studying memory, Vygotsky tries to present the transition from natural to cultural remembering under conditions of child development not just as the use of additional means (mnemotechnical) that provide the possibility of mediated remembering. Vygotsky interprets this as a change in the movement of nerve processes aided by the stimuli used that appear in the role of means of remembering. "Whoever observed the direct transition from the natural method of remembering to the mnemotechnical cannot help but have the impression that he has seen a seemingly experimentally elicited change from innate to cultural memory ... mnemotechnical remembering introduces a certain new, initially neutral stimulus-card that plays the role of a mnemotechnical sign and directs the course of nerve connections along a new path replacing one nerve connection with two new connections" (pp. 181). Thus, Vygotsky connects the process of formation of a new type of remembering with a special type of controlling nerve processes that are mediated by introduced signs as means of remembering. Cultural, voluntary remembering appears in relation to natural remembering in such a way that it is one of the methods of controlling natural processes, including physiological processes. Meanwhile, the process of development of voluntary memory presupposes its sequential intellectualization. "If in the first case, we dealt with the effect of the

Epilogue

275

mnema, remembering in the organic sense of the word, then in the second case, the child replaces direct remembering with such operations as comparison, isolating the common factor, imagination, etc." (p. 182). Vygotsky considers these intellectual operations as means of remembering that provide both sense transformations of the material and its new structuring and make possible voluntary remembering and reproducing. Thus, the process of developing memory in childhood on the basis of finding means for remembering proceeds along two main lines: along the line of using intellectual means and along the line of increasing volition. For Vygotsky, most important in the analysis of this process of development is the problem of sequential transitions from some types of memory to others. They comprise four basic stages in the development of memory characterizing the general genetic pattern of development of higher mental functions. In keeping with this pattern, "at the beginning of the development of memory there is a purely mechanical remembering corresponding in our diagram to the primitive stage in the development of any function" (p. 187) ... "This is followed by a stage of naive psychology in the application of memory .... Next follows the stage of external mnemotechnical remembering that alternates with the stage of turning inward or logical memory" (p. 187). Analyzing the general stages of the development of memory, Vygotsky considers them not as simple age-related changes, but as a process included in the general mental and intellectual development of man and occurring under conditions of training and education. It must also be noted that in studying these changes that comprise the stages of development of memory, Vygotsky was the first to propose a theoretical plan of analyzing and explaining various mnemotechnical means for remembering that were quite popular at that time. In these extremely various, empirically developed mnemotechnical devices and means, Vygotsky sees one of the links in the cultural development of memory and the means as well for controlling the memory of the adult. Considering the problem of Vygotsky's research on the development of speech and thinking, we must keep in mind the understanding that he contributed to characterizing the process of thinking, which is a special type of habit that ensures the creation of new combinations of images, concepts, relations, etc. Thinking may occur in two forms: in the form of practical action (or operating with concrete images) and in a verbal form. The relations between the one form and the other are the main problem for research. We might express it in other words. The carrying out of any difficult practical action sets up practical, natural thinking, and its "verbal implementation," that is, the verbal accomplishment of this action, sets up thinking in the true sense of the word, cultural thinking. Thus, even in the analysis of the processes of development of the child's thinking, the main thing for Vygotsky is explaining the relation between thinking with the aid of practical action and thinking done with the help of speech-oral or written, external or internal. Vygotsky compares the transition from different forms of practical action to verbal implementation of actions to using tools, that is, to the change in methods of transforming some situations. In the study itself of thinking, its operational components are most clearly expressed. Studying the relations between speech and thinking at various stages of development, Vygotsky formulates a general and fundamental position on the fact that all the various types of practical, actual solving of problems precede the verbal forms and the general position that with more complicated problems or in situations that are more or less difficult for the child, there is always a transition to a lower level of solving the problem, that is, from the verbal form of solving to the practical form or from solving the problem in written form to using oral expression. Thus, the transition to using simpler, that is, practical, methods of solving problems makes

276

Epilogue

it possible, on the one hand, to open significantly greater possibilities to the child, and on the other, to use this device as a special means for developing a verbal plan for carrying out various actions in different training situations. Vygotsky gives a precise presentation of the general plan for developing relations between speech and thinking, for example, in an analysis of the relation of speech and drawing. "In all areas of the child's activity we find the same sequence. The same thing happens also in drawing. The small child usually draws first, then speaks; at the next stage, the child talks about what he is drawing, at first about parts of the drawing; finally the last stage is formed: the child first says what he will draw, then he draws it" (p. 201). The thinking of the child changes in the same way when he tells a story from a picture with difficulty but can use and reproduce the whole system of action from the picture under conditions of dramatizing, that is, in real action. The development of voluntary behavior and world view of the personality. We have already noted the principal significance for the process of developing higher mental functions of the mechanism of mastering mental processes and elementary reactions as a prerequisite for the transformation of natural forms of behavior into cultural forms. The most typical manifestation of this psychological mechanism in the development of behavior is the establishment of voluntary behavior, the possibilities for accomplishing voluntary acts, making decisions, etc. This type of behavior, according to Vygotsky, is one of the most complex as well as the most general cases of development of higher forms of behavior, and it is an important link in the development of personality. The most typical situation that allows one to trace the features of the structure of the voluntary act and the process of its establishment is the situation of selection. Previously, we noted Vygotsky's use of this situation for analyzing the general principles of higher mental functions. The situation of opposing stimuli (the situation of Buridan's ass) pertained to this when the die acted as a means of reaching a decision. However, in all similar situations, it was necessary to make a selection between external stimuli. The child solved such problems in situations of voluntary attention with the aid of a pointing gesture, and in a situation of voluntary remembering, with the aid of mnemotechnical devices and signs as means. In the case of the voluntary act, the situation of selection pertains not to external stimuli, but to internal states that require making a decision. "This is free choice between two possibilities determined not externally, but from within by the child himself" (p. 207). In the experiments that were carried out, the main thing was how the use of an external stimulus-means transforms the internal behavior of the child. In all experimental situations, the main thing was not how the child "invents," creates one means or another for solving the problem, but how he uses the ready means proposed to him. "Sometimes we were able to observe how the subject uses the die or some other device of this type completely independently, but since our main task was not to study inventiveness of the child but the process of selection using a die ... We resorted to the device we used repeatedly: direct proposal that the child use the appropriate device" (p. 208). This point subsequently became one of the controversial points between representatives of Vygotsky's school and representatives of other scientific schools in Soviet psychology. The question is specifically what kind of role in mental development of the child does active "discovery" have in the means and methods of solving problems that he has assimilated and uses. It was noted that only in cases of active creative assimilation and use of a new means did the child move to a higher level of mental development, but a formal use of ready means did not ensure this development. However, at the stage of

Epilogue

277

research considered, these controversial problems were not yet posed. It seemed that any use of ready means ensures attaining higher levels in the mental developmen t of the child. What is the psychological mechanism of the process of selection accomplished with the help of external means? How can the psychological changes that occur in a situation of selecting between two opposite motives be explained? Vygotsky believed that the use of external means in this situation represents not only solving an intellectual problem, but primarily the creation of a new external motive in which a die or card or sign, etc., are the external means. "The child resorts to a die and introduces into the situation new stimuli that are completely neutral in comparison with the whole situation and ascribes to them the force of motives. He decides in advance that if the die turns black side up, he will choose one series and if it turns white side up, the other series. In this way, the choice is made in advance" (p. 209-210). That is, in this process, a connection is established between the "desired" object (cup, number, etc.) that acts as a primary, higher motive and the color of the side of the die. As a result, the side of the die acquires the function of motive taken outside and used to guide the subject's behavior in solving a problem of choice. Here the means used has two functions: it is a means of intellectual solving of a complex intellectual problem and it is a means of "objectifying" an internal motivation, taking it out into a system of external stimulation subject to changes and control. It is possible that these ideas of Vygotsky will serve in the future as a basis for a theory of motivation as an objectification of needs (see A. N. Leont'ev: Problems of Mental Development, Moscow, 1959, pp. 431-445). Meanwhile we must keep in mind that the position on external objectification of motivation has been discussed more than once, that for Vygotsky the external object that a person uses in the role of a motive is an additional stimulus, that is, that the motive-stimulus is added to the preceding basic stimulus and in this way ensures the possibility of mastering one's own behavior. " ... I, myself, through a signal and a connection with it, got up, that is, I controlled my behavior through an auxiliary stimulus or an auxiliary motive" (p. 211). In the general pattern of controlling one's own behavior, the motive is included in this way as a natural link between the stimulus and the reaction, a means of controlling behavior introduced from outside. But the natural, innate characteristic of this process, as in all other cases, corresponds to the patterns of formation and functioning of the conditioned reflex. "In brief, we might say that this is an artificially created reflex" (p. 213). The artificial is included in the natural, is used as a means of controlling the natural and in this way ensures achieving the level of higher mental function. We have considered the basic positions that characterize the patterns of establishing separate higher mental functions. The process of their establishment accomplished as a process of cultural development is not just "superimposed" on the natural processes but determines, as if secondarily, the further development of the natural processes themselves, that is, the development of attention, memory, and thinking, on the basis of their being established as higher mental functions, occurs in an essentially different way in comparison with their development as natural processes. Mastering devices of cultural behavior and especially speech not only depends on the natural base of these processes, but subsequently changes even the course of their development, and the processes themselves appear as a single system. Finally, as higher mental functions are established, and as higher forms of behavior are mastered, the whole system of processes that comprise cultural development results in the formation of the personality and world view of the child.

278

Epilogue

These two higher formations seemingly store all the preceding accumulation and ensure its availability for analysis of the structure and levels of development of world view and personality. Vygotsky limits the term "personality" precisely and does not include in it such characteristics of personality as individuality. "Personality as understood here has a narrower sense than in the usual sense of the word. We will not include here all the traits of individuality that distinguish it from a number of other individualities, that make up its uniqueness or relate it to one specific type or another. We are inclined to put an equals sign between the child's personality and his cultural development. Thus, the personality is a social concept; it encompasses what is supernatural and historical in humanity" (p. 242). Subsequently these positions came to be considered not in the narrow sense that the author ascribed to them, but more broadly. Correlating the narrow and the broader sense of the problem of the natural and the social in the development of personality still remains as one of the problems for research and a concrete problem of psychology as a science. For his time and the stage of development of psychology, Vygotsky proposed general and sometimes contradictory solutions of this problem. His merit lies in the fact that perhaps in the indicated contradiction, the unsolved problems appeared more exposed and more distinct. Not just in stressing the main significance of the cultural, social, and historical in the development of higher mental functions do we see Vygotsky's merit, but in disclosing the whole contradictory relation between the natural and the social that is especially clearly and dramatically apparent in the mental development of the child. The depth of the living ideas of Vygotsky, the boldness of the scientist, are contained not in a simplified system, but in accentuating this dramatism. Also included in the present volume is a shortened version of the paper, "The Question of Multilingual Children." In the paper on analysis of a comparatively personal question, a number of problems are touched upon that pertain to the connection between the cognitive processes and speech. In this sense, it continucs organically the basic ideas of The History of the Development of Higher Mental Functions. The paper is of current interest in the plan for solving the problem of being multilingual. A. M Matyushkin Editor

NOTES TO THE RUSSIAN EDITION 1. The monograph was written in 1931. The first five chapters were published in the book: Vygotsky, L. S., Razvitie Vysshikh Psikhicheskikh Funktsii [Development of Higher Mental Functions] (Moscow, 1960). Chapters 6-15 are being published for the first time. The notes to the 1960 edition indicate erroneously that these chapters had not been written. 2. Empirical psychology, see Vol. 1, p. 460. 3. Behaviorism, see Vol. 1, p. 460; Vol. 2, p. 489. 4. Reflexology, see Vol. 1, p. 459; Vol. 2, p. 487. 5. Darwin, Charles (1809-1882), see Vol. 1, p. 462. 6. Explanatory or causal psychology: a direction in psychology that attempted to find physiological reasons for mental phenomena. In the broad sense, it proposes to disclose determination, the reasons and nature of the mind. 7. As comprehending or descriptive psychology, the author has in mind the idealistic direction in German psychology presented in the works of W. Dilthey and E. Spranger and contrasting himself with natural-science "interpretive psychology." See also Vol. 1, p. 465. 8. Psychology of the mind is the idealistic direction in West European psychology that did not link mental and material physiological processes. 9. Dilthey, Wilhelm (1833-1911), see Vol. 1, p. 465. 10. Miinsterberg, Hugo (1863-1916), see Vol. 1, p. 463; Vol. 2, p. 490. 11. Husserl, Edmund (1859-1938), see Vol. 1, p. 463; Vol. 2, p. 484. 12. Natural-science, deterministic thinking. This includes such approaches to mental research in which attempts were made to disclose the natural-science basis for mental processes and phenomena. These include psychophysiological directions that disclose, for example, the link between sensory processes and anatomical structure and the physiology of organs of sensation and the nervous system. 13. Structural theory of mental development. This includes primarily Gestalt approaches to the analysis of mental development, including the book of K. Koffka, Osnovy Psikhicheskogo Razvitiya [Fundamentals of Mental Development] (Grundlagen der psychische Entwickiung, Berlin, 1925). 14. Vygotsky has in mind the idealistic conception of Faininger, widespread while he was writing his book, known by the title Psychologie der "als ob." 15. This includes all basic directions in general and child psychology, behaviorism, Gestalt psychology, and psychoanalysis. 16. Psychoanalytic theory (psychoanalysis), see Vol. 1, p. 462; Vol. 2, p. 482. 17. W. Dilthey is intended here. 18. Spranger, Eduard (1882-1963), German philosopher-idealist, psychologist, pedagogue. In the basic philosophical work Forms of Life (1914), he developed the idea of integral ("structural") psychology, which he contrasted with the 279

280

Notes to the Russian Edition

natural-science "psychology of elements." The author of works on theory and history of pedagogy Juvenile Psychology (1924; see the Russian translation in the collection Juvenile Pedology, 1931), etc. 19. Freud, Sigmund (1856-1939), see Vol. 1, p. 462. 20. Probably what is meant here is one of the first studies of primitive thinking of L. Levy-Bruhl. 21. Associative psychology, see Vol. 2, p. 481. 22. Probably this refers to L. Levy-Bruhl. 23. Buhler, Karl (1879-1963), see Vol. 1, p. 465; Vol. 2, p. 484. 24. Kretschmer, Ernst (1888-1964), see Vol. 2, p 486. 25. Wundt, Wilhelm (1832-1920), see Vol. 1, p. 461; Vol. 2, p. 484. 26. Braille, Louis (1809-1852), French teacher of the blind, inventor of the relieftactile type for the blind (1829). 27. Volkelt, Hans, German psychologist-idealist. 28. Wurzburg school, see Vol. 1, p. 460; Vol. 2, p. 481. 29. Kulpe, Oswald (1862-1915), founder and director of the first experimentallaboratory of the psychology of thinking based at Wurzburg University. The main direction of the work of the laboratory was to oppose the traditional associative concept of thinking. N. Ach, K. Buhler, O. Zelts, and others were part of this group. 30. Binet, Alfred (1857-1911), see Vol. 1, p. 462; Vol. 2, p. 490. 31. Bekhterev, Vladimir Mikhailovich (1857-1927), see Vol. 1, p. 459; Vol. 2, p. 488. 32. Watson, John Broadus (1878-1958), see Vol. 2, p. 483. 33. Ach, Narciss (1871-1946), see Vol. 1, p. 470; Vol. 2 p. 483. 34. Pavlov, Ivan Petrovich (1849-1936), see Vol. 1, p. 459; Vol. 2, p. 484. 35. Hegel, Georg Wilhelm (1770-1831), see Vol. 1, p. 464. 36. Mechnikov, Il'ya Il'ich (1845-1916), Russian biologist and pathologist, one of the founders of evolutionary embryology, originator of the comparative pathology of inflammation and the phagocytic theory of immunity. 37. Blonskii, Pavel Petrovich (1884-1941), see Vol. 1, p. 462; Vol. 2, p. 486. This position reflects the general idea expressed by P. P. Blonskii on the historical approach to the study of behavior; he wrote: "Psychology must become like natural science since as a science of behavior of living beings, it is, of course, only a part of natural science. Psychology studies the behavior of humanity as a function of time, that is, using appropriate terminology, it studies behavior of humanity genetically ... Thus, scientific psychology is first of all genetic psychology" (P. P. Blonskii, Collected Psychological Works, Moscow, 1964, pp. 43-44 [in Russian D. 38. Kohler, Wolfgang (1887-1967), see Vol. 2, p. 485. 39. Buridan, Jean (circa 1300-1358), French physicist, philosopher, and logician. Buridan is credited with the example of the so-called "Buridan ass": The ass, finding himself between two completely identical and equidistant sheaves of hay, starves to death since in the presence of two equal motivating stimuli, he cannot decide which sheaf of hay to eat first. This example illustrated Buridan's view of the interrelation of mind and will. When mind reaches the conclusion that there are possibilities of different value before him, then will becomes inactive. In literature, the opinion has been expressed that the example of "Buridan's ass" is erroneously ascribed to Buridan, since even Aristotle and Dante described this kind of situation. 40. James, William (1842-1910), see Vol. 1, p. 460; Vol. 2, p. 487. 41. Levy-Bruhl, Lucien (1857-1939), see Vol. 1, p. 464; Vol. 2, P 482.

Notes to the Russian Edition

281

42. Thurnwald, Richard (1869-1954), see Vol. 2, p. 486. 43. Arsen'ev, Vladimir Klavdievich (1872-1930), Soviet researcher of the Far East, ethnographer, and writer. During expeditions, he studied the life, customs, works, religious beliefs, and folklore of the local population. 44. Polizer, Georg (1903-1942), French philosopher and psychologist who treated the problems of psychology from the position of Marxism. 45. Lichtenberg, Georg Kristof (1742-1799), see Vol. 1, p. 471. 46. Bastian, Adolph (1826-1905), psychologist-naturalist, predecessor of William James in appreciation of the significant role of kinesthetic perception in regulating behavior. 47. Zigvart, Kristof (1830-1904), German philosopher and logician. He followed the psychological trend in logic. 48. Here and subsequently, Vygotsky has in mind one of the specific characteristic of reflexological thinking of man noted by John Dewey in his book, The Psychology and Pedagogics of Thinking; "By thinking, man also develops and creates artificial signs that warn him in advance of consequences and of ways of alleviating or avoiding them. As this trait (interpretation-A.M. [A. M. Matyushkin, ed.]) is the difference between a savage and an animal, in the same way, it is the difference between the cultured man and the savage ... The very essence of civilization consists in the fact that we purposely erect monuments and memorials in order not to forget . . . All forms of artificial adaptation are deliberately devised modifications of natural objects so that they would serve to indicate what is hidden, what is absent and remote, better than in their natural state" (John Dewey, The Psychology and Pedagogics of Thinking, Berlin, 1922, pp. 21-22). 49. Dewey, John (1859-1952), see Vol. 1, p. 463. 50. Aristotle (374-322 B.c.), philosopher and scholarly encyclopedist in ancient Greece, founder of the science of logic, one of the founders of theoretical psychology, first presented data of science and philosophy in the tract, "On the SouL" He was educated in the school of Plato (in Athens). Subsequently he opposed Plato's theory of "incorporeal forms." 51. Lewin, Kurt (1890-1947), see Vol. 2, p. 487. 52. Gestalt psychology, see Vol. 1, p. 460; Vol 2, p. 490. 53. Basov, Mikhail Yakovlevich (1892-1931), Soviet psychologist and pedagogue, a specialist in the area of general and pedagogical psychology. He worked in the Psychoneurological Institute in Petrograd under the direction of A. L. Lazursky on the study of motor-voluntary activity. His comprehensive work, General Fundamentals of Pedagogy (1928), contains a number of chapters on problems of pedagogical and child psychology and is a serious review of problems of the mental development of the child. 54. Koffka, Kurt (1886-1941), see Vol. 2, p. 484. 55. Stern, William (1871-1938), German psychologist and philosopher-idealist, author of classical works on child psychology (Psychology of Early Childhood, 1914, Russian translation, 1915). He paid special attention to giftedness in children. Stern's works encompassed a broad spectrum of problems of general, genetic, and applied psychology, including differential psychology. Stern, one of the first to turn to systematic study of individual differences, began to use tests, and introduced the coefficient of intelligence (IQ). 56. Titchener, Edward (1867-1927), see Vol. 1, p. 471. Citing the noted positions of Titchener as a law, Vygotsky has in mind the three stages in the development of attention identified by Titchener. Previously psychology identified only two forms of attention: involuntary and voluntary. Titchener stated the position

282

Notes to the Russian Edition

that while two forms of attention were noted, it passes through three phases in its development: at the first, it functions as involuntary, at the second, as voluntary, and at the third, it is carried out with mechanisms of involuntary attention. The positions noted were presented by Titchener in the following context: "Attention in its popular meaning has two forms. It can be passive and involuntary or it can be active and voluntary. These two forms of attention are actually characteristic for different stages of mental development; they differ from each other only in their complexity as earlier and later forms ... However, there is still another stage of development of attention, and it consists of nothing other than a return to the first stage ... " (E. Titchener, Psychology Text, Chapter I. Moscow, 1914, pp. 226-231). 57. Quetiet, Lambert Adolph Jacques (1796-1874), Belgian mathematician, one of the founders of modern statistics, applying it to analysis of social forms of behavior. 58. We have in mind here the following general context of analysis of complex reactions in which Titchener, as in the analysis of voluntary attention, emphasizes that complex reactions may acquire the form of simple reactions. "It must be firmly remembered that the terms given these complex reactions-reaction of differentiation, reaction of recognition, reaction of selection-are only conditional. Differentiation and selection refer to external conditions of the experiment and only to them; in the differentiation reaction, we do not differentiate, in the selection reaction, we may produce various operations, but we do not select; recognition, direct understanding is included in all reactions, both simple and complex; even in a muscular reaction, we recognize 'something other' ... " We must now add that carrying out instructions, if they are properly understood, depends on habit; the time of careful preparation of the selection reaction, if coordination is the stimulus and movement is adequately mastered, may be equal to the time of a simple sensory reaction. From these data it follows that complex reactions are not made up in parts by simple reactions; the reactions of differentiation and recognition are not sensory reactions to which the time of differentiation and time of recognition have been added; selection reactions are not reactions of differentiation to which selection time has been added (E. Titchener, Psychology Text, Chapter II. Moscow, 1914 p. 129. 59. Merkel, F. (?), German histologist. He first described (1875) the epithelial receptor cells in the deep layers of the epidermis in the skin of mammals and man. 60. H0ffding, Harold (1843-1931), Danish philosopher-idealist, historian of philosophy. Under the influence of the historical-philosophical method of Hegel, he attempted to consider philosophy in its connection with science and to compare at a theoretical level the knowledge of philosophers of different epochs considering their teachings as stages of development of integrated thought. In psychological studies, H0ffding attempted to unite introspectionist representations with ideas and methods of Darwinism in biology: he treated consciousness as a higher form of biological development. H0ffding's criticism played a positive role in history in regarding consciousness as an aggregate of independent elements-sensations and concepts. With this he contrasted "the law of relations": properties of each separate mental element are determined by the aggregate connections and relations in which they are included. 61. Goethe, Johann Wolfgang (1749-1832), see Vol. 1, p. 464. 62. Thorndike, Edward (1874-1949), see Vol. 2, p. 485. 63. Hall, Stanley (1844-1824). See Vol. 1, p. 470.

Notes to the Russian Edition

283

64. Pre-formism is the study of the presence of sex cells of the organism's maternal structures that predetermine the development of the embryo and traits that are formed in its organism. 65. Claparecte, Edward (1873-1940). See Vol. 2, p. 482. 66. Piaget, Jean (1896-1980). See Vol. 2, p. 482. We have in mind a book by Piaget, Speech and Thinking of the Child, edited with an introduction by L. S. Vygotsky (Moscow-Leningrad, 1932). 67. Jean-Batiste Lamarck (1744-1829). French biologist. 68. We have in mind a book by K. Koffka, Fundamentals of Mental Development (Grundlagen der psychischen Entwicklung, Berlin, 1925). 69. Hering, Ewald (1834-1918). See Vol. 1, p. 466; Vol. 2, p. 490. 70. Janet, Pierre (1859-1947). French psychologist and psychopathologist. Continuing the work of the French doctor, J. Charceau, he developed the psychological concept of neuroses based, according to Janet, on disturbance in synthetic functions of consciousness. The sphere of the unconscious is limited by him to the simplest forms of mental automatisms. In the 1920's and 1930's, Janet developed a general psychological theory proceeding from an understanding of psychology as the science of behavior. As distinct from behaviorism, Janet does not reduce behavior to elementary acts, including in the system the psychology of consciousness. On this basis, Janet developed a complex, hierarchical system of forms of behavior from the simplest reflexive acts to higher intellectual actions. Janet developed a historical approach to the human mind, emphasizing especially the social level of behavior and its derivatives: the will, memory, thinking, and self-consciousness. He considered thinking genetically as a replacement for real action, functioning in the form of internal speech. 71. Baldwin, James Mark (1861-1934). American psychologist, sociologist, and historian. He was one of the founders of American social psychology; in philosophical views, he was an idealist. Baldwin saw the task of general psychology to be a study of individual differences. He tried to introduce the principle of evolutionism into psychology and considered the mental development of the child from the position of biogenetism. In pedagogics, Baldwin tried to substantiate the necessity for an individual approach to the student based on data from experimental psychology. 72. Seguin, Edouard (1812-1880). A doctor and pedagogue, he was one of the founders of oligophrenopedagogics, and the author of a medical-pedagogical system of education and training of seriously retarded children. Seguin believed that it was possible to educate and train all the retarded. He understood retardation as a disorder of the nervous system in which complete lack of will and inertia are manifested. The task of the educator was to bring the retarded out of this inert state and make him capable of working. 73. Buhler, Charlotte (1886-). See Vol. 1, p. 464; Vol. 2, p. 485. 74. We have in mind children with an underdevelopment of the cerebrum. 75. Wallon, Henri (1879-1962). An outstanding French psychologist who made a great contribution to child development and pedagogical psychology. 76. Potebnya, Aleksandr Afanas'evich (1835-1891). Ukrainian and Russian philologist-Slavicist. He worked on problems of the theory of philology, folklore, and ethnography (separating the views of the mythological school), mainly in general linguistics, phonetics, morphology, syntax and semantics. From the theoretical aspect, he studied problems of the interrelation of language and thinking, language and nation, and the origin of language. It was his opinion that the thinking-speech act is an individual-mental creative act, but in speech activity, the social, which is language (more precisely, its sound aspect), is in-

284

Notes to the Russian Edition

volved together with its individual origin and appears as "objectivized thought." Tracing the historical development of concrete language, Potebnya reached conclusions on the historical changes in the character of verbal thinking of a given people and of humanity as a whole (see Thinking and Language [in Russian], 1862). 77. Shakhmatov, Aleksei Aleksandrovich (1864-1920). Russian linguist, researcher of Russian chronicles. He discovered ancient oral languages different from living languages and studied problems of the ancient mother country and parent language (see: Introduction to a Course on the History of the Russian Language, Petersburg, 1916, Part I). 78. Dal', Vladimir Ivanovich (1801-1872). Russian writer, ethnographer. He devoted more than a half century to his basic work, The Great Dictionary of the Living Great Russian Language (Vols. 1-4, 1863-1866). 79. Delacroix, Henri Joachim (1873-1937). See Vol. 2, p. 484. 80. J. Sully (?). One of the researchers of voluntary attention, he spoke at a discussion of the motor concept of attention proposed by N. N. Lange. 81. Luria, Aleksandr Romanovich (1902-1977). See Vol. 2, p. 483. 82. Troshin, Petr Yakovlevich (?). Psychologist and pedagogue, author of the anthropological theory of education; he shared the concept of "morally defective" children (see "Anthropological Theory of Education" in Comparative Psychology of Normal and Abnormal Children, Petersburg, 1915). 83. Montessori, Maria (1870-1952). Italian pedagogue, professor of anthropology and hygiene. Montessori sharply criticized the traditional school for regimentation and for ignoring the natural needs of the child. According to Montessori, the elementary school should be a laboratory that would make it possible to study the mental life of the child. She developed an original system of sensory development for children in special preschool institutions and in elementary school. In theoretical work and in practical activity, she promoted the ideas of free education. 84. Burt, Cyril Lodovik (1883-?). English psychologist who worked on problems of capabilities and giftedness, professional orientation, retarded ness, its cause and prevention. In his studies, he depended exclusively on testing. 85. Frobel, Friedrich (1782-1852). German pedagogue, theoretician of preschool education. The concept of the active nature of the child, his mobility, directness, the constant development of physical and intellectual powers, sociability, and curiosity was original in his system of education. He advocated the creation of kindergartens and proposed special didactic material, the so-called "gifts" of Frobel. His teaching made possible the separation of preschool pedagogy into a separate branch of pedagogical science. The defect in his system was the severe regulation of the child's activity. 86. Laj, Wilhelm August (1862-1926). German pedagogue, representative of experimental pedagogics. On the basis of data from biology, he attempted to forge a new direction in pedagogy. The basis for Laj's concept was the biological concept of reaction-action, which, with respect to the process of teaching, is treated as a unit of perception, a mental processing of the perceived, external expression of a configuration of ideas with the help of various means (the word, drawing, experiment, work, etc.). 87. Leont'ev, Aleksei Nikolaevich (1903-1979). See Vol. 1, p. 464; Vol. 2, p. 483. 88. Ribot, Theodule (1839-1916). French psychologist, a specialist in pathopsychology and general psychology. He worked in the area of the psychology of feelings, memory, voluntary attention, etc. Considering the origin of voluntary attention, Ribot noted, "How did it arise? It arose due to necessity, under the

Notes to the Russian Edition

285

pressure of need and together with successes of intellectual development. It is a perfected apparatus, a product of civilization. Progress in the area of intellectual development caused man to make a transition from the rule of involuntary attention to the rule of voluntary attention" (The Psychology ofAttention, St. Petersburg, 1897, p. 33). 89. Considering the development of attention, E. Titchener traces the conditions for the development of its higher forms and the interconnections between them. "Attention in its hemispheric significance has two forms. It may be passive and involuntary or it may be active and voluntary. Primary attention, however, represents a certain stage of development, specifically, the earliest stage of development of attention. For this reason, it is not enough to consider it only from the aspect of determining physiological factors; we must also consider it from the biological point of view ... But what can we say of novelty, surprise, and movement? Stimuli of this type have a special biological significance; of course, the new, unexpected, and moving are probably sources of danger, and the living being that would not turn its attention to them would soon die. But there are many cases in which an impression not only does not attract and keep our attention, but on the contrary, it seems that we maintain our attention to an impression through our own effort. A problem in geometry does make as strong an impression on us as a clap of thunder ... Attention to such objects is termed ordinary active or voluntary attention; but we will call it secondary attention ... There is, however, still a third stage in the development of attention, and it consists in nothing other than a return to the first stage. When we solve a geometry problem, we gradually become interested in it and become completely absorbed by it; in a short time, the problem acquires the same power over our consciousness as the clap of thunder had at the instant it penetrated our consciousness" (Psychology Text, Moscow, Part I, 1914, pp. 226-230). 90. Meumann, Ernst (1862-1915). See Vol. 1, p. 464; Vol. 2, p. 485. 91. Bergson, Henri (1859-1941). See Vol. 2, p. 485. 92. Rubin, E. (1850-1934). German psychologist, a student of G. Muller. He was the first to describe the psychological phenomenon "figure and background" (1915), studied in detail in Gestalt psychology. 93. Muller, George Elias (1850-1934). See Vol. 1, p. 462; Vol. 2, p. 486. 94. Ukhtomskii, Aleksei Alekseevich (1875-1942). See Vol. 1, p. 462. 95. We have in mind H. Bergson, Matter and Memory (1889). 96. Bain, Alexander (1818-1903), English philosopher, psychologist, and pedagogue. A major representative of associative psychology in the 19th century, he considered the phenomena of consciousness to be subject to the psychological laws of association and tried at the same time to link them with bodily processes, studying reflexes, habits, instincts, and motor activity of the organism. New forms of the latter, according to Bain, arise as a result of selection of advantageous movements on the basis of the trial and error mechanism. Defending the indivisibility of the mental and the physiological, Bain also rejected a causal connection between them and adopted the positions of psychological parallelism. 97. The experimental data analyzed by L. S. Vygotsky present the results of the work done by A. N. Leont'ev in 1928 and 1930 in the N. K. Krupskaya Psychological Laboratory of the Academy of Communist Education. Formulating the law of development considered by Vygotsky, subsequently called the parallelogram of the development of memory, Leont'ev said: "In this way, the common dynamic of these two lines of development may be most simply ex-

286

Notes to the Russian Edition

pressed in the graphic form of a parallelogram, one pair of opposite angles of which is formed by the drawing together of the coefficients in their upper and lower ranges and the other two angles, joined by a shorter diagonal, correspond to the point of their greatest divergence. In the future we will briefly designate this pattern of development of remembering by the arbitrary term, 'the parallelogram of development' . . . The principle of the parallelogram of development also represents nothing other than an expression of the same general law that development of higher human forms of memory proceeds through the development of remembering with the aid of external stimuli-signs." (Problems of Mental Development, Moscow, 1959, pp. 351-353). 98. Spinoza, Benedict (1633-1677), see Vol. 1, p. 464. 99. Jaensch, Erik (1883-1940), See Vol. 1, p. 464. 100. Bacon, Francis (1561-1626), see Vol. 2, p. 483. 101. Spiritualism: objective-idealistic philosophical view that considered the soul as the fundamental principle of reality, as a special incorporeal substance existing outside material and independent 6f it. 102. Ramon y Cajal, Santiago (1852-1934), Spanish histologist. Through his research, he developed a basis for the neuron theory of the structure of the nervous system. He is the author of works on the structure of the retina of the eye, the medulla, and the pons. He developed a number of special histological methods. 103. Neurath, Otto (1882-1945), Austrian philosopher, sociologist, and economist. His philosophical and sociological views unite eclectically the tendencies of natural science materialism with the views of logical positivism. Seeing the establishment of unity of knowledge as the major task of the philosophy of science, he believed that it could be attained with a "unified language of science"; he gave most of this attention to a translation of the views of psychology and sociology into such "language." 104. Bleuler, Eugene (1857-1939), see Vol. 1, p. 464; Vol. 2, p. 482. 105. Sherrington, Charles Scott (1859-1952), English physiologist whose main work was on the physiology of the central nervous system. Of special significance is his work on the study of the laws of reflex activity of the medulla. Sherrington's work enriched physiology with new data on the relation between processes of excitation and inhibition and on the nature of muscle tone and its disruption. He formulated the principle of convergence of excitation and the "funnel" principle. 106. Katz, David (1884-1953), West European psychologist who worked on a broad range of problems in theoretical, experimental, comparative, genetic, and social psychology. He gave significant attention to problems of child psychology. In his interpretation of some questions, he was close to Gestalt psychology, but did not consider it possible to include in the concept of Gestalt all of the manifestations of the mind, especially those connected with the formation of personality. 107. Driesch, Hans (1867-1941), see Vol. 1, p. 468. 108. We have in mind the book of Jean Piaget: Speech and Thinking in the Child (Moscow; Leningrad, 1932). 109. Fichte, Johann Gottlieb (1762-1814), German philosopher and social activist, representative of German classical idealism.

REFERENCES Leont'ev, A N., Problems in Mental Development, Moscow (1959). Pavlov, I. P., Complete Works, Vol. 4, Moscow, Leningrad (1951). Ribot, T., The Psychology of Attention, St. Petersburg (1897). Titchener, E. B., Psychology Text, Moscow (1914). Tolstoy, L. N., Complete Works, Vol. 11, Moscow (1932).
